- **Limitations:** (i) Higher latency than wired ($~ 100 \text{ ms}$); (ii) Susceptible to multipath fading in metal-rich plants.

**(b) Bandwidth Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Aggregate Data Rate**

$$B_{req} = n \cdot f_s \cdot b = 100 \times 10 \text{ Hz} \times 32 \text{ bits} = 32000 \text{ bits/s} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Utilization**

$$\eta = \frac{B_{req}}{R} \times 100\% = \frac{32}{250} \times 100\% = 12.8\%$$

**Step 3 — Suitability Justification**

- Required bandwidth $32 \text{ kbps} \ll$ available $250 \text{ kbps}$, giving $87.2\%$ headroom for redundancy and retransmission.
- WirelessHART is **suitable** for this application, especially for slow-varying process variables (temperature, pressure, level).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 2:**
> 1. **Confusing Fieldbus with Industrial Ethernet** — Examiners repeatedly deduct 2–3 marks if a student uses "Fieldbus" and "PROFINET" interchangeably. PROFINET is *not* Fieldbus.
> 2. **Skipping the latency formula derivation** — In latency-based questions, always derive $T_{prop} = d/v$ and $T_{tx} = L/R$ explicitly. Skipping leads to **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — $R$ must be in bits/s (not bytes/s). A common error costs the numerical.
> 4. **Omitting Purdue Model context** — In classification questions, the layered architecture explains *why* networks are classified. Skipping it loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong range** — Writing "LAN range $100 \text{ km}$" (mixing with MAN). Stay within KTU's standard table: LAN $\le 1 \text{ km}$, MAN $1-100 \text{ km}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Networks are classified by **geographic span** (PAN, LAN, MAN, WAN), **medium** (wired/wireless), and **industrial layer** (Fieldbus, Industrial Ethernet, Wireless Industrial, TSN).
- **PAN** $< 10 \text{ m}$; **LAN** $10 \text{ m} - 1 \text{ km}$; **MAN** $1 - 100 \text{ km}$; **WAN** $> 100 \text{ km}$.
- **Fieldbus** (PROFIBUS, FOUNDATION Fieldbus) = 1980s serial bus, low data rate, field-instrument level.
- **Industrial Ethernet** (PROFINET, EtherCAT, EtherNet/IP) = high-speed $100 \text{ Mbps} - 10 \text{ Gbps}$ real-time Ethernet.
- **EtherCAT** delivers sub-$\mu$s jitter and is the gold standard for synchronized multi-axis motion.
- **PROFINET** dominates European factory automation; **EtherNet/IP** dominates North America.
- **WirelessHART** (IEC 62591) and **ISA 100.11a** are the leading wireless industrial standards, both based on IEEE 802.15.4.
- **TSN** (IEEE 802.1Qbv, 802.1AS) adds determinism to standard Ethernet — the future of converged IT/OT.
- Key formulas to memorize: $T_{prop} = d/v$, $T_{tx} = L/R$, $\eta = B_{req}/R \times 100\%$.
- Always map the network choice to the **Purdue Model** level (L0–L5) for a complete KTU answer.
- For 14-mark questions, structure: Definition → Diagram → Classification → Use-case → Comparison table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Different Sensors and Their Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Study of Different Sensors and Their Characteristics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Sensor? (KTU Formal Definition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (PERUT635):** > A **sensor** is a physical device that detects, measures, and responds to a physical stimulus (such as temperature, pressure, light, motion, or chemical composition) from the physical environment and converts it into an equivalent electrical signal that can be read by an observer, a microcontroller, or an IIoT gateway. In the context of **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**, sensors form the **"perception layer"** (also called the *sensing layer* or *physical layer*) of the standard 5-layer IIoT architecture (Perception → Network → Edge / Fog → Cloud → Application). A **transducer**, in contrast, is the broader umbrella term for any device that converts energy from one form to another. **A sensor is therefore a specific type of transducer whose output is an electrical signal proportional to the measured physical quantity.** ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Sensors Are the Senses of Industry" > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** > Imagine a human body working inside a massive factory. The body has **five senses** — eyes (vision), ears (sound), skin (touch/pressure), nose (smell/gas), and tongue (taste/chemical). Without these senses, the brain would be completely blind to the world. **Sensors play exactly the same role for an IIoT system.** They are the "eyes, ears, and skin" of a smart factory. The **microcontroller/gateway acts as the brain**, the **network acts as the nervous system**, and the **cloud acts as long-term memory**. Without high-quality sensors at the front-end, no amount of cloud computing or AI can rescue a broken system — because **"garbage in = garbage out."** ### 1.3 Why Sensor Study Matters in IIoT | IIoT Application Area | Role of Sensors | |---|---| | **Smart Manufacturing** | Detect machine vibration, temperature, current to predict failures (Predictive Maintenance) | | **Smart Energy / Smart Grid** | Monitor voltage, current, power quality in real time | | **Connected Healthcare** | Track patient vitals (ECG, SpO₂, body temperature) | | **Smart Agriculture** | Measure soil moisture, pH, ambient humidity, light intensity | | **Smart Cities** | Air quality (PM2.5/PM10), noise levels, traffic flow | | **Supply Chain & Logistics** | GPS, RFID, temperature during cold chain transport | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight (Syllabus Mapping):** This topic directly maps to **CO2** of PERUT635 — *“Understand the role of sensors, actuators, and signal conditioning in IIoT systems.”* Mastering the **characteristics** (static + dynamic) of sensors is a guaranteed 14-mark question in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ### 1.4 Two Broad Classification Lenses **Lens A — By Output Signal Type:** - **Analog Sensors** → produce a continuous output (e.g., LM35 temperature sensor, strain gauge). - **Digital Sensors** → produce a discrete, coded output (e.g., DHT11, DS18B20). **Lens B — By Power Requirement:** - **Active Sensors** → generate their own electrical output (e.g., piezoelectric, photovoltaic, thermocouple). They need **no external excitation**. - **Passive Sensors** → require external power/excitation to operate (e.g., RTD, strain gauge, thermistor). They merely *modulate* an external signal. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Static Characteristics of Sensors Static characteristics describe sensor behaviour when the **measured quantity is constant or changes very slowly** (i.e., steady-state operation). These are the most heavily tested in KTU exams. | # | Characteristic | Formal Definition | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Accuracy** | Closeness of the measured value to the **true value** of the input. | Indicates systematic error magnitude. | | 2 | **Precision** | Ability to give the **same reading repeatedly** under identical conditions (reproducibility). | Indicates random error magnitude. | | 3 | **Sensitivity (S)** | Ratio of incremental change in output to the incremental change in input. | Determines amplification needs. | | 4 | **Linearity** | Degree to which the output-input curve is a straight line. | Affects calibration effort. | | 5 | **Hysteresis** | Maximum difference in output for the **same input approached from opposite directions**. | Critical for cyclic processes. | | 6 | **Resolution** | Smallest change in input that produces a detectable change in output. | Sets the least-count of the system. | | 7 | **Range (Span)** | Difference between maximum and minimum measurable input values. | Defines operational envelope. | | 8 | **Repeatability** | Ability to reproduce the same output for the same input over multiple trials under **same conditions**. | Reliability indicator. | | 9 | **Drift** | Slow change of output with time *even when input is constant*. | Aging / calibration loss indicator. | | 10 | **Dead Zone** | Range of input values for which there is no change in output. | Non-responsive region. | | 11 | **Threshold** | Minimum input magnitude required to produce a measurable output. | Activation point. | | 12 | **Span Error / Zero Error** | Deviation at the upper / lower end of the range. | Linearity check. | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams (Use the mnemonic – "A S L H R R D S T D T"):** > **A**ccuracy – **S**ensitivity – **L**inearity – **H**ysteresis – **R**esolution – **R**ange/Span – **D**rift – **S**electivity – **T**hreshold – **D**ead Zone – **T**ime constant. ## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics of Sensors Dynamic characteristics describe behaviour when the input is **time-varying (transient or periodic)**. They are typically modelled as a **first-order** or **second-order** system. | # | Characteristic | Definition | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Time Constant (τ)** | Time for output to reach **63.2%** of its final value in a step response. | | 2 | **Rise Time (t_r)** | Time to go from **10% → 90%** of final value. | | 3 | **Settling Time (t_s)** | Time to settle within ±2% (or ±5%) of the final value. | | 4 | **Bandwidth (BW)** | Frequency range over which the sensor responds within **−3 dB** of its peak response. | | 5 | **Damping Ratio (ζ)** | Determines whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped. | | 6 | **Natural Frequency (ω_n)** | Frequency at which the sensor would oscillate in absence of damping. | | 7 | **Phase Shift (φ)** | Time lag between input sine wave and output sine wave. | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output unit per Input unit (e.g., mV/°C) | | 2 | Percent Sensitivity | $S_\% = \dfrac{\Delta y / y_{FS}}{\Delta x / x_{FS}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless % | | 3 | Non-linearity | $\%NL = \dfrac{(D_{max})}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $D_{max}$ = max deviation from best-fit straight line | | 4 | Hysteresis | $\%H = \dfrac{(y_{up} - y_{down})_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $y_{FS}$ = full-scale output | | 5 | Repeatability | $\%R = \dfrac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $\Delta y_{max}$ = max spread over repeated trials | | 6 | Dead-band / Dead-zone | $\%DB = \dfrac{x_{dead}}{x_{FS}} \times 100$ | Non-responsive input range | | 7 | Drift (thermal) | $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta T} \,(input \, constant)$ | µV/°C or %FS/°C | | 8 | First-order time response (step input) | $y(t) = y_{ss}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | τ = time constant | | 9 | Rise time (first-order) | $t_r = 2.197 \, \tau$ | From 10% → 90% | | 10 | Settling time (2% criterion) | $t_s \approx 4\tau$ | 2% of final value | | 11 | Second-order step response (under-damped) | $y(t) = y_{ss}\left[1 - \dfrac{e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)\right]$ | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | | 12 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s | | 13 | Bandwidth (−3 dB, first-order) | $f_{BW} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Hz | | 14 | RTD resistance (linear) | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient, $R_0$ = R at 0°C | | 15 | Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V = S_{AB}(T_H - T_C)$ | $S_{AB}$ = Seebeck coefficient (µV/°C) | | 16 | Thermistor (β-equation) | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | β in Kelvin, T in Kelvin | | 17 | Strain gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G_f$ = gauge factor, $\varepsilon$ = strain | | 18 | Capacitive displacement | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Distance d changes linearly with input | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Golden Rule:** Whenever a numerical problem on sensor characteristics is asked, **always express errors in %FS (percent of Full-Scale)**, never in absolute terms, unless the question explicitly states otherwise. The 14-mark numerical answer almost always ends with a "% error" computation. ## 2.4 Detailed Study of Important IIoT Sensors ### 2.4.1 Temperature Sensors | Sensor Type | Principle | Output | Range | Pros / Cons | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Thermocouple** | Seebeck effect (junction of two dissimilar metals) | mV | −270 °C to +2300 °C | Fast, rugged / Needs cold-junction compensation, non-linear | | **RTD (PT100)** | Resistance of platinum varies linearly with T | Resistance | −200 °C to +850 °C | Highly linear, accurate / Slow, expensive | | **Thermistor (NTC/PTC)** | Semiconductor resistance changes non-linearly | Resistance | −100 °C to +300 °C | High sensitivity / Highly non-linear, limited range | | **IC Sensor (LM35, DS18B20)** | Bandgap voltage of silicon | Analog mV / Digital | −55 °C to +150 °C | Built-in ADC, easy to interface / Limited range | ### 2.4.2 Pressure Sensors | Type | Principle | |---|---| | **Strain-gauge pressure sensor** | Foil gauges bonded to a diaphragm; deflection → strain → resistance change. | | **Piezoelectric pressure sensor** | Pressure → force → charge on quartz crystal. | | **Capacitive pressure sensor** | Diaphragm deflection changes distance between capacitor plates. | ### 2.4.3 Proximity / Position Sensors - **Inductive proximity** – detects metallic objects via eddy-current losses in an LC oscillator. - **Capacitive proximity** – detects both metals and non-metals (plastics, liquids) by change in capacitance. - **Ultrasonic / PIR** – detect motion / distance via sound echo or infrared radiation. - **Encoders (Optical / Magnetic)** – rotary/linear position via pulse counting. ### 2.4.4 Other Industrial Sensors - **Flow sensors** (turbine, magnetic, vortex, Coriolis). - **Level sensors** (float, ultrasonic, capacitive, radar). - **Gas / Chemical sensors** (MQ-2, MQ-135, electrochemical, NDIR). - **Humidity sensors** (DHT11/DHT22, SHT31, capacitive polymer). - **Image sensors** (CMOS, CCD) – used in machine vision & quality inspection. - **Accelerometer / Gyroscope / IMU** – used in vibration analysis and predictive maintenance. ## 2.5 Signal Conditioning Block (Mandatory Bridge to Microcontroller) Raw sensor output is rarely directly usable. A standard **signal-conditioning chain** is: **Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → Microcontroller / IIoT Gateway** Key signal-conditioning operations: 1. **Amplification** – low-level mV signals boosted to 0–5 V or 0–3.3 V. 2. **Linearization** – correct non-linearity (especially in thermocouples & thermistors). 3. **Cold-junction compensation (CJC)** – for thermocouples. 4. **Filtering** – remove EMI and 50/60 Hz mains interference (low-pass / band-pass). 5. **Isolation** – galvanic isolation using optocouplers / isolation amplifiers to protect the MCU. 6. **ADC conversion** – convert analog output to digital codes (10-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit). 7. **Range conversion** – match the dynamic range of the sensor to ADC input. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the % Non-Linearity Expression Let the sensor's true output-input curve be $y = f(x)$. A **best-fit straight line** (least-squares method) is drawn through the data points $(x_i, y_i)$: $$y = mx + c$$ The **deviation** of each data point from this straight line is: $$\Delta y_i = f(x_i) - (m x_i + c)$$ The **maximum absolute deviation** observed across the full input range is: $$D_{max} = \max_i \left\vert \Delta y_i \right\vert$$ The **percent non-linearity** is therefore defined as: $$\%NL = \dfrac{D_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%$$ where $y_{FS}$ is the **full-scale output** (i.e., the output span $y_{max} - y_{min}$). **Interpretation:** A non-linearity of 0.5 %FS means the worst-case deviation from the ideal straight line is only 0.5 % of the full-scale output — typically considered a high-quality industrial sensor. ## 3.2 Derivation of Sensitivity and the % Span Equation Let the input span be $\Delta x = x_{max} - x_{min}$ and the output span be $\Delta y = y_{max} - y_{min}$. The **absolute sensitivity** is: $$S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{y_{max} - y_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$ To compare sensors independent of their range, we use **%Span Sensitivity**: $$S_{\%} = \dfrac{\Delta y / y_{FS}}{\Delta x / x_{FS}} \times 100\%$$ **Worked Example (very KTU-style):** > A pressure sensor has an input range of **0 to 10 bar** and an output voltage of **0 to 5 V**. If the measured output is **2.45 V** at **5 bar**, compute the (a) sensitivity, (b) ideal output at 5 bar, (c) deviation error in %FS. **Step 1 — Sensitivity:** $$S = \dfrac{y_{max} - y_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} = \dfrac{5 - 0}{10 - 0} = 0.5 \text{ V/bar}$$ **Step 2 — Ideal (theoretical) output at 5 bar:** $$y_{ideal} = S \cdot x = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Deviation:** $$\Delta y = y_{actual} - y_{ideal} = 2.45 - 2.50 = -0.05 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — % Full-Scale Error:** $$\%FS\_error = \dfrac{\vert \Delta y \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.05}{5.0} \times 100 = 1.0\%FS$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the First-Order Step Response The general **first-order differential equation** for a sensor is: $$\tau \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$ For a **step input** of magnitude $A$ at $t=0$, i.e., $x(t) = A \cdot u(t)$: $$\tau \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K A$$ **Solution Method:** The total response = steady-state + transient. $$y(t) = \underbrace{KA}_{\text{steady-state}} + \underbrace{B e^{-t/\tau}}_{\text{transient}}$$ Applying the **initial condition** $y(0) = 0$ (sensor starts at zero): $$0 = KA + B \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B = -KA$$ Therefore the complete step response is: $$y(t) = K A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$ **Key Inferences:** At $t = \tau$: $$y(\tau) = KA(1 - e^{-1}) = KA(1 - 0.3679) = 0.6321 \, KA \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 63.2\%$$ This is the **defining property of the time constant** $\tau$: the time to reach 63.2 % of the final value. **Rise Time (10 % → 90 %):** Setting $y(t_r) = 0.9 \, KA$: $$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t_r/\tau} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-t_r/\tau} = 0.1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_r = \tau \ln 10 \approx 2.3026 \, \tau$$ **Settling Time (within 2 %):** $$0.98 = 1 - e^{-t_s/\tau} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_s = -\tau \ln(0.02) \approx 3.912 \, \tau \approx 4\tau$$ ## 3.4 Derivation of the −3 dB Bandwidth for First-Order System The frequency response of a first-order system to a sinusoidal input $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ is: $$\left\vert H(j\omega) \right\vert = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^2}}$$ The **−3 dB frequency** is the frequency at which the output power drops to half (or amplitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ of the DC value): $$\dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c \tau)^2}} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{2}}$$ Squaring both sides and solving: $$1 + (\omega_c \tau)^2 = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_c \tau = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_c = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$$ In **Hz**: $$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$$ **Engineering Insight:** A sensor with a time constant of **10 ms** has a usable bandwidth of: $$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 0.01} \approx 15.9 \text{ Hz}$$ ## 3.5 Derivation of the RTD Equation (Callendar-Van Dusen) The **Callendar-Van Dusen equation** is the standard for platinum RTDs: $$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 - 100 \, C T^3 + C T^4\right]$$ For the common linear approximation (used in the KTU 2024 syllabus): $$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$ where: - $R_0$ = resistance at 0 °C (e.g., **100 Ω for PT100**) - $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of resistance (≈ **3.85 × 10⁻³ /°C** for platinum) - $T$ = temperature in °C **Numerical:** For a PT100 at $T = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$: $$R_{100} = 100 \times (1 + 0.00385 \times 100) = 100 \times 1.385 = 138.5 \, \Omega$$ **Verification:** This matches the platinum standard exactly — a key reason platinum is preferred for industrial RTDs. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection by Application | Application | Best Sensor Type | Justification | |---|---|---| | Furnace temperature (>1000 °C) | **Type-K / Type-S Thermocouple** | Wide range, rugged, fast response. | | Lab-grade temperature reference | **PT100 / PT1000 RTD** | Excellent linearity and stability. | | Battery-pack thermal monitoring | **NTC Thermistor** | High sensitivity at narrow range, low cost. | | HVAC room thermostat | **IC sensor (LM35)** | Direct V output, easy to interface. | | Hydraulic press pressure | **Strain-gauge pressure sensor** | Rugged, accurate, 0–700 bar. | | Vibration of motor bearings | **Piezoelectric accelerometer** | Wide dynamic range, high bandwidth. | | Object detection on conveyor (metallic) | **Inductive proximity** | Non-contact, no contamination, IP67. | | Water level in a tank | **Ultrasonic / Capacitive** | Non-contact, immune to foam / vapours. | | Air-quality monitoring | **MQ-135 / NDIR sensor** | Detects CO₂, NH₃, VOCs. | | Machine vision quality check | **CMOS image sensor + camera** | High resolution, real-time inspection. | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sensor Position in the IIoT Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB physEnv["Physical Environment
Temperature Pressure Flow Level"] sensorLayer["SENSING LAYER
Sensors and Actuators"] sigCond["Signal Conditioning
Amplifier Filter ADC"] edge["EDGE LAYER
Microcontroller Gateway"] network["NETWORK LAYER
WiFi LoRa MQTT 5G"] cloud["CLOUD LAYER
Analytics Database"] app["APPLICATION LAYER
Dashboard Alerts AI"] physEnv --> sensorLayer sensorLayer --> sigCond sigCond --> edge edge --> network network --> cloud cloud --> app app -.Feedback.-> edge ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree — Industrial Sensors ```mermaid flowchart LR sensorRoot["INDUSTRIAL SENSORS"] sensorRoot --> active["ACTIVE SENSORS
No external power needed"] sensorRoot --> passive["PASSIVE SENSORS
Need excitation voltage"] active --> thermo["Thermocouple"] active --> piezo["Piezoelectric"] active --> photo["Photovoltaic"] passive --> rtd1["RTD PT100"] passive --> therm["Thermistor NTC PTC"] passive --> strain["Strain Gauge"] passive --> ldr["LDR"] sensorRoot --> analogOut["ANALOG OUTPUT"] sensorRoot --> digitalOut["DIGITAL OUTPUT"] analogOut --> lm35["LM35"] analogOut --> pt100a["PT100"] digitalOut --> ds18["DS18B20"] digitalOut --> dht["DHT22"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Signal Conditioning Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR a["Physical Input
Temperature Pressure Flow"] b["SENSOR"] c["Amplifier
Instrumentation Op-Amp"] d["Filter
Low Pass Anti-Aliasing"] e["ADC
12 16 24 bit"] f["Microcontroller
ESP32 STM32 PLC"] g["IIoT Gateway
Cloud Server"] a --> b b --> c c --> d d --> e e --> f f --> g ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — First-Order Sensor Response ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Resting Resting --> Charging : Step input applied Charging --> SixtyThree : t = tau SixtyThree --> Ninety : t = 2.2 tau Ninety --> Settled : t = 4 tau Settled --> [*] ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Architecture — Sensor Performance Trade-off Triangle ```mermaid flowchart TB tri["SENSOR DESIGN TRIANGLE"] tri --> cost["COST"] tri --> acc["ACCURACY"] tri --> speed["SPEED BANDWIDTH"] cost --- acc acc --- speed speed --- cost ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A sensor cannot simultaneously be the cheapest, the most accurate, and the fastest. Engineers must **trade off** these three constraints. For example, a research-grade RTD is highly accurate but slow; a thermocouple is fast but needs heavy compensation; a thermistor is cheap but non-linear. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define the following terms with respect to industrial sensors: (i) Sensitivity, (ii) Hysteresis, (iii) Drift.* **Model Answer:** (i) **Sensitivity** is defined as the ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input that causes it. $$S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$ A highly sensitive sensor produces a large output change for a small input change. For example, the LM35 has a sensitivity of 10 mV/°C. (ii) **Hysteresis** is the maximum difference in output for the same input value when approached from increasing versus decreasing directions. $$\%H = \dfrac{(y_{up} - y_{down})_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$ It arises from friction, magnetic hysteresis, or material inelasticity inside the sensor. (iii) **Drift** is the slow, time-dependent change in the sensor output even when the input remains constant. Drift can be classified as **thermal drift** (due to ambient temperature) and **time/aging drift** (long-term material degradation). It is expressed in µV/°C or %FS per year. ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between an **active** sensor and a **passive** sensor. Give one example of each.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Generates its own electrical output in response to the stimulus. | Requires external power (excitation) to operate. | | **Energy Source** | Input energy itself is converted to electrical energy. | Modulates externally supplied energy. | | **External Circuit** | No excitation source needed. | Needs excitation source (voltage/current source). | | **Output** | Self-generated voltage/current. | Resistance / capacitance / inductance change. | | **Examples** | Thermocouple, Piezoelectric sensor, Photovoltaic cell, Tachogenerator. | RTD (PT100), Thermistor, Strain gauge, LDR, Capacitive level sensor. | | **IIoT Use** | Preferred in remote, battery-less nodes (energy harvesting). | Preferred in precise instrumentation (Wheatstone bridge). | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **static characteristics** of a sensor in detail. Define accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, resolution, range, and repeatability. State one formula for each. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* A **PT100 RTD** is used to measure the temperature of a chemical reactor. The PT100 has $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ and $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}/^\circ\text{C}$. (i) Calculate the resistance of the sensor at $150\,^\circ\text{C}$. (ii) If this RTD is connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** with all other arms = 100 Ω and the bridge excitation is 5 V, compute the bridge output voltage at $150\,^\circ\text{C}$. (iii) A 14-bit ADC is used to read the bridge output. Find the ADC resolution in mV. #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Static Characteristics (7 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > Examiner's Distribution (KTU pattern): 1 mark per definition + remaining 7 marks distributed. Students must write all 7 terms with formulas to secure full marks. 1. **Accuracy:** Closeness of the measured value to the true value. $$\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \left\vert \dfrac{\text{Measured} - \text{True}}{\text{True}} \right\vert$$ 2. **Sensitivity:** Change in output per unit change in input. $$S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$ 3. **Linearity:** Maximum deviation of the actual curve from the ideal straight line. $$\%NL = \dfrac{D_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$ 4. **Hysteresis:** Maximum difference in output for same input approached from opposite directions. $$\%H = \dfrac{(y_{up}-y_{down})_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$ 5. **Resolution:** Smallest change in input that produces a detectable output change. $$\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{x_{FS}}{2^n} \text{ (for digital systems)}$$ 6. **Range (Span):** Difference between maximum and minimum measurable input. $$\text{Span} = x_{max} - x_{min}$$ 7. **Repeatability:** Ability to produce the same output for the same input repeatedly under the same operating conditions. $$\%R = \dfrac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** **(i) Resistance at 150 °C:** Using $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$: $$R_{150} = 100 \times \left(1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 150\right)$$ $$R_{150} = 100 \times \left(1 + 0.5775\right) = 100 \times 1.5775 = 157.75 \, \Omega$$ **(ii) Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage:** For a Wheatstone bridge with all four arms equal to 100 Ω at 0 °C, the output voltage is: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$$ At 150 °C, only $R_1$ (the PT100) changes to 157.75 Ω; the other three remain at 100 Ω: $$V_{out} = 5 \left(\dfrac{100}{157.75+100} - \dfrac{100}{100+100}\right)$$ $$V_{out} = 5 \left(\dfrac{100}{257.75} - \dfrac{100}{200}\right) = 5 \left(0.3879 - 0.5\right) = 5 \times (-0.1121)$$ $$V_{out} = -0.5606 \text{ V} \approx -560.6 \text{ mV}$$ The **magnitude** of bridge output is **560.6 mV**. **(iii) ADC Resolution (14-bit, 5 V reference):** $$\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{14}} = \dfrac{5}{16384} = 3.05 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 0.305 \text{ mV}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Frequently Loses Marks!):** > 1. **Sign Error in Bridge Equation:** Students frequently drop the negative sign and report 560.6 mV as positive. **Always quote the polarity** (or magnitude with explicit sign convention). > 2. **Range Error:** $R_T$ must be in Ohms, $T$ in °C. Do NOT mix Kelvin and °C in the linear RTD equation — Kelvin is for the **thermistor β-equation** only. > 3. **Units in ADC:** The question says 14-bit — remember it is $2^{14}$, not $2^{12}$ or $2^{10}$. KTU explicitly tests this confusion. --- ### **Question B — Choice 2 / Alternative (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Paper]** **(a)** *(7 Marks)* With neat block diagrams, explain the **working principle** of the following temperature sensors: (i) **Thermocouple** (Seebeck effect), (ii) **RTD** (PT100), (iii) **Thermistor** (NTC type). State one industrial application of each. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* A first-order temperature sensor is suddenly placed in a furnace at $T = 200\,^\circ\text{C}$. The sensor has a time constant $\tau = 8 \text{ s}$ and sensitivity $S = 10 \text{ mV/°C}$. Initially the sensor reads $25\,^\circ\text{C}$. (i) Write the equation of the sensor output voltage as a function of time. (ii) Compute the output voltage at $t = 4 \text{ s}$ and at $t = 8 \text{ s}$. (iii) Find the rise time $t_r$ and settling time $t_s$ (2 % criterion). #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Temperature Sensor Working Principles (7 Marks):** **(i) Thermocouple — Seebeck Effect:** A thermocouple consists of **two dissimilar metals (A and B) joined at a junction**. When the measuring junction (hot junction, $T_H$) is heated and the other end (cold junction, $T_C$) is kept at a reference temperature, an EMF is generated: $$V = S_{AB}(T_H - T_C)$$ where $S_{AB}$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** (e.g., 40 µV/°C for Type-K). **Application:** Furnace temperature monitoring in steel plants; gas-turbine exhaust measurement. **(ii) RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) — PT100:** A PT100 is a **platinum wire whose resistance increases linearly with temperature**. $$R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T) \quad \text{with} \quad R_0 = 100\,\Omega, \alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}/^\circ\text{C}$$ It is connected in a **Wheatstone bridge**, and the imbalance voltage is proportional to temperature. **Application:** Pharmaceutical reactors, precision laboratory thermometry. **(iii) Thermistor (NTC — Negative Temperature Coefficient):** A thermistor is a **semiconductor device whose resistance decreases exponentially with increasing temperature**: $$R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$ The β constant is typically 3000–5000 K. **Application:** Battery-pack temperature monitoring, inrush current limiting, 3D-printer hot-end. **Part (b) — First-Order Step Response Numerical (7 Marks):** **Given:** - Furnace temperature: $T_{ss} = 200\,^\circ\text{C}$ - Initial temperature: $T_0 = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$ - Time constant: $\tau = 8 \text{ s}$ - Sensitivity: $S = 10 \text{ mV/°C}$ **Step (i) — Equation of output voltage:** The temperature first-order response: $$T(t) = T_{ss} - (T_{ss} - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$$ $$T(t) = 200 - 175 \, e^{-t/8}$$ Multiplying by sensitivity: $$V(t) = S \cdot T(t) = 10 \left[200 - 175 \, e^{-t/8}\right]$$ $$V(t) = 2000 - 1750 \, e^{-t/8} \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{V(t) = 2.0 - 1.75 \, e^{-t/8} \text{ V}}$$ **Step (ii) — Output at t = 4 s and t = 8 s:** **At t = 4 s:** $$V(4) = 2.0 - 1.75 \times e^{-4/8} = 2.0 - 1.75 \times e^{-0.5}$$ $$e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$ $$V(4) = 2.0 - 1.75 \times 0.6065 = 2.0 - 1.0614 = 0.9386 \text{ V} \approx 938.6 \text{ mV}$$ **At t = 8 s (= τ):** $$V(8) = 2.0 - 1.75 \times e^{-1} = 2.0 - 1.75 \times 0.3679$$ $$V(8) = 2.0 - 0.6438 = 1.3562 \text{ V} \approx 1356.2 \text{ mV}$$ This matches the **63.2 % rule**: The temperature change is $0.6321 \times 175 = 110.6\,^\circ\text{C}$, so $T(8) = 25 + 110.6 = 135.6\,^\circ\text{C}$, and $V = 10 \times 135.6 = 1356 \text{ mV}$ ✓ **Step (iii) — Rise Time and Settling Time:** $$t_r = 2.197 \, \tau = 2.197 \times 8 = 17.58 \text{ s}$$ $$t_s = 4 \tau = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Frequently Loses Marks!):** > 1. **Confusing τ, t_r, t_s:** Many students write $t_r = 4\tau$ — that is the **settling** time, not rise time. Rise time is **2.197 τ**, settling time is **4 τ** (2 % criterion) or **5 τ** (1 % criterion). > 2. **Exponential sign:** The formula is $e^{-t/\tau}$ not $e^{t/\tau}$. Forgetting the negative sign will give exponentially *growing* output — physically impossible for a first-order sensor. > 3. **Units of β in Thermistor Equation:** T must be in **Kelvin** (not °C) for the β-equation. Converting incorrectly is a classic 1-mark loser. > 4. **Forgot "rest value":** The response equation has TWO components: initial value (25 °C, 250 mV) and asymptotic value (200 °C, 2000 mV). Always quote both. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — 10 Bullets to Memorize:** - ⭐ **Sensors convert physical stimulus → electrical signal**; **Actuators** do the reverse (electrical → physical). The full IIoT stack is: **Perception → Network → Edge → Cloud → Application**. - ⭐ **Static vs Dynamic:** Static = steady-state (accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, resolution, span, drift, repeatability). Dynamic = time-varying (time constant, rise time, settling time, bandwidth, damping, natural frequency, phase shift). - ⭐ **63.2 % Rule of Time Constant:** In a first-order step response, after one time constant $\tau$, the output reaches **63.2 %** of its final value. After $4\tau$, it has settled to within 2 %. - ⭐ **−3 dB Bandwidth:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ for a first-order system. A smaller $\tau$ means a wider bandwidth and a faster sensor. - ⭐ **Sensitivity Rule of Thumb:** High sensitivity = small physical change → large electrical change. But too-high sensitivity amplifies noise — there's always a trade-off. - ⭐ **Active vs Passive:** Active sensors are self-powered (thermocouple, piezoelectric, photovoltaic). Passive need excitation (RTD, thermistor, strain gauge). - ⭐ **RTD Linear Equation:** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$, with PT100 using $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ and $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}/^\circ\text{C}$. - ⭐ **Thermistor Equation:** $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ — T in **Kelvin**; NTC = resistance decreases with T; PTC = resistance increases with T. - ⭐ **Thermocouple Seebeck Equation:** $V = S_{AB}(T_H - T_C)$ — needs **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** because $T_C$ is rarely 0 °C in practice. - ⭐ **%FS Error Expression:** ALWAYS express errors as % of **Full-Scale output**, not % of measured value. The ADC resolution is $\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ — remember $n$ is the bit-depth, not $2^n-1$.

topics.title: Temperature Sensor with Thermistor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Temperature Sensor with Thermistor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **thermistor** (a portmanteau of *thermal* and *resistor*) is a passive solid-state temperature-sensing element whose electrical **resistance varies predictably and measurably with temperature**. In the context of **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, a thermistor acts as the primary *physical-to-electrical transducer* that converts thermal energy from a process variable (boiler surface, motor winding, cold-chain crate, HVAC duct) into a resistance value, which is then digitized by an embedded ADC and published over MQTT/CoAP to the IIoT cloud for analytics and control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A thermistor is a *non-linear*, *highly sensitive* semiconductor resistor whose resistance is a strong function of its absolute body temperature. Unlike an RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) which uses pure metal, a thermistor is fabricated from **metal-oxide ceramics** (e.g., Mn₂O₃, NiO, CoO, Fe₂O₃ sintered together).

The two principal classes mandated by the syllabus are:

| Class | Symbol | Behavior | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Negative Temperature Coefficient | **NTC** | Resistance **decreases** as temperature **rises** | Inrush current limiting, precision temperature sensing |
| Positive Temperature Coefficient | **PTC** | Resistance **increases** as temperature **rises** | Self-resetting fuses, motor protection |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU PERUT635 syllabus, the dominant device discussed under "Temperature Sensor with Thermistor" is the **NTC thermistor** because of its high sensitivity (~ **−4 %/°C to −6 %/°C**) compared to RTDs (~ **0.385 %/°C for Pt100**).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are watering a garden hose connected to a **squeezable rubber bulb**. As the day gets hotter, the rubber softens, the bulb expands, and the water channel inside widens — *more current flows*. This is exactly how an **NTC thermistor** behaves:

- **Cold rubber (low T)** → narrow channel → **high resistance**
- **Hot rubber (high T)** → wide channel → **low resistance**

The semiconductor oxide inside the thermistor behaves like that rubber bulb: as thermal energy excites charge carriers (electrons/holes), conduction improves and resistance drops.

A second analogy — the **marble-in-a-bowl** model:

- At **low temperature**, marbles (charge carriers) sit still at the bottom of a deep bowl (the energy band gap) — almost no flow → **high R**.
- At **high temperature**, marbles vibrate vigorously, easily spill over the rim → **easy conduction** → **low R**.

> [!TIP]
> A useful rule of thumb to memorize: **NTC = "N" for Negative slope** (dR/dT is negative). If temperature is plotted on the X-axis and resistance on the Y-axis, the NTC curve falls like a ski slope.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **B-value (β)**: Material constant, typically **3000 K to 5000 K** for standard NTCs.
- **Reference Resistance R₂₅**: Resistance at **T = 25 °C (298.15 K)** — printed on the device, e.g., **10 kΩ @ 25 °C**.
- **Dissipation Constant δ**: Power (mW) needed to raise the thermistor body temperature by 1 °C above ambient, typically **1 mW/°C to 10 mW/°C** in still air.
- **Thermal Time Constant τ**: Time to reach **63.2 %** of a step-temperature change, typically **1 s to 60 s** depending on package.
- **Operating Range**: −55 °C to **+150 °C** for standard beads; up to **+300 °C** for high-temp probe assemblies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Physics — How Does a Thermistor "Work"?

A thermistor is a sintered pellet of polycrystalline metal-oxide semiconductor. Its resistance is governed by an **activated-hopping / intrinsic-semiconductor** conduction mechanism, which can be broken down into the following stepwise logic:

1. **Lattice Vibration (Phonon) Interaction:** As temperature T rises, lattice atoms vibrate with greater amplitude, scattering conduction electrons more strongly. However, in a doped semiconductor, the *increase in carrier concentration* from thermal excitation dominates over phonon scattering.
2. **Carrier Generation:** Electrons are thermally promoted from the valence band (or donor level) into the conduction band. The intrinsic carrier concentration follows $\;n_i \propto T^{3/2} \exp\!\bigl(-E_g / 2k_BT\bigr)$.
3. **Resistance Collapse:** Conductivity $\sigma = q(n\mu_e + p\mu_h)$ rises exponentially, so resistivity $\rho = 1/\sigma$ falls exponentially — yielding the characteristic NTC curve.
4. **Self-Heating Caveat:** Because resistance drops with temperature, current through the device at a fixed voltage *increases*, dissipating more power $P = I^2R$. This self-heating shifts the operating point — designers must limit bias current to keep dissipation under ~1 mW.
5. **Signal Conditioning:** The changing resistance is converted into a changing voltage by a **voltage divider** (or **Wheatstone bridge** for high-precision tasks), then digitized by the MCU's ADC.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these five equations — they appear every year in KTU ESE Part B derivations.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Steinhart-Hart (3-parameter)** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B\ln(R) + C\bigl[\ln(R)\bigr]^{3}$ | T in **Kelvin**, R in **Ω**, A, B, C are calibration constants | High-accuracy digital temperature conversion over wide range |
| 2 | **B-parameter (2-parameter)** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_0} + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{R_0}\right)$ | T, T₀ in **K**, β in **K**, R₀ resistance at T₀ | Simplified linearised model used in microcontroller code |
| 3 | **Resistance vs Temperature** | $R(T) = R_0\,\exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | R₀ known reference, β from datasheet | Direct R-to-T lookup in firmware |
| 4 | **Voltage Divider Output** | $V_{out} = V_{ref}\,\dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_{T}}$ | R_s fixed series resistor, R_T thermistor | ADC interface equation |
| 5 | **Temperature Coefficient** | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{R}\dfrac{dR}{dT} = -\dfrac{\beta}{T^{2}}$ | α in **K⁻¹** or **%/°C** | Sensitivity benchmark for sensor selection |
| 6 | **Self-Heating Limit** | $I_{max} < \sqrt{\dfrac{\delta\,(T_{max}-T_{amb})}{R_{T}}}$ | δ dissipation constant (W/°C) | Design rule to avoid sensor self-heating error |

> [!NOTE]
> Critical unit hygiene: **Temperatures in Steinhart-Hart and B-parameter equations MUST be in Kelvin (K)**. K = °C + **273.15**. A common student error is to plug °C directly into the equation, producing wildly incorrect answers.

---

### 2.3 Steinhart-Hart vs B-Parameter — When to Use Which?

- **B-parameter**: Quick, accurate over a narrow band (e.g., 0–70 °C medical / cold-chain). Uses 2 known resistance-temperature points.
- **Steinhart-Hart**: Used when accuracy better than ±0.1 °C is required across a wide span (−40 °C to +150 °C). Requires 3 calibration points (typically 0 °C, 25 °C, 70 °C).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numericals, the B-parameter is usually sufficient unless the question explicitly demands Steinhart-Hart. Always state the assumption in the first line of your answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold-Chain Logistics:** NTC 10 kΩ probes inside pharmaceutical reefers stream temperature to AWS IoT Core.
- **EV Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Each Li-ion cell uses an NTC for thermal-runaway detection.
- **3-D Printer Hot-Ends:** NTC 100 kΩ (β = 3950) inside the heater block.
- **Industrial Predictive Maintenance:** PTC thermistors embedded in motor windings trigger shutdown at 130 °C.
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-temperature NTCs modulate irrigation valves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — B-Parameter Equation From First Principles

The intrinsic semiconductor carrier concentration in a metal oxide obeys an Arrhenius-type activation:

$$
n \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{2k_BT}\right)
$$

Since resistance $R$ is inversely proportional to carrier density:

$$
R(T) \propto \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{2k_BT}\right)
$$

**Step 1 —** Take the ratio of resistances at two temperatures $T$ and $T_0$ to eliminate the proportionality constant:

$$
\dfrac{R(T)}{R(T_0)} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{2k_B}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 2 —** Define the **B-value** as $\beta = \dfrac{E_a}{2k_B}$. Substitute:

$$
\dfrac{R(T)}{R_0} = \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 3 —** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{R_0}\right) = \beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)
$$

**Step 4 —** Rearrange to isolate $1/T$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_0} + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{R_0}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the canonical **B-parameter equation** examiners love. Final boxed answer must include the units: **T, T₀ in Kelvin; β in Kelvin**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Steinhart-Hart Equation From Curve Fitting

A more accurate model fits three calibration data points $(R_1, T_1), (R_2, T_2), (R_3, T_3)$ and assumes a cubic relation in $\ln R$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B\ln(R) + C\bigl[\ln(R)\bigr]^{3}
$$

The constants $A, B, C$ are solved by inverting the 3×3 Vandermonde system:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & \ln R_1 & (\ln R_1)^{3} \\
1 & \ln R_2 & (\ln R_2)^{3} \\
1 & \ln R_3 & (\ln R_3)^{3}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} A \\ B \\ C \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 1/T_1 \\ 1/T_2 \\ 1/T_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving (Cramer's rule, often pre-computed by manufacturer):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \left[ \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)(\ln R_2 - \ln R_3) - \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_3}\right)(\ln R_1 - \ln R_2) \right] \Big/ \\
&\qquad \left[ (\ln R_1 - \ln R_2)\bigl[(\ln R_1)^{2} + (\ln R_1)(\ln R_2) + (\ln R_2)^{2} - (\ln R_2)(\ln R_3) - (\ln R_3)^{2}\bigr]\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** You will *not* be asked to invert the matrix in ESE, but you must **state the form, identify the calibration triplets, and interpret the constants** for full marks.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

> **Problem (Model Q):** An NTC thermistor has $R_{25} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and B-value $\beta = 3950\ \text{K}$. It is connected in a voltage divider with a 10 kΩ fixed resistor across $V_{cc} = 3.3\ \text{V}$ (thermistor at the bottom). The ADC reads $V_{out} = 1.85\ \text{V}$. Find the measured temperature.

**Step 1 — Find the thermistor resistance from the divider.**

$$
V_{out} = V_{cc}\,\dfrac{R_T}{R_s + R_T} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R_T = \dfrac{V_{out}\,R_s}{V_{cc} - V_{out}}
$$

$$
R_T = \dfrac{1.85 \times 10\,000}{3.3 - 1.85} = \dfrac{18\,500}{1.45} = 12\,758.6\ \Omega \approx 12.76\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the B-parameter equation. Convert to Kelvin first.**

$$
T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15\ \text{K}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{298.15} + \dfrac{1}{3950}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{12\,758.6}{10\,000}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the log term.**

$$
\ln(1.27586) = 0.24360
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = 0.0033540 + \dfrac{0.24360}{3950} = 0.0033540 + 0.00006167 = 0.0034157\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert and convert back to °C.**

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{0.0034157} = 292.75\ \text{K}
$$

$$
T_{^\circ C} = 292.75 - 273.15 = 19.6\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $R_T = 12.76\ \text{k}\Omega$ is **higher** than the 25 °C value of 10 kΩ, so the temperature must be **lower** than 25 °C. Our 19.6 °C result satisfies this physical intuition.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Scenarios

| Scenario | Given | To Find | Key Equation | Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cold-chain IOT | R at two temps | β | $\beta = \dfrac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}$ | 3 |
| Sensor calibration | Three R–T pairs | A, B, C of Steinhart-Hart | Matrix solve above | 7 |
| ADC interface | $V_{out}, R_s, V_{cc}$ | $R_T$ | Voltage divider | 2 |
| Sensitivity check | β, T range | α (%/°C) | $\alpha = -\beta/T^2$ | 2 |
| Self-heating | δ, $I_{max}$ | Allowed ambient | $I^2 R_T < \delta(T_{max}-T_a)$ | 4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `procA`); labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text, no bold/italic tags. Reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are never used as node names.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Thermistor in an IIoT Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PHYSICAL PROCESS BOILER OR MOTOR] --> H[HEAT FLUX Q WATTS]
    H --> T[NTC THERMISTOR BEAD]
    T --> R[RESISTANCE R T OHM]
    R --> D[VOLTAGE DIVIDER R S FIXED]
    D --> V[DC VOLTAGE V OUT 0 TO 3V3]
    V --> A[MCU ADC CHANNEL 12 BIT]
    A --> M[MCU FIRMWARE STEINHART HART OR B EQUATION]
    M --> S[PUBLISH JSON PAYLOAD]
    S --> N[MQTT BROKER MOSQUITTO]
    N --> C[CLOUD DASHBOARD GRAFANA]
    C --> AL[ALERT THERMAL RUNAWAY SMS EMAIL]

    classDef sensor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef compute fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef network fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class T,R,D sensor
    class V,A,M compute
    class S,N,C,AL network
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Schematic — ADC Front End

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC[VCC 3V3] --> RS[FIXED R S 10 K]
    RS --> NODE[NODE X]
    NODE --> RT[NTC R T 10K AT 25C]
    RT --> GND[GND]
    NODE --> ADC[MCU ADC INPUT]
    ADC --> CAP[100 NF DECOUPLING CAP]
    CAP --> GND

    classDef active fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ground fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class RS,RT,NODE,ADC active
    class GND,CAP ground
```

---

### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit Detail (Voltage Divider Biasing)

The thermistor forms the **lower leg** of the divider so that $V_{out}$ *increases* with temperature (easier to monitor "rising temperature" alarms):

- $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot R_T / (R_S + R_T)$
- When $T \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; R_T \downarrow \;\Rightarrow\; V_{out} \downarrow$
- For monotonic rise with T, swap the positions (thermistor on top).

> [!TIP]
> Always include a **100 nF ceramic capacitor** in parallel with the ADC pin to filter high-frequency EMI from the motor drive.

---

### 4.5 State Machine — Temperature Processing in Firmware

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> READ_ADC: Configure ADC 12 bit
    READ_ADC --> CONVERT_VOLTS: V = ADC x 3V3 / 4095
    CONVERT_VOLTS --> COMPUTE_R: R T = V x R S / 3V3 MINUS V
    COMPUTE_R --> COMPUTE_T: Apply Steinhart Hart or B equation
    COMPUTE_T --> FILTER: Exponential moving average alpha 0.2
    FILTER --> PUBLISH: MQTT publish every 5 s
    PUBLISH --> READ_ADC
    PUBLISH --> ALARM_CHECK: If T greater than 80 C
    ALARM_CHECK --> PUBLISH: Send alert JSON
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Distinguish between NTC and PTC thermistors. State two industrial applications of each.*

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

| Feature | NTC | PTC |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Negative Temperature Coefficient | Positive Temperature Coefficient |
| dR/dT sign | Negative | Positive |
| Material | Mn, Ni, Co oxides | BaTiO₃ doped |
| Sensitivity | High −4 to −6 % per °C | Low, but sharp at Curie point |
| Application 1 | Battery cell monitoring | Motor winding protection |
| Application 2 | HVAC air duct sensing | Self-resetting fuse |

**(3 marks: 1 mark NTC definition + 1 mark PTC definition + 1 mark applications)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, S2 Supplementary]**
*What is the B-value of a thermistor? An NTC has $R_{25}=10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_{100}=1\ \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the B-value.*

**Model Solution:**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{\ln(R_{25}/R_{100})}{(1/T_{100}-1/T_{25})}
$$

$T_{25} = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $T_{100} = 373.15\ \text{K}$

$$
\beta = \dfrac{\ln(10)}{(1/373.15 - 1/298.15)} = \dfrac{2.3026}{-0.000758} = 3038\ \text{K}
$$

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks numerical)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Steinhart-Hart + Signal Conditioning**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a thermistor-based temperature measurement system in an IIoT environment. Identify each block from physical process to cloud dashboard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Steinhart-Hart equation for an NTC thermistor. A thermistor has the following calibration data: $(R, T) = (10000\ \Omega,\ 298.15\ \text{K}),\ (4079\ \Omega,\ 323.15\ \text{K}),\ (1000\ \Omega,\ 373.15\ \text{K})$. Compute the constants A, B, C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

1. **Physical process** — Heat flux $Q$ from the industrial process (boiler, motor, furnace) impinges on the thermistor bead. **(1 mark)**
2. **Transduction** — NTC resistance $R_T$ changes with bead temperature according to $R_T = R_0\exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Signal conditioning** — Voltage divider converts $R_T$ into $V_{out} = V_{cc}R_T/(R_S+R_T)$. **(1 mark)**
4. **ADC** — Embedded 12-bit ADC digitizes $V_{out}$ into 0–4095 counts. **(1 mark)**
5. **MCU compute** — Firmware applies Steinhart-Hart to convert counts → temperature. **(1 mark)**
6. **Network** — MQTT publish over Wi-Fi / LoRaWAN to broker (Mosquitto/EMQX). **(1 mark)**
7. **Cloud** — Dashboard visualisation (Grafana) and threshold-based alerts. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution — (b):**

The Steinhart-Hart equation:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B\ln(R) + C[\ln(R)]^3
$$

Let $y_i = 1/T_i$ and $x_i = \ln R_i$:

| i | $R_i$ | $x_i = \ln R_i$ | $x_i^3$ | $y_i = 1/T_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10000 | 9.2103 | 781.0 | 0.0033540 |
| 2 | 4079 | 8.3128 | 574.6 | 0.0030943 |
| 3 | 1000 | 6.9078 | 329.6 | 0.0026799 |

The 3×3 system:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 9.2103 & 781.0 \\
1 & 8.3128 & 574.6 \\
1 & 6.9078 & 329.6
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} A \\ B \\ C \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
0.0033540 \\ 0.0030943 \\ 0.0026799
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving (Cramer's rule — calculator permitted in exam):

- $C = 1.246\times 10^{-7}$
- $B = 2.367\times 10^{-4}$
- $A = 8.213\times 10^{-4}$

**(7 marks: 1 mark equation statement + 1 mark table + 1 mark matrix + 3 marks solving + 1 mark conclusion)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — B-Parameter + ADC Interface Design**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Derive the B-parameter equation of an NTC thermistor from the Arrhenius relation. Show that the temperature coefficient of resistance $\alpha = -\beta/T^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 kΩ NTC ($β = 3950$ K, $R_{25} = 10$ kΩ) is interfaced to a 3.3 V MCU via a series resistor $R_S$. Design $R_S$ for maximum sensitivity at 50 °C and find the expected ADC count at 80 °C if the ADC is 10-bit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

From Arrhenius: $R(T) = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$. Take log and differentiate:

$$
\ln R = \ln R_0 + \beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)
$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $T$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{R}\dfrac{dR}{dT} = -\dfrac{\beta}{T^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{1}{R}\dfrac{dR}{dT} = -\dfrac{\beta}{T^{2}}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 marks derivation + 2 marks differentiation + 3 marks final expression)**

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1 — Find $R_T$ at the design centre 50 °C = 323.15 K.**

$$
R_T(50) = 10\,000 \cdot \exp\!\left[3950\!\left(\dfrac{1}{323.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15}\right)\right]
$$

$$
= 10\,000 \cdot \exp[-1.246] = 10\,000 \cdot 0.2878 = 2878\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum sensitivity in a divider occurs when $R_S = R_T$ at the design centre. So $R_S \approx 2.87\ \text{k}\Omega$ (use standard 2.87 kΩ or 2.7 kΩ).** **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Find $R_T$ at 80 °C = 353.15 K.**

$$
R_T(80) = 10\,000 \cdot \exp\!\left[3950\!\left(\dfrac{1}{353.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15}\right)\right]
$$

$$
= 10\,000 \cdot \exp[-2.067] = 10\,000 \cdot 0.1265 = 1265\ \Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $V_{out}$ and ADC count.**

$$
V_{out} = 3.3 \times \dfrac{1265}{2870 + 1265} = 3.3 \times 0.3060 = 1.010\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\text{ADC count} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{3.3} \times (2^{10} - 1) = 0.3060 \times 1023 = 313
$$

**(7 marks: 2 marks step 1, 2 marks step 2, 2 marks step 3, 1 mark step 4)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** before substituting into B-parameter equation — instant 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing R₀ and R₂₅** terminology — the symbol is usually $R_{25}$ for 25 °C reference.
> 3. **Writing the wrong sign for α** — for NTC it is *negative*. Students often write +β/T². Deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring self-heating** — in numericals, if $V_{cc}$ exceeds 5 V across a 10 kΩ bead, mention the dissipation check.
> 5. **In Part B (b)** — forgetting to state the assumption that $R_S = R_T$ gives maximum sensitivity (this is a 2-mark concept that many skip).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] Thermistor = *thermal* + *resistor*; passive semiconductor transducer.
- [ ] Two flavours: **NTC** (resistance drops with T) and **PTC** (resistance rises with T).
- [ ] NTC sensitivity ≈ **−4 to −6 %/°C**, far higher than RTD (~0.385 %/°C).
- [ ] **B-parameter equation**: $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_0} + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\ln(R/R_0)$, T in **Kelvin**.
- [ ] **Steinhart-Hart**: 3-parameter cubic, accuracy < 0.01 °C over wide range.
- [ ] **Temperature coefficient**: $\alpha = -\beta/T^2$ (negative for NTC).
- [ ] **Voltage divider** is the standard IIoT ADC interface; place 100 nF cap for noise.
- [ ] For **maximum sensitivity** at a design temperature, choose $R_S = R_T$ at that point.
- [ ] **Self-heating** is a real error source — keep dissipation below δ(T_max − T_amb).
- [ ] Standard reference: $R_{25}$ measured at 25 °C = **298.15 K**.
- [ ] B-value range: typically **3000–5000 K** for general-purpose NTCs.
- [ ] Industrial use cases: BMS, HVAC, cold chain, motor protection, 3-D printer hot-ends.
- [ ] In KTU answers, always: state assumption → show equation → substitute with units → final answer with correct significant figures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Voltage Sensor # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Voltage Sensor** is an electronic transducer that detects, measures, and converts an analog electrical potential difference (measured in **Volts (V)**) into a standardized, processable output signal — typically an analog voltage scaled to a microcontroller-compatible range (0–3.3 V, 0–5 V) or a digital value via an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. In the context of **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, a voltage sensor is a foundational **physical-layer (PHY) data acquisition block** that bridges high-voltage industrial machinery (e.g., 230 V AC mains, 415 V three-phase, 24 V DC control rails, battery banks) with low-voltage embedded sensing nodes (e.g., ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi, PLCs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A voltage sensor is a device used to detect and measure the potential difference between two points in an electrical circuit, providing an output signal proportional to the input voltage for monitoring, protection, and control applications in IIoT systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at the top of a water tank and you want to know **how much water is inside** without actually opening the tank. You could: 1. **Listen** to the sound of water splashing (acoustic sensing). 2. **Feel** the weight of the tank (force sensing). 3. **Use a pressure gauge** at the bottom (pressure sensing). A **voltage sensor** is the "pressure gauge" of the electrical world. Just as a pressure gauge translates the invisible force of water into a readable number, a voltage sensor translates the invisible "electrical pressure" (potential difference) into a readable signal. In an industrial plant: - The **power line** is like a high-pressure water main. - The **machine** is the equipment being powered. - The **voltage sensor** is the gauge on the wall that tells the operator (or the IIoT dashboard) whether the electrical "pressure" is healthy, too low (under-voltage), or dangerously high (over-voltage). > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:** > - **Unit:** Volt (V), with subunits mV, kV > - **ADC Reference Voltage:** $V_{ref}$ (typically **3.3 V** or **5 V**) > - **Industrial Standard:** 0–10 V, 4–20 mA, ±10 V (for legacy systems) > - **Safety Threshold (IEC 60950):** Below **50 V AC** / **120 V DC** considered safe extra-low voltage (SELV) ## 1.3 Classification of Voltage Sensors Voltage sensors used in IIoT can be broadly classified as follows: | Type | Operating Principle | Typical Range | IIoT Use Case | |------|---------------------|---------------|---------------| | **Resistive Voltage Divider** | Ohm's Law — scales down voltage | 0–1000 V DC | Battery monitoring, solar PV | | **Hall-Effect Voltage Sensor** | Magnetic field generated by current proportional to voltage | 0–1500 V | Motor drives, SMPS feedback | | **Capacitive Voltage Transformer (CVT)** | Capacitive coupling + step-down | kV range | Power grid substation metering | | **Potential Transformer (PT)** | Electromagnetic induction (step-down) | 0–36 kV | High-voltage transmission monitoring | | **Optocoupler-Based Isolated Sensor** | Optical isolation using LED + photodiode | 0–250 V AC | PLC digital input cards | | **Active Conditioned Sensor Module** | On-board op-amp + Zener protection | 0–25 V | Arduino/ESP32 plug-in modules | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Operational Breakdown A voltage sensor operates through a well-defined **signal conditioning chain**. The core steps are: 1. **Input Voltage Reception:** The sensor terminals (V+ and V− or GND) are connected across the load/device under measurement. 2. **Attenuation (Scaling Down):** High voltages are scaled down using a resistive divider, transformer, or Hall-effect element to a level safe for downstream electronics. 3. **Signal Conditioning:** - **Level Shifting:** Adjusts the signal so that its minimum is 0 V (or matches ADC ground). - **Filtering:** A low-pass RC filter removes switching noise (typical cutoff: **1 kHz–10 kHz**). - **Clamping/Protection:** Zener diodes or TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor) diodes clamp the output to within ADC limits. 4. **Isolation (in industrial-grade sensors):** Galvanic isolation via **opto-couplers**, **transformers**, or **capacitive barriers** prevents ground loops and protects the low-voltage IIoT node from surges. 5. **Output Generation:** The conditioned signal is delivered to: - The **ADC** of a microcontroller, which converts it to a **digital count** $D$. - Or directly to a **comparator** for threshold-based digital alarms. 6. **Software Reconstruction:** The microcontroller firmware uses the formula below to reconstruct the original voltage. ## 2.2 The Voltage Divider — Core Building Block The **resistive voltage divider** is the most common and conceptually important sub-block. Two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ are connected in series across the input voltage $V_{in}$. The output is taken across $R_2$: $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ This ratio is called the **division ratio** $K$: $$K = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a Divider is Needed:** A microcontroller's ADC cannot tolerate more than **3.3 V** or **5 V**. If we connect 230 V AC directly, the microcontroller will instantly be destroyed. The divider brings the voltage into a safe measurable range. ## 2.3 ADC Conversion Formula Once $V_{out}$ is within ADC range, the **digital reading** $D$ produced by an $n$-bit ADC is: $$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$$ Where: - $V_{ref}$ = ADC reference voltage (e.g., **3.3 V**) - $n$ = resolution in bits (e.g., **10-bit** for ESP32, **12-bit** for Arduino Uno, **16-bit** for ADS1115) - $2^n - 1$ = maximum digital count The reconstructed input voltage is then: $$V_{in} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{(2^n - 1)} \cdot \frac{1}{K}$$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | Voltage divider output | V | | 2 | $K = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | Division ratio (dimensionless) | — | | 3 | $D = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$ | ADC digital output | counts | | 4 | $V_{in} = D \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{(2^n-1)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{K}$ | Reconstructed input voltage | V | | 5 | $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS value of sine wave | V | | 6 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC filter time constant | s | | 7 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Low-pass filter cutoff frequency | Hz | | 8 | $V_{out}^{max} = I_{divider}^{max} \cdot (R_1 + R_2)$ | Power dissipation in divider | W | | 9 | $I_{divider} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Current through voltage divider | A | | 10 | $P_{divider} = \dfrac{V_{in}^2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Power lost in voltage divider | W | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Always design $R_1 + R_2$ such that the divider current is between **0.1 mA and 1 mA** to balance accuracy (high current) and power loss (low current). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In IIoT deployments, voltage sensors enable: - **Predictive Maintenance:** Monitoring motor supply voltage sag indicates bearing wear or load imbalance. - **Energy Management:** Real-time voltage measurement combined with current sensors yields **instantaneous power** $P = V \cdot I$ and **energy** $E = \int P \, dt$. - **Grid Synchronization:** Voltage sensors in solar inverters ensure phase/frequency match before grid connection. - **Battery Health Monitoring (BMS):** Cell voltage monitoring prevents over-charge/over-discharge in Li-ion packs. - **Equipment Protection:** Under-voltage and over-voltage relays disconnect loads during faults. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Input Voltage Reconstruction from ADC Reading **Problem Statement:** A voltage sensor module uses a resistive divider with $R_1 = 30\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. The output is fed to a **10-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 3.3\,\text{V}$. The ADC returns a digital value of $D = 620$. Compute the original input voltage $V_{in}$. ### Step 1 — Compute the Division Ratio We apply the voltage divider formula: $$K = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Substituting the values: $$K = \frac{10{,}000}{30{,}000 + 10{,}000} = \frac{10{,}000}{40{,}000}$$ $$K = 0.25$$ > **Interpretation:** The output is **one-quarter** of the input voltage. So if we read 1 V at the divider output, the actual input is 4 V. ### Step 2 — Convert ADC Digital Reading to Output Voltage For a 10-bit ADC, the maximum count is: $$2^{10} - 1 = 1023$$ Applying the ADC formula: $$V_{out} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$ $$V_{out} = \frac{620 \cdot 3.3}{1023}$$ $$V_{out} = \frac{2046}{1023} = 2.0000\,\text{V}$$ > **Note:** The coincidence of $D = 620$ yielding exactly **2.0000 V** is a pedagogical choice for clarity. In practice, integer rounding produces a value very close to this. ### Step 3 — Reconstruct the Original Input Voltage We invert the divider relationship: $$V_{in} = \frac{V_{out}}{K}$$ $$V_{in} = \frac{2.0000}{0.25}$$ $$V_{in} = 8.0\,\text{V}$$ > **Conclusion:** The original input voltage at the sensor terminals is **8.0 V**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: AC Voltage Measurement Using a DC-Biased Divider **Problem Statement:** A voltage sensor must measure a **230 V AC, 50 Hz** mains supply. The output must be biased to **1.65 V midpoint** (for a 3.3 V ADC) and scaled such that the peak AC signal swings between 0 V and 3.3 V. Design the divider and bias network. ### Step 1 — Determine the Peak Voltage $$V_{peak} = V_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \cdot 1.4142 \approx 325.27\,\text{V}$$ ### Step 2 — Determine the Required Division Ratio We want $V_{peak}$ at the divider output to be **1.65 V** (half of 3.3 V, so the signal swings symmetrically around the bias point): $$K = \frac{V_{peak}^{target}}{V_{peak}^{input}} = \frac{1.65}{325.27}$$ $$K \approx 0.005073$$ ### Step 3 — Choose R₂ and Compute R₁ Select $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Then: $$R_1 = R_2 \cdot \frac{1 - K}{K}$$ $$R_1 = 10{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.005073}{0.005073} = 10{,}000 \cdot 196.131$$ $$R_1 \approx 1.961\,\text{M}\Omega$$ > **Practical Note:** In real designs, $R_1$ is split into multiple series resistors (e.g., four 470 kΩ resistors) to distribute the voltage stress and improve surge-withstand capability. ### Step 4 — Verify Divider Current and Power $$I_{divider} = \frac{V_{RMS}}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{230}{1{,}971{,}000} \approx 0.1167\,\text{mA}$$ $$P_{divider} = \frac{V_{RMS}^2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{230^2}{1{,}971{,}000} \approx 26.84\,\text{mW}$$ > **Acceptable:** A 1/4 W (250 mW) resistor for $R_1$ is more than sufficient. ### Step 5 — Compute the Required Low-Pass Filter Cutoff To reject switching noise above **1 kHz**, choose a filter capacitor $C = 100\,\text{nF}$ across $R_2$: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_2 C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$f_c = \frac{1}{6.283 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 159.15\,\text{Hz}$$ > **Result:** The filter passes the 50 Hz mains frequency with significant margin and blocks noise above ~159 Hz. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Resistor Selection for Common Voltage Ranges | Target Max Input | $V_{ref}$ (ADC) | Resolution | Recommended $R_1$ | Recommended $R_2$ | $K$ | |------------------|-----------------|------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----| | 5 V | 3.3 V | 12-bit | 5.1 kΩ | 10 kΩ | 0.6623 | | 12 V | 3.3 V | 12-bit | 30 kΩ | 10 kΩ | 0.2500 | | 24 V | 3.3 V | 12-bit | 75 kΩ | 11 kΩ | 0.1279 | | 48 V | 3.3 V | 12-bit | 150 kΩ | 10.7 kΩ | 0.0667 | | 230 V AC | 3.3 V | 12-bit | 1.96 MΩ | 10 kΩ | 0.0051 | | 415 V AC | 3.3 V | 12-bit | 3.6 MΩ | 10 kΩ | 0.0028 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always add a Zener diode (e.g., 3.6 V) at the ADC pin** to protect against transients that could exceed $V_{ref}$. --- ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: RMS Voltage Computation in Firmware For AC measurement, the microcontroller samples the biased waveform many times per cycle and computes the **true RMS**: $$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^2}$$ Where $v_i$ is the **reconstructed instantaneous voltage** at sample $i$, with the DC bias removed: $$v_i = V_{sample}^{i} - V_{bias}$$ > **Numerical Example:** Suppose 1000 samples of a 50 Hz waveform are taken at 5 kHz sampling rate. The squared deviations are summed and divided by 1000, then square-rooted. For a clean 230 V RMS sine wave biased at 1.65 V, this computation should yield **230 V** (after scaling). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Voltage Sensor Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB srcA[HV Source
230V AC Mains] --> divA srcB[DC Bus
24V to 48V] --> divB srcC[Battery Cell
3.7V Li-ion] --> divC divA[Resistive Divider
R1 high R2 low
Ratio 0.0051] --> protA divB[Resistive Divider
Ratio 0.1279] --> protB divC[Resistive Divider
Ratio 0.6623] --> protC protA[Protection Stage
Zener Diode 3.6V
TVS Clamp] --> filtA protB[Protection Stage
Zener Diode 3.6V] --> filtB protC[Protection Stage
Zener Diode 3.6V] --> filtC filtA[RC Low-Pass Filter
fc 159 Hz] --> biasA filtB[RC Low-Pass Filter
fc 1.5 kHz] --> biasB filtC[RC Low-Pass Filter
fc 15 kHz] --> biasC biasA[DC Bias Network
Midpoint 1.65V] --> muxA biasB[DC Bias Network
Midpoint 1.65V] --> muxA biasC[DC Bias Network
Optional] --> muxA muxA[Analog Multiplexer
Channel Select] --> adcA adcA[12-bit ADC
Vref 3.3V
4096 counts] --> mcuA mcuA[Microcontroller
ESP32 STM32] --> cloudA cloudA[IIoT Gateway
MQTT Publish
AWS IoT Azure] style srcA fill:#ffcccc style srcB fill:#ffe0b3 style srcC fill:#fff5cc style adcA fill:#cce5ff style mcuA fill:#d4edda style cloudA fill:#e2d4f0 ``` ## 4.2 Signal Conditioning Pipeline (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR v1[Raw Input Voltage
0 to 1000V] --> v2[Attenuation
Voltage Divider] v2 --> v3[Transient Protection
TVS and Zener] v3 --> v4[Galvanic Isolation
Optocoupler or Hall] v4 --> v5[Level Shifting
and DC Biasing] v5 --> v6[Low-Pass Filtering
RC Active] v6 --> v7[Amplification
Op-Amp Buffer] v7 --> v8[ADC Input
0 to 3.3V Range] v8 --> v9[Digital Code
0 to 4095] v9 --> v10[Reconstructed
Engineering Value] style v1 fill:#ffe6e6 style v8 fill:#cce5ff style v10 fill:#d4edda ``` ## 4.3 Software Reconstruction Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB start([Periodic ADC Interrupt]) --> read[Read ADC Register
Get Digital Count D] read --> convert[Compute Vout
Vout equals D times Vref divided by 4095] convert --> removebias{AC Signal?} removebias -- Yes --> sub[Subtract DC Bias
v equals Vout minus 1.65] removebias -- No --> scale[Proceed to Scaling] sub --> rms[Square the Sample
Accumulate Sum of Squares] rms --> count{All Samples Done?} count -- No --> read count -- Yes --> rmscalc[Divide by N then Square Root
Compute True RMS] scale --> scale2[Multiply by 1 over K
Reconstruct Vin] rmscalc --> scale2 scale2 --> check[Threshold Check
Under or Over Voltage] check --> alarm{Out of Range?} alarm -- Yes --> send[Send MQTT Alert
Publish to IIoT Cloud] alarm -- No --> store[Store in Local Buffer
Send Periodic Telemetry] store --> done([Wait for Next Cycle]) send --> done done --> start style alarm fill:#ffcccc style rmscalc fill:#fff5cc style scale2 fill:#cce5ff style store fill:#d4edda ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define a voltage sensor. List any four types of voltage sensors used in industrial applications.** **Model Answer:** A **voltage sensor** is an electronic device that detects the potential difference between two points in an electrical circuit and produces an output signal proportional to the measured voltage, suitable for monitoring, protection, or control systems in an IIoT framework. Four types of voltage sensors used in industrial applications are: 1. **Resistive Voltage Divider Sensor** — Uses two series resistors to scale down voltage according to Ohm's law. 2. **Hall-Effect Voltage Sensor** — Uses the magnetic field generated by a current proportional to the voltage being measured. 3. **Potential Transformer (PT)** — A step-down transformer used in high-voltage AC metering. 4. **Optocoupler-Based Isolated Sensor** — Provides galvanic isolation using an LED–photodiode pair. --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **State and explain the voltage divider formula. Why is it important in voltage sensing circuits?** **Model Answer:** The voltage divider formula is: $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ **Explanation:** When two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ are connected in series across a voltage source $V_{in}$, the voltage across $R_2$ is proportional to its share of the total resistance. The ratio $K = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ is called the **division ratio**. **Importance in Voltage Sensing:** - It scales down high voltages (e.g., 230 V) to a low level (e.g., 3.3 V) that the ADC of a microcontroller can safely read. - It is a **passive**, **low-cost**, and **highly reliable** method requiring no active components. - It forms the **front-end** of almost every commercial voltage sensor module used in IIoT nodes. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model – Set A]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a voltage sensor module used in an IIoT system for monitoring a 230 V AC mains supply. Describe the role of each block. **(b) [7 Marks]** A voltage sensor uses a resistive divider with $R_1 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. The output is connected to a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$. If the ADC produces a digital count of $D = 512$, calculate: 1. The divider ratio $K$. 2. The output voltage of the divider $V_{out}$. 3. The original input voltage $V_{in}$. 4. The minimum and maximum measurable input voltages. #### Model Solution to Part (a): The functional architecture of a voltage sensor module for 230 V AC mains monitoring in an IIoT system consists of the following blocks: 1. **High-Voltage Attenuation Stage (Resistive Divider):** A high-impedance resistive divider using $R_1 \approx 1.96\,\text{M}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ scales the 230 V RMS (325 V peak) down to a few volts. The high total resistance limits current to a safe level. 2. **Transient Protection Stage:** A **Zener diode** (e.g., 5.1 V) and a **Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV)** clamp voltage spikes caused by lightning, switching transients, or inductive loads. 3. **Galvanic Isolation Stage:** An **opto-coupler** (e.g., PC817) or **Hall-effect isolation amplifier** (e.g., AMC1200) provides galvanic isolation, breaking the ground loop and protecting the low-voltage IIoT side. 4. **Signal Conditioning Stage:** An op-amp circuit performs: - **DC biasing** to mid-supply (1.65 V) so the AC waveform swings between 0 V and 3.3 V. - **Active low-pass filtering** (cutoff ~1 kHz) to remove HF noise. 5. **ADC and Microcontroller Block:** A 12-bit ADC samples the conditioned signal at **≥2 kHz** (to satisfy Nyquist for 50 Hz mains). The microcontroller reconstructs the RMS voltage. 6. **Communication Block:** The microcontroller transmits data to the **IIoT gateway** using **MQTT over Wi-Fi/Ethernet/LoRa**. #### Model Solution to Part (b): **Given:** $R_1 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$, $n = 10$, $D = 512$. **(1) Divider Ratio:** $$K = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10{,}000}{100{,}000 + 10{,}000} = \frac{10{,}000}{110{,}000} = 0.0909$$ **(2) Output Voltage of the Divider:** The maximum count for a 10-bit ADC is $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$. $$V_{out} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{512 \cdot 5}{1023} = \frac{2560}{1023} \approx 2.5024\,\text{V}$$ **(3) Original Input Voltage:** $$V_{in} = \frac{V_{out}}{K} = \frac{2.5024}{0.0909} \approx 27.53\,\text{V}$$ **(4) Minimum and Maximum Measurable Input Voltages:** The ADC can output digital counts from $0$ to $1023$: - **Minimum input (when D = 0):** $$V_{in}^{min} = \frac{0 \cdot 5}{1023 \cdot 0.0909} = 0\,\text{V}$$ - **Maximum input (when D = 1023):** $$V_{in}^{max} = \frac{1023 \cdot 5}{1023 \cdot 0.0909} = \frac{5}{0.0909} \approx 55.0\,\text{V}$$ > **Interpretation:** The sensor can measure voltages from **0 V to 55 V** with this divider configuration. --- ### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model – Set B – Alternative Choice]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare resistive voltage divider, Hall-effect voltage sensor, and potential transformer based on: operating principle, voltage range, isolation provided, accuracy, and typical IIoT application. Use a comparative table. **(b) [7 Marks]** Design a voltage divider to measure a 48 V DC battery bank using a 3.3 V ADC (12-bit).
