If the sensor's minimum detectable frequency is $f_{min} = 50$ Hz, what is the minimum velocity it can detect? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
$f_0 = 10.525 \times 10^{9}$ Hz, $f_d = 150$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $f_{min} = 50$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Compute radial velocity for $f_d = 150$ Hz.**

$$v_r = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 f_0} = \frac{150 \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10.525 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$v_r = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{10}}{2.105 \times 10^{10}} = 2.138 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute minimum detectable velocity for $f_{min} = 50$ Hz.**

$$v_{r,min} = \frac{50 \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10.525 \times 10^{9}} = 0.713 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.** A velocity of 0.713 m/s corresponds to a slow, cautious walk (~2.5 km/h), making the sensor suitable for detecting crawling or slow-moving intruders in secure zones. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B — Module Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Past Year Pattern]`

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an HC-SR04 ultrasonic distance sensor in an IIoT context. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction.** The HC-SR04 is an active ranging sensor widely deployed in industrial autonomous guided vehicles (AGVs) and liquid-level measurement. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Block diagram description.** (3 marks)

```
   [MCU Trigger Pin] ----> [Transmitter 40kHz] ---> [Air Medium] ---> [Object]
   [MCU Echo Pin] <---- [Receiver 40kHz] <--- [Air Medium] <--- [Reflected Wave]
```

**Step 3 — Working steps.**
- The MCU sends a 10 µs HIGH pulse to the Trigger pin.
- The sensor emits eight 40 kHz ultrasonic bursts.
- The wave reflects off the object and returns to the receiver.
- The Echo pin goes HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time $t_{echo}$.
- The MCU uses a hardware timer to measure this pulse width.
- Distance is computed as $d = \frac{v_s \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$.

(2 marks for working)

**Step 4 — IIoT integration.** The computed distance is published over MQTT to the ThingsBoard cloud dashboard for real-time monitoring of silo levels or robotic obstacle distances. (1 mark)

#### (b) An ultrasonic sensor returns an echo pulse of width $4.7$ ms at a temperature of $30\,°C$. Find the distance to the obstacle. If the same obstacle is measured at $10\,°C$, what echo width would the MCU measure? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute speed of sound at 30°C.**

$$v_s = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 349 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute distance at 30°C.**

$$d = \frac{349 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{1.6403}{2} = 0.820 \text{ m} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute speed of sound at 10°C.**

$$v_s' = 331 + 0.6 \times 10 = 337 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute echo width at 10°C for the same physical distance $d = 0.820$ m.**

$$t_{echo}' = \frac{2d}{v_s'} = \frac{2 \times 0.820}{337} = 4.866 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Inference.** The echo width is *longer* at lower temperatures because sound travels slower. A naive MCU program that assumes a constant $v_s = 343$ m/s will systematically overestimate distance in winter. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In Doppler problems, forgetting the **factor of 2** in the numerator is the #1 reason for losing full marks. The wave encounters the moving object *twice* — once on the way out, once on the way back.
> 2. In ToF problems, students often forget to convert milliseconds to seconds (multiply by $10^{-3}$), resulting in distance values that are off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. Many students confuse the *frequency of the wave* with the *Doppler shift* itself. Always write the equation in terms of $f_d$, not $f_0$.
> 4. For Part A 3-mark questions, examiners expect a tabular or bullet-point format. A paragraph-style answer will lose presentation marks even if the content is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PIR sensors** are passive, detect **9.4 µm infrared** emitted by human bodies, and use a **dual pyroelectric element** to reject common-mode noise.
- **Ultrasonic sensors** are active, operate at **40 kHz**, and use the **time-of-flight** equation $d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$.
- **Microwave / Doppler sensors** are active, operate at **10.525 GHz**, and rely on the **Doppler shift** $f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$.
- **MEMS accelerometers** measure vibration by detecting proof-mass displacement via capacitance change; output $V_{out} = V_0 + S \cdot a$.
- The **speed of sound** is temperature-dependent: $v_s = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ m/s.
- The **factor of 2** in the Doppler equation is non-negotiable and frequently tested.
- **FSPL (Free-Space Path Loss)** grows logarithmically with both distance and frequency, which is why microwave sensors degrade in range at higher frequencies.
- **Dual-technology sensors** (PIR + Microwave) drastically reduce false alarms in industrial security systems.
- In an **IIoT stack**, motion sensors reside at the **Perception Layer (Layer 1)** and feed data to the **Edge Layer (Layer 2)** for pre-processing.
- For KTU valuation: always include **units**, **direction conventions** (e.g., "approaching" vs. "receding"), and a brief **physical interpretation** of the final numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Bluetooth Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wireless Bluetooth Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless Bluetooth Sensors** are battery-powered, low-energy embedded sensing devices that utilize the **Bluetooth wireless personal area network (WPAN) protocol** — particularly **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** — to wirelessly transmit telemetry data (temperature, pressure, vibration, humidity, motion, etc.) to a central gateway, smartphone, or industrial controller. In Industrial IoT (IIoT), these sensors operate primarily in the **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio band** and conform to the IEEE 802.15.1 specifications (legacy) and the Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG) standards for BLE (v4.0 onwards).

A Bluetooth sensor node is essentially a **three-stage transducer-radio stack**:
1. A **physical sensor** (e.g., thermistor, MEMS accelerometer) that converts a physical quantity into an analog/digital electrical signal.
2. A **System-on-Chip (SoC)** — typically Nordic nRF52/53, Texas Instruments CC26xx, or STM32WB series — that digitizes, processes, and packetizes the data.
3. A **2.4 GHz radio transceiver** that modulates the data using **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)** and transmits it over a BLE advertising or connected channel.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Loudspeaker in a Noisy Factory" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a factory floor where hundreds of small temperature gauges are mounted on machines. Wiring each one back to a central computer is a cabling nightmare. Instead, each gauge is fitted with a tiny **short-range voice broadcaster** (the BLE radio). When a gauge has a new reading, it quickly *shouts* it out — but only for a millisecond, on a specific "channel" (a specific frequency). The central computer (the *observer* or *central device*) is constantly *listening* on these channels. Because each shout is so brief and the gauge sleeps the rest of the time, a single **coin cell battery (3 V, 240 mAh)** can power it for **months or even years**. This is exactly how BLE sensors work — they spend **99% of their time in deep sleep**, waking up only to transmit a tiny data packet.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following canonical specifications govern every Bluetooth sensor in an IIoT deployment:
>
> - **Operating Frequency Band:** **2.400 GHz – 2.4835 GHz** (ISM band)
> - **Number of RF Channels (BLE):** **40 channels** (Channel 0–39), each **2 MHz** wide
> - **Modulation Scheme:** **GFSK** with a modulation index of **0.5** and a symbol rate of **1 Msymbol/s** (BLE 1M PHY)
> - **Transmit Power Range:** **−20 dBm to +20 dBm** (typical industrial: +4 dBm or +8 dBm)
> - **Reference Clock:** **32.768 kHz** low-frequency crystal for sleep timing
> - **Standard Coin Cell:** **CR2032**, **3 V**, **220–240 mAh**

### 1.4 Why Bluetooth Sensors are Critical in IIoT

| Industrial Requirement | Bluetooth Sensor Response |
|---|---|
| Long battery life (5–10 years) | BLE duty-cycled sleep, peak current < 15 mA |
| Easy pairing with smartphones | Native BLE Generic Access Profile (GAP) |
| Mesh networking for plant-wide coverage | Bluetooth Mesh (v5.0 onwards) |
| Secure industrial data | LE Secure Connections, AES-128 CCM |
| Low-cost BOM | Single SoC + passive components < $5 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bluetooth Protocol Stack for Sensors

A wireless Bluetooth sensor implements a **layered protocol stack** — understanding each layer is essential for KTU examination answers.

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer (PHY):**
- Operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band**.
- Uses **GFSK** modulation where a logical '1' is represented by a **positive frequency deviation** and a logical '0' by a **negative frequency deviation**.
- BLE defines **two PHYs** in v5.0:
  - **LE 1M PHY:** 1 Mbps data rate
  - **LE 2M PHY:** 2 Mbps data rate (double speed)
  - **LE Coded PHY:** 125 kbps or 500 kbps (4× range via Forward Error Correction — FEC)

**Layer 2 — Link Layer (LL):**
- Manages the **radio state machine** of the BLE device.
- Defines **5 states**: *Standby, Advertising, Scanning, Initiating, Connection*.
- Handles **packet assembly, whitening, and CRC-24** checksum.
- Controls the **hopping algorithm** — although BLE uses **fixed channels** (no hopping for data), advertising hops across 3 channels (37, 38, 39) to avoid interference.

**Layer 3 — Host Controller Interface (HCI):**
- A standardized serial command interface (UART/SPI) between the radio and the host MCU.
- Relevant mainly for **dual-chip** sensor designs.

**Layer 4 — Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP):**
- Performs **packet segmentation, reassembly, and multiplexing** of higher-layer services.
- For BLE sensors, it manages **LE Credit Based Flow Control**.

**Layer 5 — Attribute Protocol (ATT) & Generic Attribute Profile (GATT):**
- The **heart of a BLE sensor's data model**.
- Data is organized as a **hierarchical table of Attributes**.
- Each attribute contains: **Handle (2 bytes), UUID (2 or 16 bytes), Permissions, Value**.

**Layer 6 — Generic Access Profile (GAP):**
- Defines **roles** that a device plays:
  - *Broadcaster* (sensor transmitting only)
  - *Observer* (gateway receiving only)
  - *Peripheral* (sensor accepting connections)
  - *Central* (gateway initiating connections)

**Layer 7 — Security Manager Protocol (SMP):**
- Handles **pairing, bonding, and encryption** using **AES-128**.

### 2.2 The GATT Data Model — How a Sensor Exposes Data

> [!NOTE]
> A BLE sensor exposes its data through a **Service–Characteristic hierarchy**:
>
> **Profile → Service(s) → Characteristic(s) → Descriptor(s) → Value**
>
> Example: An industrial **temperature sensor** exposes a *Health Thermometer Service* (UUID 0x1809) which contains a *Temperature Measurement Characteristic* (UUID 0x2A1C) which holds a 4-byte value (flag byte + 24-bit IEEE-11073 32-bit FLOAT).

### 2.3 BLE Channel Architecture

The 40 BLE channels are partitioned as follows:

| Channel Type | Channel Index | Count | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Channels | 0 – 36 | 37 | Bidirectional connected communication |
| Advertising Channels | 37, 38, 39 | 3 | Discovery, broadcasting, connection setup |

This 3-channel advertising strategy is a **brilliant anti-interference trick**: Wi-Fi's most-used channels in the 2.4 GHz band are 1, 6, and 11, so BLE deliberately chose the *guard bands* (2402, 2426, 2480 MHz) to avoid collision.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantitative problems on Wireless Bluetooth Sensors in the KTU exam are solved using the equations below. Memorize the boundary conditions (in **bold**).

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_c = 2402 + k \cdot 2 \text{ MHz}$ | BLE channel center frequency (k = 0…39) | MHz |
| 2 | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | Bit duration; for 1M PHY: **1 µs**; for 2M PHY: **0.5 µs** | seconds |
| 3 | $E_{packet} = V \cdot I_{TX} \cdot t_{packet}$ | Energy consumed per transmission | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $I_{avg} = \dfrac{I_{TX} \cdot t_{TX} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{T_{period}}$ | Average current draw (duty-cycled) | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}}$ | Theoretical battery lifetime | hours |
| 6 | $R_{b} = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{T_{conn\_interval}}$ | Effective throughput (connected) | bits/second |
| 7 | $L_{total} = L_{preamble} + L_{access\_addr} + L_{header} + L_{payload} + L_{CRC}$ | BLE packet length (bytes) | bytes |
| 8 | $L_{max\_payload} = 27$ bytes (v4.x), $251$ bytes (v5.x) | Maximum application payload per packet | bytes |
| 9 | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{path}$ | Link budget / Friis-style power | dBm |
| 10 | $N_{hops} = \dfrac{T_{event}}{T_{conn\_interval}}$ | Number of channel hops per connection event | dimensionless |
| 11 | $D_{duty} = \dfrac{t_{active}}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}$ | Sleep duty cycle (typically **0.1 % to 1 %**) | ratio |
| 12 | $L_{path} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 147.55$ | Free-space path loss at distance $d$ (meters), $f$ in Hz | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *battery life equation* ($T_{life} = C/I_{avg}$) is the **single most exam-relevant formula**. In an IIoT deployment with a 240 mAh CR2032, if $I_{avg} = 10\,\mu A$, then $T_{life} = 24{,}000$ hours $\approx$ **2.74 years**. This is precisely why BLE dominates industrial wireless sensor design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: BLE Channel Center Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A BLE sensor is configured to advertise on the second advertising channel. Calculate the exact center frequency of the RF carrier.

**Step 1 — Identify the channel index.**
The three advertising channels are at $k = 37, 38, 39$.
The **second** advertising channel is therefore $k = 38$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the BLE frequency formula.**

$$
f_c = 2402 \text{ MHz} + k \cdot 2 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the arithmetic.**

$$
f_c = 2402 + (38 \times 2)
$$

$$
f_c = 2402 + 76
$$

$$
\boxed{f_c = 2478 \text{ MHz}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification via boundary check.**
The highest channel $k = 39$ yields $f_c = 2402 + 78 = 2480$ MHz, which exactly matches the upper guard band of the 2.4 GHz ISM spectrum. Our result (2478 MHz) is correctly bounded between 2402 MHz and 2480 MHz. **Validated.**

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Average Current and Battery Lifetime

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An industrial BLE temperature sensor operates with the following parameters: it transmits a packet every $T_{period} = 5$ seconds. Each transmission takes $t_{TX} = 3$ ms at a peak current $I_{TX} = 8$ mA. Between transmissions, it enters deep sleep with $I_{sleep} = 1.5\,\mu A$. The sensor is powered by a CR2032 coin cell of $C = 240$ mAh. Calculate the average current and the expected battery lifetime.

**Step 1 — State the average current formula.**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{I_{TX} \cdot t_{TX} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{T_{period}}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the sleep time.**
The sensor is *active* only for the 3 ms transmission; everything else is sleep.

$$
t_{sleep} = T_{period} - t_{TX} = 5.000 \text{ s} - 0.003 \text{ s} = 4.997 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the TX charge contribution per cycle.**

$$
Q_{TX} = I_{TX} \cdot t_{TX} = (8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}) \times (3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s})
$$

$$
Q_{TX} = 24 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C} = 24 \text{ µC per cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sleep charge contribution per cycle.**

$$
Q_{sleep} = I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep} = (1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}) \times (4.997 \text{ s})
$$

$$
Q_{sleep} = 7.4955 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C} \approx 7.50 \text{ µC per cycle}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the charge per cycle.**

$$
Q_{total} = 24 + 7.50 = 31.50 \text{ µC per 5 s cycle}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the average current.**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{T_{period}} = \dfrac{31.50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}}{5 \text{ s}}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{avg} = 6.30 \text{ µA}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the battery lifetime.**

$$
T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{240 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ah}}{6.30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}}
$$

$$
T_{life} = 38{,}095.24 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to years.**

$$
T_{life} = \dfrac{38{,}095.24}{24 \times 365} = \dfrac{38{,}095.24}{8760}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{life} \approx 4.35 \text{ years}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **76 % of the average current is the sleep current**, not the transmit current. This is why BLE SoCs invest enormous silicon area in ultra-low-power sleep modes (e.g., Nordic's *System OFF* mode with full RAM retention draws only **0.3 µA**). Optimizing sleep current is the *single biggest lever* in industrial sensor design.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Effective Throughput in a Connected Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A BLE pressure sensor in a connected state uses a connection interval of $T_{conn\_interval} = 50$ ms. Each connection event transfers the maximum 1M-PHY payload of $L = 244$ bytes (with the v5.x extension). Calculate the effective throughput.

**Step 1 — Convert payload to bits.**

$$
L_{bits} = 244 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 1952 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the throughput formula.**

$$
R_b = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{T_{conn\_interval}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numeric value.**

$$
R_b = \dfrac{1952 \text{ bits}}{50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} = 39{,}040 \text{ bits/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kbps.**

$$
\boxed{R_b \approx 39.04 \text{ kbps}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to theoretical maximum.**
The 1M PHY offers a raw 1 Mbps, but with protocol overhead (preamble, access address, header, CRC, inter-frame spacing, acknowledgment turnaround) and the fact that the radio is silent for **49.5 ms out of every 50 ms**, the *application-level throughput* drops to roughly **4 % of the raw bit rate**. This is fundamental — the link is *starvation-dominated*, not bandwidth-dominated.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Bluetooth Versions vs. Sensor Suitability

| Bluetooth Version | Release Year | Key Sensor-Relevant Feature | Max Payload | Range (outdoor) | Suitable for IIoT? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| v4.0 (BLE) | 2010 | Introduction of LE PHY | 27 B | ~50 m | Yes (legacy) |
| v4.1 | 2013 | Coexistence improvements | 27 B | ~50 m | Yes |
| v4.2 | 2014 | LE Privacy 1.2, LE Data Length Extension | 244 B | ~75 m | **Recommended minimum** |
| v5.0 | 2016 | 2M PHY, Coded PHY (4× range) | 244 B | **~200 m** (coded) | **Industry standard** |
| v5.1 | 2019 | Direction Finding (AoA/AoD) | 244 B | ~200 m | Asset tracking |
| v5.2 | 2020 | LE Audio, LE Power Control | 244 B | ~200 m | Audio + sensors |
| v5.3 | 2021 | Connection Subrating, Adv. Filtering | 244 B | ~200 m | **Current best** |
| v5.4 | 2023 | Periodic Adv. with Responses, EAD | 244 B | ~200 m | **PAwR for sensor meshes** |

> [!NOTE]
> **PAwR (Periodic Advertising with Responses)**, introduced in v5.4, is a game-changer for IIoT — it allows **thousands** of sensors to be addressed in a *star-bus* configuration by a single gateway, with bi-directional slot-based communication, making it ideal for **electronic shelf labels, sensor meshes, and large-scale industrial monitoring**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Block Diagram of a Wireless Bluetooth Sensor Node

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal architecture of a typical industrial BLE sensor node, from the physical phenomenon being measured to the radio transmission.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHY[Physical Phenomenon\nTemperature Pressure Vibration] --> SEN[Analog Sensor Front End\nThermistor MEMS Bridge]
    SEN --> ADC[ADC\n12 bit or 24 bit Sigma Delta]
    ADC --> MCU[BLE SoC\nNordic nRF52 Series]
    MCU --> PROC[Signal Processing Unit\nFIR Filter Calibration]
    PROC --> STACK[BLE Protocol Stack\nATT GATT GAP SMP]
    STACK --> RADIO[2.4 GHz Radio Transceiver\nGFSK Modulation]
    RADIO --> ANT[Antenna\nChip Antenna or PCB Trace]
    ANT -. BLE 2.4 GHz .-> GW[IIoT Gateway Smartphone PLC]
    MCU --> PWR[Power Management Unit]
    PWR --> BAT[CR2032 Coin Cell 3V 240mAh]
    PWR --> REG[LDO Voltage Regulator 1.8V]
```

### 4.2 BLE Radio State Machine (Link Layer)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> Advertising : Sensor sends ADV_IND
    Standby --> Scanning : Observer looks for sensors
    Advertising --> Connection : Central sends CONNECT_REQ
    Scanning --> Initiating : Central sends CONNECT_REQ
    Initiating --> Connection : Link established
    Connection --> Standby : TERMINATE_IND received
    Connection --> Advertising : Re advertising after disconnect
    Scanning --> Standby : Scan window timeout
```

### 4.3 GATT Data Hierarchy (Sensor Service Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PRO[BLE Sensor Profile] --> SVC1[Environmental Sensing Service\nUUID 0x181A]
    PRO --> SVC2[Device Information Service\nUUID 0x180A]
    SVC1 --> CH1[Temperature Characteristic\nUUID 0x2A6E Read Notify]
    SVC1 --> CH2[Humidity Characteristic\nUUID 0x2A6F Read Notify]
    SVC1 --> CH3[Pressure Characteristic\nUUID 0x2A6D Read Notify]
    SVC2 --> CH4[Manufacturer Name\nUUID 0x2A29 Read]
    SVC2 --> CH5[Firmware Revision\nUUID 0x2A26 Read]
    CH1 --> DESC1[CCCD Descriptor\nUUID 0x2902]
    CH1 --> DESC2[User Description\nUUID 0x2901]
    CH1 --> VAL1[Sensor Value\nIEEE 11073 32 bit FLOAT]
```

### 4.4 Piconet and Scatternet Topology (Classical Bluetooth vs. BLE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Classical Bluetooth supports a **Piconet** (1 master + up to 7 active slaves) and **Scatternet** (interconnected piconets). BLE, however, uses a **simpler star topology** with an essentially unlimited number of slaves in advertising mode, and supports **Bluetooth Mesh** for many-to-many in v5.0+.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PiconetA[Piconet 1]
        MA[Master Device]
        SA1[Slave 1]
        SA2[Slave 2]
        SA3[Slave 3]
    end
    subgraph PiconetB[Piconet 2]
        MB[Master Device]
        SB1[Slave 1]
        SB2[Slave 2]
    end
    MA -. Scatternet Bridge .- MB
    MA --- SA1
    MA --- SA2
    MA --- SA3
    MB --- SB1
    MB --- SB2
```

### 4.5 Bluetooth Mesh Architecture (Industrial Use)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRV[Provisioner\nSmartphone Gateway] --> N1[Sensor Node 1\nTemperature]
    PRV --> N2[Sensor Node 2\nVibration]
    PRV --> N3[Sensor Node 3\nHumidity]
    PRV --> N4[Sensor Node 4\nPressure]
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N1
    N1 --> N5[Relay Node\nForwarding]
    N2 --> N5
    N5 --> GW[IIoT Gateway\nSink Node]
    N3 --> GW
    N4 --> GW
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a wireless Bluetooth sensor node used in an IIoT application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A wireless Bluetooth sensor node consists of four major functional blocks:

1. **Sensing Sub-system:** A physical transducer (e.g., thermistor, MEMS accelerometer) followed by a signal-conditioning analog front end and an **ADC (typically 12-bit to 24-bit sigma-delta)**.
2. **Processing Sub-system:** A **BLE SoC** (e.g., Nordic nRF52840) that runs the embedded firmware, performs local calibration, and executes the BLE protocol stack (ATT/GATT/GAP).
3. **Radio Sub-system:** A **2.4 GHz GFSK transceiver** that transmits the packetized sensor data over BLE advertising or connected channels.
4. **Power Sub-system:** A **CR2032 coin cell (3 V, 240 mAh)** feeding an LDO regulator (1.8 V) that powers the SoC in deep-sleep and active modes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key:** *Sensing block (1 M) + Processing + Radio (1 M) + Power block (1 M).* A neat labelled diagram is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*List the various states defined in the BLE Link Layer and explain the role of the advertising channels.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The BLE Link Layer defines **five states**:
1. **Standby** – Does not transmit or receive; default idle state.
2. **Advertising** – The sensor transmits *ADV_IND* packets on the three advertising channels.
3. **Scanning** – The observer listens for advertising packets.
4. **Initiating** – The central sends a *CONNECT_REQ* to establish a link.
5. **Connection** – Bi-directional data exchange over data channels.

**Role of Advertising Channels:** The 3 advertising channels are at **2402 MHz, 2426 MHz, and 2480 MHz**. They are deliberately placed at the **edges of the 2.4 GHz ISM band** to avoid interference with Wi-Fi channels 1, 6, and 11, which are centred at 2412, 2437, and 2462 MHz respectively. The sensor hops pseudo-randomly across these three channels during advertising to maximize discovery probability.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **GATT data model** used by a BLE sensor to expose temperature and humidity readings to a central gateway. Explain the role of *Services*, *Characteristics*, and *Descriptors*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial BLE vibration sensor operates with the following parameters:
- Transmission interval: $T_{period} = 2$ s
- Active TX duration: $t_{TX} = 4$ ms
- TX current: $I_{TX} = 12$ mA
- Sleep current: $I_{sleep} = 2\,\mu A$
- Battery: CR2032, $C = 240$ mAh

Compute the **average current** and the **expected battery lifetime in months**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — GATT Data Model (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Introduction to GATT (1 mark).**
The **Generic Attribute Profile (GATT)** is the BLE framework that defines *how* a sensor's data is organized, discovered, and exchanged. All data is exposed as **Attributes** stored in a table on the GATT server (the sensor).

**Step 2 — The Service Layer (2 marks).**
A **Service** is a logical collection of related data items. Each service is identified by a **128-bit UUID** (or a reserved 16-bit UUID for standard services). A temperature/humidity sensor typically exposes:
- *Environmental Sensing Service* (UUID **0x181A**)

**Step 3 — The Characteristic Layer (2 marks).**
A **Characteristic** is the leaf-level data container. Each characteristic contains:
- A **Properties** byte (Read, Write, Notify, Indicate)
- A **Value** (the actual sensor reading)
- Optional **Descriptors**

For our sensor:
- *Temperature Characteristic* (UUID **0x2A6E**) — properties: Read + Notify
- *Humidity Characteristic* (UUID **0x2A6F**) — properties: Read + Notify

**Step 4 — The Descriptor Layer (1 mark).**
**Descriptors** provide metadata about a characteristic. The most important is the **CCCD (Client Characteristic Configuration Descriptor, UUID 0x2902)** which the central writes to *subscribe* to notifications.

**Step 5 — Neat Diagram (1 mark).**
Draw the hierarchical tree: *Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor → Value*. Use the GATT hierarchy diagram from Section 4.3 of these notes as a model.

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Battery Lifetime (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the given data (1 mark).**
$T_{period} = 2$ s, $t_{TX} = 4$ ms = 0.004 s, $I_{TX} = 12$ mA, $I_{sleep} = 2\,\mu A$, $C = 240$ mAh.

**Step 2 — Calculate the sleep time (1 mark).**

$$
t_{sleep} = T_{period} - t_{TX} = 2.000 - 0.004 = 1.996 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the average current formula (2 marks).**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{I_{TX} \cdot t_{TX} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{T_{period}}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{(12 \times 10^{-3}) \times (4 \times 10^{-3}) + (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.996}{2}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{48 \times 10^{-6} + 3.992 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{51.992 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{avg} \approx 26.0 \text{ µA}}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the battery lifetime in hours (1 mark).**

$$
T_{life} = \dfrac{C}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{240 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ah}}{26.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}} \approx 9230.77 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to months (2 marks).**

$$
T_{life} = \dfrac{9230.77}{24 \times 30.44} = \dfrac{9230.77}{730.56}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{life} \approx 12.64 \text{ months} \approx 1 \text{ year and 18 days}}
$$

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Bluetooth Classic** and **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** protocol stacks in terms of architecture, power consumption, data rate, connection time, and suitability for industrial sensor applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Bluetooth Mesh Networking** in an IIoT context. With a suitable diagram, describe the four fundamental node features (*Relay, Proxy, Friend, Low-Power*) defined by the mesh specification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Classic vs BLE (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Architecture (2 marks).**
- *Bluetooth Classic* uses a **Piconet/Scatternet** architecture with a strict **Master-Slave** relationship; max 7 active slaves per piconet. It is optimized for **continuous, streaming data** (e.g., audio).
- *BLE* uses a **Star** topology with *Broadcaster/Observer* and *Peripheral/Central* roles. It is optimized for **short, bursty data** (e.g., sensor packets).

**Step 2 — Power Consumption (2 marks).**
- Classic Bluetooth: Typical average current **30–100 mA**; standby current ~1 mA.
- BLE: Average current **1–15 µA** (duty-cycled); sleep current as low as **0.3 µA**. **BLE is 10–100× more power-efficient**, making it the only viable choice for coin-cell-powered industrial sensors.

**Step 3 — Data Rate and Latency (1 mark).**
- Classic: 1–3 Mbps (DH packets), connection setup ~100 ms.
- BLE 1M PHY: 1 Mbps; BLE 2M PHY: 2 Mbps; connection setup as fast as **3 ms**; Coded PHY trades speed for range (125 kbps at 4× range).

**Step 4 — Suitability for IIoT Sensors (2 marks).**
- Classic Bluetooth is **not suitable** for long-life, low-duty-cycle sensors due to its power profile.
- BLE is **purpose-built** for IIoT sensors — it dominates the industrial wireless sensor market (TI CC26xx, Nordic nRF52/53, Silicon Labs EFR32).

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Bluetooth Mesh (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks).**
**Bluetooth Mesh** is a **many-to-many** flooding-based networking protocol standardized in **2017** as part of the Bluetooth v5.0 specification. In an IIoT plant, it allows **hundreds or thousands of sensors** (temperature, pressure, gas, vibration) to relay messages to a central sink (gateway) without requiring each sensor to be within direct radio range. Messages propagate via **managed flooding** across up to **126 hops**.

**Step 2 — Node Features (4 marks).**
The mesh specification defines **four optional features** that a node can implement:
1. **Relay Node:** Re-broadcasts received messages to extend network coverage. Requires the radio to be always on.
2. **Proxy Node:** Acts as a bridge between the BLE mesh network and a smartphone/tablet using standard BLE GATT.
3. **Friend Node:** Stores messages on behalf of **Low-Power Nodes (LPNs)** that spend most of their time sleeping.
4. **Low-Power Node (LPN):** A battery-powered sensor that sleeps aggressively and only periodically polls its *Friend Node* for accumulated messages.

**Step 3 — Diagram (1 mark).**
Draw the Bluetooth Mesh architecture from Section 4.5 of these notes (Provisioner → Sensor Nodes → Relay → Gateway Sink).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mAh to Ah or ms to s** in the battery-life derivation → loses 1 full mark. Always show unit conversions.
> 2. **Confusing 1M PHY with 2M PHY** in throughput problems → BLE 1M = 1 Mbps, BLE 2M = 2 Mbps.
> 3. **Writing the wrong number of advertising channels** — the correct answer is **3** (channels 37, 38, 39), not 40.
> 4. **Forgetting the LDN rule** in the GATT hierarchy: *Service → Characteristic → Descriptor → Value*. Skipping the descriptor layer loses 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Confusing "Bluetooth Classic Piconet" with "BLE Piconet"** — BLE does **not** use piconets in the classical sense; it uses a star with peripheral/central roles.
> 6. **Forgetting the antenna gain and path-loss units** (dBi and dB respectively) in link-budget problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Wireless Bluetooth Sensors:**

- BLE operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** with **40 channels**, each **2 MHz** wide.
- There are **3 advertising channels** (indices 37, 38, 39 at 2402, 2426, 2480 MHz) and **37 data channels** (indices 0–36).
- **Modulation:** GFSK with modulation index **0.5**; symbol rate **1 Msymbol/s** (1M PHY).
- **Max payload:** **27 bytes** in BLE v4.x; **244 bytes** in BLE v4.2+ with Data Length Extension.
- The **battery life equation** $T_{life} = C_{battery}/I_{avg}$ is the most heavily tested formula.
- The **average current** of a duty-cycled BLE sensor is **dominated by the sleep current**, not the TX current.
- The **GATT data model** is a hierarchy: *Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor → Value*.
- The **UUID** 16-bit standardized services (e.g., 0x180A, 0x181A) must be quoted correctly in the exam.
- The **GAP roles** in BLE are *Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central* — distinct from Classic Bluetooth's Master/Slave.
- **Bluetooth Mesh** is a *flooding-based* many-to-many network introduced in v5.0 with four node features: *Relay, Proxy, Friend, Low-Power*.
- **PAwR (Periodic Advertising with Responses)** in v5.4 enables massive sensor deployments with bi-directional slot-based communication.
- **AES-128 CCM** is the encryption standard; **LE Secure Connections** uses Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) for pairing.
- The **throughput efficiency** of a connected BLE link is only about **4 % of the raw bit rate** because of the heavily duty-cycled connection intervals.
- The **maximum transmit power** of a BLE radio is **+20 dBm (100 mW)**, but industrial sensors typically use **+4 to +8 dBm** to balance range and battery life.
- **Connection interval** ranges from **7.5 ms to 4 s**; **slave latency** allows the peripheral to skip connection events to save power.
- **Coexistence with Wi-Fi** is achieved by the advertising channels being deliberately placed at the **band edges** of 2.4 GHz ISM.
- The **IEEE standard** for Bluetooth is **802.15.1** (legacy); modern BLE is governed by the **Bluetooth SIG** specifications, not IEEE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Level Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Level Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Level Sensor (Industrial IoT Definition):** A *Level Sensor* is a field-grade transducer that converts the instantaneous level (height, volume, or mass) of a process medium — liquid, slurry, granular solid, or powder — held inside a vessel, tank, silo, or open channel into a standardized electrical signal (analog 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, HART, IO-Link, or digital wireless) suitable for acquisition by an Industrial IoT (IIoT) edge node, gateway, or PLC.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** PERUT635 syllabus, level sensors are classified as **primary process measurement devices** in the perception layer (Layer 1) of the **IIoT reference architecture (ISA-95 / RAMI 4.0)**. They form the boundary between the *physical process* and the *cyber/edge layer*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bathtub Water Gauge" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy (Bathtub & Dipstick):** Imagine you have a bathtub filled with water. To know how full it is, you could (a) dip a marked stick in it (float), (b) tap the wall and listen to the echo (ultrasonic), (c) weigh the tub (load cell), or (d) blow a small hole at the bottom and measure the water squirt pressure (hydrostatic). Each method gives you the same answer — *how full the tub is* — but the best method depends on the tub's material, the water's purity, and how often you need to check. A **Level Sensor is the engineered, industrial-grade "dipstick + brain"** that automatically and continuously reports the answer to a control room, phone, or cloud dashboard.

## 1.3 Classification of Level Sensors (Per KTU Module 2)

```
Level Sensors
├── Contact Type
│   ├── Float-based
│   ├── Capacitive (contact probe)
│   ├── Conductive / Resistive
│   ├── Vibrating Fork / Tuning Fork
│   ├── Magnetostrictive
│   └── Hydrostatic (submersible)
└── Non-Contact Type
    ├── Ultrasonic
    ├── Radar (Pulsed / FMCW)
    ├── Laser / LiDAR
    ├── Optical (refraction-based)
    └── Weight / Load Cell
```

## 1.4 Key Engineering Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard performance indicators a KTU examiner will expect you to know:
> - **Range (Span):** Typically **0.1 m to 50 m** for industrial tanks.
> - **Accuracy:** From **±0.05 %** (radar, magnetostrictive) to **±1 %** (basic float).
> - **Resolution:** Smallest detectable level change — often **1 mm to 10 mm**.
> - **Repeatability:** How consistently the sensor reports the same level — usually **±0.1 % of span**.
> - **Output:** **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V**, **HART**, **IO-Link**, **Modbus RTU/TCP**, **WirelessHART**, **LoRaWAN**, **NB-IoT**.
> - **IP Rating:** **IP65 / IP67 / IP68** for harsh process environments.
> - **Process Temperature:** **−40 °C to +400 °C** depending on construction.
> - **Process Pressure:** **Vacuum to 100 bar**.

## 1.5 IIoT Layer Mapping of a Level Sensor

In an IIoT deployment, the level sensor is **never standalone**; it is part of a layered stack:

| Layer | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception (L0)** | Level Sensor + Signal Conditioner | Physical measurement |
| **Edge (L1)** | RTU / Smart Transmitter / Microcontroller | Local processing, filtering |
| **Network (L2)** | Gateway (LoRa/NB-IoT/Wi-Fi/Ethernet) | Wireless data transport |
| **Platform (L3)** | MQTT Broker / Cloud Ingestion | Time-series storage |
| **Application (L4)** | SCADA / Digital Twin / Mobile App | Visualization & analytics |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Each level-sensing family operates on a **distinct physics principle**. The KTU paper expects you to map *medium property → physical principle → electrical output*. Master the table below.

### 2.1.1 Capacitive Level Sensor

**Principle:** A two-electrode (or coaxial cylinder) capacitor is formed around or inside the tank. The capacitance changes as the dielectric (air ↔ liquid ↔ solid) between the electrodes changes.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. The probe acts as one plate; the tank wall (or reference rod) acts as the other.
2. Above the liquid line, dielectric is air (εᵣ ≈ 1).
3. Below the liquid line, dielectric is the process medium (εᵣ = 2 to 80 for water-based liquids).
4. Total capacitance is the **parallel sum** of the two segments.
5. A bridge / oscillator / charge-time circuit converts C into a voltage or frequency.
6. Microcontroller computes the filled height as a fraction of total probe length.

### 2.1.2 Ultrasonic Level Sensor

**Principle:** A piezoelectric transducer emits a **40–200 kHz** burst. The burst reflects off the medium surface and returns. Distance is derived from time-of-flight (ToF).

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Tx fires an acoustic pulse.
2. Pulse travels through air at **velocity cₛ ≈ 343 m/s** (at 20 °C).
3. Pulse reflects from the liquid/solid surface.
4. Rx detects the echo; a timer captures the round-trip time **t**.
5. Distance **d = (cₛ × t) / 2** is calculated.
6. Actual level = **Tank height − measured distance − blanking zone offset**.

### 2.1.3 Radar (Microwave) Level Sensor

**Principle:** Same ToF concept, but uses **EM waves (5.8 GHz, 10 GHz, 26 GHz, 80 GHz)**. EMC propagation is **independent of air density, temperature, dust, and foam**.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave) or pulsed radar emits a microwave burst.
2. Frequency shift of the reflected echo is proportional to the round-trip delay.
3. **Beat frequency f_b = (ΔF / T_sweep) × τ**, where τ is the round-trip delay.
4. Distance d = c × τ / 2.
5. 80 GHz (W-band) radar offers **narrow beam (< 8°)** — ideal for narrow tanks.

### 2.1.4 Hydrostatic Pressure Level Sensor

**Principle:** The pressure at the bottom of a fluid column is given by Pascal's law. Submersible probe measures gauge pressure; the head is computed from the hydrostatic equation.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Probe is lowered to the bottom of the tank.
2. Diaphragm senses the **hydrostatic head pressure** of the liquid above.
3. Output: P = ρ × g × h.
4. Knowing ρ (calibration input), the controller computes h.
5. Requires **density compensation** for temperature variation.

### 2.1.5 Vibrating Fork / Tuning Fork Level Sensor

**Principle:** A piezo-electrically excited fork vibrates at its **natural frequency (~ 1–1.3 kHz)**. When immersed in a medium, the **added mass and viscous damping** shift the frequency or amplitude.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Free-air vibration: f₀.
2. Fork is immersed → frequency drops by **5–15 Hz** (or amplitude drops).
3. Threshold detection → switching output.
4. Used as **point-level** (high/low alarm), not continuous.

### 2.1.6 Magnetostrictive Level Sensor

**Principle:** A wire in the probe carries a current pulse. At the position of a **floating magnet** (on the surface), a torsional strain wave is generated. The time between current pulse and detected strain wave gives the float position.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Current pulse launched in magnetostrictive wire.
2. Float's magnetic field at position **L** triggers Wiedemann effect — torsional wave.
3. Wave reaches pickup coil at top in time **t**.
4. **L = v_strain × t**, with v_strain ≈ **2,800 m/s**.
5. **Resolution < 0.1 mm** — the most accurate continuous technology.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated cheat-sheet you must memorize for the ESE. Replace any vertical pipe with `\vert` or `\mid` to keep tables intact.

| Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Symbol Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitive (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | ε₀ permittivity of free space, εᵣ relative permittivity, A area, d gap | F (farads) |
| Capacitive (cylindrical, partial fill) | $C_{total} = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} \left[\varepsilon_{air} L_{air} + \varepsilon_{liq} L_{liq}\right]$ | a, b radii; L segment lengths | F |
| Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | ρ density, g gravity, h head height | Pa |
| Hydrostatic level | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, g}$ | same as above | m |
| Ultrasonic ToF | $d = \dfrac{c_s \, t}{2}$ | cₛ speed of sound, t round-trip time | m |
| Radar ToF (FMCW beat) | $f_b = \dfrac{2 \Delta F \, d}{c \, T_{sw}}$ | ΔF sweep bandwidth, c EM velocity, T_sw sweep time | Hz |
| Radar ToF (pulsed) | $d = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$ | c = 3×10⁸ m/s, t round-trip time | m |
| Vibrating fork shift | $\Delta f \approx \dfrac{f_0}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{fork}}$ | f₀ natural freq, Δm added mass, m_fork fork mass | Hz |
| Magnetostrictive position | $L = v_{strain} \cdot t$ | v_strain ≈ 2,800 m/s, t propagation time | m |
| Speed of sound (air) | $c_s = 331.4 \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T}{273.15}}$ | T temperature in °C | m/s |
| EM wave velocity | $c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ | μ₀, ε₀ vacuum constants | m/s |

## 2.3 Engineering Application Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do KTU examiners expect this knowledge?** Because level sensors underpin critical industrial verticals:
> - **Oil & Gas** — Tank farm inventory management (radar + wireless gateway → ERP).
> - **Water Treatment** — Reservoir level + pump control via SCADA + Modbus/TCP.
> - **Food & Pharma** — CIP (Clean-In-Place) level verification (capacitive + IO-Link).
> - **Chemicals** — Corrosive acid level in FRP tanks (guided-wave radar).
> - **Cement & Mining** — Solid silo level (vibrating fork + laser triangulation).
> - **Smart Agriculture** — Silo / grain-bin level via NB-IoT (autonomy > 10 years on battery).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Hydrostatic Level from Submersible Pressure Probe

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** A submersible pressure transmitter is mounted 0.5 m above the bottom of a vertical tank. It reads **P = 24.525 kPa** when a liquid of density **1000 kg/m³** is filled to a certain height. Compute the **filled height h** of the liquid above the tank bottom.

### Step 1 — Write Pascal's hydrostatic law

The pressure at depth (h − 0.5) below the liquid surface is given by:

$$
P = \rho \, g \, h_{above\_probe}
$$

where $h_{above\_probe}$ is the liquid column height above the probe diaphragm.

### Step 2 — Substitute the sensor height offset

Total height from tank bottom to liquid surface:

$$
H_{total} = h_{above\_probe} + h_{probe}
$$

Therefore:

$$
h_{above\_probe} = H_{total} - h_{probe}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the known values

Given:
- $P = 24{,}525 \, \text{Pa}$
- $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- $h_{probe} = 0.5 \, \text{m}$

Insert into the pressure equation:

$$
24{,}525 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times h_{above\_probe}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $h_{above\_probe}$

$$
h_{above\_probe} = \dfrac{24{,}525}{1000 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
h_{above\_probe} = \dfrac{24{,}525}{9{,}810}
$$

$$
h_{above\_probe} = 2.5 \, \text{m}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the total fill height

$$
H_{total} = h_{above\_probe} + h_{probe} = 2.5 + 0.5 = 3.0 \, \text{m}
$$

**Result:** The tank is filled to a height of **3.0 m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** If the liquid density changes with temperature, ρ is not constant. For a ±10 % density variation (e.g., diesel vs. crude oil), the error is **directly 10 % of level**. Always compensate using a **density RTD or densitometer input** in the IIoT edge node.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight Distance

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** An ultrasonic level sensor fires a 75 kHz pulse. The round-trip echo time recorded is **t = 12 ms**. Ambient temperature is **35 °C**. Compute the measured distance to the surface and the resulting **tank level** if the tank is **6 m tall** and the sensor is mounted at the **top center**.

### Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound at T = 35 °C

$$
c_s = 331.4 \times \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{T}{273.15}}
$$

$$
c_s = 331.4 \times \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{35}{273.15}}
$$

$$
c_s = 331.4 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.1281}
$$

$$
c_s = 331.4 \times \sqrt{1.1281}
$$

$$
c_s = 331.4 \times 1.0621
$$

$$
c_s \approx 351.97 \, \text{m/s}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the ToF distance equation

$$
d = \dfrac{c_s \times t}{2}
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{351.97 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{4.2236}{2}
$$

$$
d \approx 2.1118 \, \text{m}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the tank level

The sensor is mounted at the top, so the **distance from sensor to surface = empty head space**:

$$
h_{empty} = d = 2.1118 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
h_{liquid} = H_{tank} - h_{empty}
$$

$$
h_{liquid} = 6.0 - 2.1118 = 3.8882 \, \text{m}
$$

**Result:** Liquid level ≈ **3.89 m** in a 6 m tank (≈ 64.8 % fill).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students forget to divide by **2** in the ToF formula. The 12 ms is the *round-trip* time — the wave must travel down AND back. **Always divide by 2.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Capacitive Partial-Fill Level

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** A vertical cylindrical capacitive probe has inner radius **a = 5 mm** and outer radius **b = 25 mm**. Total length **L = 4 m**. The tank contains oil of **εᵣ,oil = 2.4** and the empty portion contains air with **εᵣ,air = 1.0**. The sensor reads **C_total = 180 pF**. Find the fill height.

### Step 1 — Cylindrical capacitance per unit length (air segment)

$$
C'_{air} = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,air}}{\ln(b/a)}
$$

$$
\ln(b/a) = \ln(25/5) = \ln(5) = 1.6094
$$

$$
C'_{air} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0}{1.6094}
$$

$$
C'_{air} = \dfrac{5.562 \times 10^{-11}}{1.6094}
$$

$$
C'_{air} = 3.456 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{F/m} = 34.56 \, \text{pF/m}
$$

### Step 2 — Cylindrical capacitance per unit length (oil segment)

$$
C'_{oil} = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,oil}}{\ln(b/a)} = C'_{air} \times \varepsilon_{r,oil}
$$

$$
C'_{oil} = 34.56 \times 2.4 = 82.94 \, \text{pF/m}
$$

### Step 3 — Total capacitance expression

$$
C_{total} = C'_{air} \cdot L_{air} + C'_{oil} \cdot L_{oil}
$$

Let $L_{oil} = x$ (the filled length we want to find) and $L_{air} = L - x = 4 - x$:

$$
180 = 34.56 \cdot (4 - x) + 82.94 \cdot x
$$

### Step 4 — Expand and simplify

$$
180 = 138.24 - 34.56 x + 82.94 x
$$

$$
180 = 138.24 + 48.38 x
$$

$$
48.38 x = 180 - 138.24
$$

$$
48.38 x = 41.76
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{41.76}{48.38} = 0.863 \, \text{m}
$$

**Result:** Fill height ≈ **0.863 m** (21.6 % of the 4 m probe length).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Sensor Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table to answer "Which sensor should I select for X?" type KTU questions.**

| Selection Criterion | Best Technology | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Clean liquid, low cost, simple | Float | No electronics in tank |
| Corrosive / acidic liquid, no contact preferred | Radar (FMCW) | EM wave immune to vapor, foam |
| Foaming, dusty, harsh environment | Guided-Wave Radar (GWR) | TDR along probe — ignores foam |
| Solid powder / granular, high temp | Vibrating Fork + Laser triangulation | Handles dust + heat |
| Sub-cm accuracy, custody transfer | Magnetostrictive | ±0.1 mm, hygienic |
| Open channel flow / river level | Ultrasonic | Non-contact, long range, low power |
| High-pressure, sealed tank | Hydrostatic submersible | Withstands high pressure |
| Hygienic / CIP, food & pharma | Capacitive (IO-Link) | Sanitary, no moving parts |
| Interface detection (oil + water) | Capacitive (multi-electrode) | Distinguishes εᵣ of two liquids |
| Battery-powered remote site | Radar / Ultrasonic (NB-IoT) | 10+ year battery life |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between contact and non-contact type level sensors. Give two examples for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Contact Type | Non-Contact Type |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Probe physically touches the medium | Sensor measures without touching the medium |
| Examples | Float sensor, Capacitive probe, Vibrating fork, Hydrostatic submersible, Magnetostrictive | Ultrasonic, Radar (FMCW), Laser triangulation, Optical refraction |
| Pros | Higher accuracy, lower cost, simple | No contamination, no wear, suitable for corrosive/hygienic media |
| Cons | Wears out, contaminates hygienic media, fouling | Higher cost, requires clear line-of-sight, sensitive to foam for some types |

*(Award 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for examples + 1 mark for pros/cons comparison.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the working principle of a guided-wave radar (GWR) level sensor. Why is it preferred over conventional ultrasonic in foaming liquids?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** A GWR uses a low-energy electromagnetic pulse (typically 100 MHz to 1.5 GHz) that propagates along a **metallic probe** (coaxial, rod, or wire) immersed in the medium. The pulse travels down the probe at the speed of light. At the **liquid surface**, a portion of the pulse is reflected back. The round-trip time is measured using **Time-Domain Reflectometry (TDR)**. Distance is calculated as $d = c \cdot t / 2$, and the level is derived from the known tank geometry.

2. **Why preferred over ultrasonic in foaming liquids (1 mark):** Ultrasonic waves are **highly attenuated by foam** because the air-liquid interfaces scatter acoustic energy. GWR uses **EM waves travelling along a solid metallic guide**, which **bypasses the foam layer** and reflects only from the actual bulk liquid surface. Hence GWR provides reliable readings even in heavy foam, chemical surfactants, and vapor environments.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Module pattern: each Module typically offers **2 full 14-mark questions** with **internal choice** (you answer ONE of the two given). Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The complete model solution is provided below for both alternatives.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a capacitive level sensor suitable for IIoT deployment. Derive the relationship between fill height and total capacitance for a cylindrical probe geometry.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram and operation (4 marks):**

A capacitive level sensor for IIoT deployment consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Probe (sensing element):** Two concentric cylinders — outer is the tank wall (or a reference tube), inner is the active electrode of length L. The dielectric between them changes with fill height.
2. **Excitation source:** A stable AC sine generator (typically 50 kHz to 1 MHz, low amplitude) drives the probe through a current-limiting resistor.
3. **Charge amplifier / front-end:** Converts the small capacitance variation into a proportional AC voltage.
4. **Precision rectifier + LPF:** Converts AC to a clean DC signal proportional to the capacitance.
5. **16-bit or 24-bit ADC:** Digitises the DC voltage.
6. **Edge MCU (e.g., STM32 / ESP32):** Computes the fill height using the calibration equation and applies temperature compensation for εᵣ drift.
7. **IIoT output stage:** Pushes the level data via **IO-Link / Modbus TCP / MQTT-SN** to a gateway, and converts to a **4–20 mA** loop as a backup channel.

**Derivation for cylindrical geometry (3 marks):**

Consider an inner rod of radius **a** and outer cylinder of radius **b**, both of total length **L**. Let the fill height be **x**, with the medium of relative permittivity **εᵣ,m** and air above of **εᵣ,air = 1**.

The capacitance per unit length in any region is:

$$
C' = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}
$$

The total capacitance is the sum of the two segments in parallel:

$$
C_{total} = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,air}}{\ln(b/a)} (L - x) + \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,m}}{\ln(b/a)} x
$$

$$
C_{total} = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} \left[ (L - x) + \varepsilon_{r,m} x \right]
$$

Let $k = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)}$. Then:

$$
C_{total} = k L + k (\varepsilon_{r,m} - 1) x
$$

Solving for **x**:

$$
x = \dfrac{C_{total} - k L}{k (\varepsilon_{r,m} - 1)}
$$

This is the **calibration equation** stored in the IIoT edge firmware, which converts the measured capacitance to a fill height in real time.

---

**(b) A cylindrical capacitive probe has inner radius 8 mm and outer radius 32 mm, total length 3 m. The medium is oil with εᵣ = 2.5. The sensor measures C_total = 165 pF. Compute the fill height.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric constant**
$$
\ln(b/a) = \ln(32/8) = \ln(4) = 1.3863
$$

**Step 2 — Per-unit-length capacitance in air**
$$
k_{air} = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \times 1}{\ln(b/a)} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1.3863}
$$

$$
k_{air} = \dfrac{5.562 \times 10^{-11}}{1.3863} = 4.012 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{F/m} = 40.12 \, \text{pF/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-unit-length capacitance in oil**
$$
k_{oil} = 40.12 \times 2.5 = 100.3 \, \text{pF/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Total capacitance equation**
Let x = fill height in metres.

$$
C_{total} = k_{air}(3 - x) + k_{oil} \cdot x
$$

$$
165 = 40.12(3 - x) + 100.3 x
$$

$$
165 = 120.36 - 40.12 x + 100.3 x
$$

$$
165 = 120.36 + 60.18 x
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for x**
$$
60.18 x = 165 - 120.36 = 44.64
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{44.64}{60.18} = 0.7417 \, \text{m}
$$

**Final Answer:** The fill height is **x ≈ 0.742 m** (≈ 24.7 % of the 3 m probe length).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the working of an FMCW radar level sensor with a block diagram. Derive the relationship between the beat frequency and the measured distance.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram and operation (4 marks):**

An FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave) radar level sensor consists of the following blocks:

1. **VCO (Voltage-Controlled Oscillator):** Generates a linear frequency sweep (e.g., 5.72–5.88 GHz for a 26 GHz radar with 160 MHz sweep).
2. **Power divider / coupler:** Splits the VCO output into two paths — one to the antenna, one to the mixer reference.
3. **Antenna (horn or lens):** Radiates the FMCW signal towards the medium surface. 80 GHz radars use a small lens antenna producing a beam < 8°.
4. **Target (medium surface):** Reflects the signal.
5. **Receive antenna / mixer RF input:** Captures the reflected echo.
6. **Mixer:** Multiplies the received echo with the instantaneous transmitted signal. The output is the **beat frequency** f_b.
7. **IF amplifier + ADC:** Amplifies and digitises the beat signal.
8. **FFT processor / edge MCU:** Performs an FFT on the beat signal to find f_b and hence distance.
9. **Output interface:** Industrial 4–20 mA, HART, IO-Link, or wireless IIoT protocol.

**Derivation of beat-frequency vs. distance (3 marks):**

Let the VCO sweep linearly from $f_1$ to $f_2$ over a sweep period $T_{sw}$, so the sweep bandwidth is:

$$
\Delta F = f_2 - f_1
$$

The rate of frequency change is:

$$
\dfrac{df}{dt} = \dfrac{\Delta F}{T_{sw}}
$$

If the target is at distance **d**, the round-trip delay is:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{2 d}{c}
$$

During this delay, the transmitted signal has shifted in frequency by:

$$
\Delta f = \tau \cdot \dfrac{\Delta F}{T_{sw}} = \dfrac{2 d \cdot \Delta F}{c \cdot T_{sw}}
$$

The mixer multiplies transmitted and received signals, and the resulting IF (beat) frequency equals this frequency shift:

$$
f_b = \dfrac{2 \Delta F \, d}{c \, T_{sw}}
$$

Solving for distance:

$$
d = \dfrac{f_b \, c \, T_{sw}}{2 \Delta F}
$$

This is the **fundamental equation of FMCW radar ranging**, and the MCU uses it to map every measured f_b to a precise distance, irrespective of air properties, foam, dust, or temperature.

---

**(b) An FMCW radar operates with a sweep bandwidth of 1 GHz, sweep period of 10 ms, and centre frequency of 80 GHz. The mixer output shows a beat frequency of 6.67 kHz. Calculate the distance to the target and the percentage fill of a 10 m tank (sensor mounted at top).** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\Delta F = 1 \text{ GHz} = 1 \times 10^9 \, \text{Hz}$
- $T_{sw} = 10 \, \text{ms} = 0.01 \, \text{s}$
- $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$
- $f_b = 6.67 \, \text{kHz} = 6.67 \times 10^3 \, \text{Hz}$

**Step 1 — Apply the FMCW distance equation**

$$
d = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T_{sw}}{2 \Delta F}
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{6.67 \times 10^3 \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.01}{2 \times 1 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{6.67 \times 10^3 \times 3 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{2.001 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
d = \dfrac{2.001 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^9} = 10.005 \, \text{m}
$$

**Wait — re-check arithmetic:** $6.67 \times 10^3 \times 3 \times 10^8 = 2.001 \times 10^{12}$. Then $\times 0.01 = 2.001 \times 10^{10}$. Divide by $2 \times 10^9$ gives:

$$
d = 10.005 \, \text{m} \quad \text{→ ROUNDED} \approx 10.0 \, \text{m}
$$

This implies the radar is reading the bottom of the tank — i.e., the tank is **empty** (or the sensor is at the very bottom of the empty head space).

**Step 2 — Determine the percentage fill**

If the sensor is mounted at the **top** of a 10 m tank and the measured distance to the liquid surface is $d = 10 \, \text{m}$, then the **empty head space = 10 m** and the **liquid level = 0 m**.

$$
\% \text{ Fill} = \dfrac{H_{tank} - d}{H_{tank}} \times 100 = \dfrac{10 - 10}{10} \times 100 = 0 \,\%
$$

**Alternative interpretation:** If we assume the beat frequency corresponds to a half-full scenario (problem-typo), let us state the general formula:

$$
\% \text{ Fill} = \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{H_{tank}}\right) \times 100
$$

**Final Answer:** With the given beat frequency, distance ≈ 10.0 m, so the tank is **0 % filled** (empty). If a more realistic smaller beat frequency such as 3.33 kHz were observed, $d = 5.0 \, \text{m}$, fill = 50 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF** (both ultrasonic and pulsed radar) — losing 1–2 marks.
> - **Mixing up hydrostatic formulas** (writing $P = \rho g$ instead of $P = \rho g h$) — losing 1 mark.
> - **In cylindrical capacitance, omitting the `ln(b/a)` term** — losing 2 marks.
> - **Confusing FMCW sweep bandwidth with carrier frequency** — they are different. $\Delta F$ is small (MHz to GHz), while $f_c$ is the carrier (5.8 / 10 / 26 / 80 GHz).
> - **Forgetting to convert units** (kHz to Hz, ms to s, mm to m) — losing 1 mark.
> - **Failing to mention IIoT integration** in descriptive answers — KTU 2024 scheme emphasises *IIoT context*; a generic answer without the network/cloud/digital-twin touch gets 1–2 marks deducted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**
> - A **level sensor** is the *Layer 0 (perception)* transducer in the IIoT stack.
> - Two broad families: **Contact** (float, capacitive, conductive, vibrating fork, hydrostatic, magnetostrictive) and **Non-Contact** (ultrasonic, radar FMCW, laser, optical).
> - **Capacitive sensors** rely on the change of dielectric constant — equation: $C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ for a cylindrical geometry.
> - **Hydrostatic sensors** use Pascal's law: $P = \rho g h$ → $h = P / (\rho g)$.
> - **Ultrasonic sensors** use acoustic ToF: $d = c_s t / 2$; speed of sound depends on temperature: $c_s = 331.4 \sqrt{1 + T/273.15}$.
> - **Radar (FMCW)** uses beat frequency: $d = \dfrac{f_b \, c \, T_{sw}}{2 \Delta F}$.
> - **Vibrating fork** is a *point-level* (high/low) sensor, not continuous — uses frequency shift on immersion.
> - **Magnetostrictive** offers the best accuracy (±0.1 mm) — uses $L = v_{strain} \cdot t$, with $v_{strain} \approx 2800$ m/s.
> - **Radar (especially GWR and 80 GHz)** is preferred for **foaming, dusty, vapour-rich, high-temperature, or corrosive** applications.
> - **Ultrasonic** is the most common *low-cost* solution for open-channel flow and water reservoirs.
> - **Hydrostatic submersibles** are best for **high-pressure sealed tanks** and sludge / slurry.
> - Always include the **factor of 2** in any ToF equation.
> - Always convert **units consistently** (mm, cm, m; ms, s; kHz, Hz).
> - **IIoT integration adds value**: edge filtering, predictive maintenance, digital twin, ERP/MES connectivity.
> - KTU 2024 Scheme demands you frame sensor discussions in the **IIoT 5-layer architecture** (Perception → Edge → Network → Platform → Application).
> - The **80 GHz W-band radar** is the modern gold standard — narrow beam, immune to foam, hygienic, fits in small nozzles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A hydraulic system is a power transmission mechanism that uses a pressurized incompressible fluid (typically hydraulic oil) as the working medium to transmit energy from one location to another, converting hydraulic energy into mechanical energy (linear or rotary motion) to perform useful work. In the context of **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**, hydraulic systems are integrated with smart sensors, actuators, and networked controllers to enable real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance, and closed-loop automated control.

### 1.2 Key Governing Principle — Pascal's Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Pascal's Law (KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight):** Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid and acts perpendicular to the walls of the containing vessel.

$$\boxed{P = \frac{F}{A}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Pressure (in **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²**)
- $F$ = Applied Force (in **Newton (N)**)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional Area (in **m²**)

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a water-filled balloon connected to a syringe on one end and a larger syringe on the other. When you push the small syringe plunger down with a small force, the water transmits that pressure equally, and because the larger syringe has a bigger area, it pushes UP with a **multiplied force**. This is exactly how a hydraulic jack lifts a 2-ton car using just one hand!

In IIoT terms, think of this balloon as a **networked hydraulic cylinder** — pressure sensors at the small end and a force/position sensor at the large end send live data to a cloud dashboard (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub) so the operator can see the lifting force in real-time from anywhere in the world.

### 1.4 Classification of Hydraulic Systems in IIoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Classification:**
> - **Open-Center (Open Loop):** Fluid returns to tank when control valve is neutral; used in mobile hydraulics (JCBs, tractors).
> - **Closed-Center (Closed Loop):** Fluid circulates continuously under pressure; used in precision industrial IIoT applications (CNC machines, robotic arms).
> - **Hydrostatic Transmission:** Direct fluid coupling without intermediate mechanical linkage.

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Industrial Range |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Working Pressure | $P$ | **bar** (1 bar = **10⁵ Pa**) | 100 – 350 bar |
| Flow Rate | $Q$ | **L/min (lpm)** | 20 – 200 lpm |
| Fluid Viscosity | $\mu$ | **cSt (centistokes)** | 15 – 68 cSt |
| Bulk Modulus | $\beta$ | **bar** (≈ 16,000 bar for mineral oil) | 13,000 – 17,000 bar |
| Specific Gravity | $SG$ | dimensionless | 0.85 – 0.92 |
| Power | $W$ | **kW** | 1 – 250 kW |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block Sequence of a Hydraulic System

A typical IIoT-enabled hydraulic system operates through the following structured sequence:

1. **Prime Mover Activation** — An electric motor (now often VFD-controlled via IIoT) drives the hydraulic pump.
2. **Fluid Intake** — The pump draws hydraulic oil from the reservoir through a suction strainer (removes particulates ≥ **25 µm**).
3. **Pressurization** — Pump pressurizes the fluid; pressure relief valve opens at **set pressure (P_set)** to prevent overpressure damage.
4. **Directional Control** — A 4/3 or 4/2 solenoid-actuated DCV (Directional Control Valve) routes fluid to either the cap-end or rod-end of the cylinder.
5. **Energy Conversion** — Hydraulic energy → Mechanical energy (linear motion via cylinder, rotary via motor).
6. **Return Path** — Exhaust fluid returns to tank through a return-line filter (typically **10 µm** absolute).
7. **IIoT Sensing Layer** — Pressure transducers (0–400 bar), flow meters, temperature sensors (PT100), and LVDT position sensors continuously publish data via **MQTT / OPC-UA** to an edge gateway.
8. **Cloud Analytics** — Predictive maintenance algorithms (vibration FFT, oil-debris analysis) trigger alerts on anomalies.

### 2.2 The Core "WHY" Behind Each Step

- **Why pressurized fluid?** Because liquids are nearly **incompressible**, they transmit force instantly without the elastic loss seen in pneumatic (air) systems — giving hydraulics their characteristic **stiffness** and **high force density**.
- **Why a reservoir?** Hydraulic fluid heats up during operation (every **1 bar** of pressure drop across a valve ≈ **0.1 °C** rise); the reservoir acts as a heat exchanger and de-aeration chamber.
- **Why IIoT integration?** Unplanned hydraulic failures cause **30%** of all industrial downtime. Real-time pressure/flow/temperature monitoring converts reactive maintenance into **predictive maintenance**, reducing downtime by up to **70%**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of KTU 2024 Module 2 numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $P$ [Pa], $F$ [N], $A$ [m²] | Cylinder force calculation |
| 2 | Pascal's Law (Force Multiplication) | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | $F$ [N], $A$ [m²] | Hydraulic jack, press |
| 3 | Cylinder Output Force | $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta_m$ | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency (0.85–0.95) | Linear actuator sizing |
| 4 | Cylinder Bore Area | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2$ | $D$ = bore diameter [m] | Cylinder design |
| 5 | Annulus (Rod-side) Area | $A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2)$ | $d$ = rod diameter [m] | Extension vs retraction force |
| 6 | Flow Rate – Velocity Relation | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $Q$ [m³/s], $v$ [m/s] | Pump sizing, cylinder speed |
| 7 | Cylinder Piston Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | $v$ [m/s] | Speed calculation |
| 8 | Hydraulic Power | $W = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{600}$ | $W$ [kW], $P$ [bar], $Q$ [lpm] | Energy audit, motor sizing |
| 9 | Pump Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | dimensionless | Pump health monitoring (IIoT) |
| 10 | Bulk Modulus (Stiffness) | $\beta = \dfrac{-\Delta P}{\Delta V / V}$ | $\beta$ [Pa] | Predicting system stiffness |
| 11 | Reynolds Number (Oil in Pipe) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d_h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime (laminar if $Re < 2300$) |
| 12 | Pressure Drop (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi d^4}$ | $L$ = pipe length [m], $d$ = pipe dia [m] | Line loss calculation |

### 2.4 Real-World IIoT Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Bosch Rexroth CytroForce and Danfoss PLUS+1 integrate hydraulic systems with cloud dashboards.
- **Mobile Equipment:** John Deere and Caterpillar use IIoT-enabled hydraulics for **autonomous tractors and excavators**.
- **Energy Sector:** Offshore drilling platforms use networked hydraulic actuators monitored for leak detection.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control surface actuation uses **fly-by-optics** IIoT architectures with hydraulic backup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Numerical Reminder:** For KTU problems, always convert bar → Pa using **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**, and lpm → m³/s using **1 lpm = 1/600,000 m³/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydraulic Force Amplification (Pascal's Principle)

**Problem Setup:** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter $D_1 = 30 \text{ mm}$ and a large piston of diameter $D_2 = 150 \text{ mm}$. A force of $F_1 = 200 \text{ N}$ is applied on the small piston. Find the output force $F_2$ on the large piston.

**Step 1:** State Pascal's Law — pressure is transmitted equally.

$$P_1 = P_2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the pressure formula on both pistons.

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply to isolate $F_2$.

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 4:** Compute cross-sectional areas.

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (30 \times 10^{-3})^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$A_1 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (150 \times 10^{-3})^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 2.25 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$A_2 = 1.7671 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Compute the area ratio (mechanical advantage).

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{1.7671 \times 10^{-2}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 25$$

**Step 6:** Calculate output force.

$$F_2 = 200 \cdot 25 = 5000 \text{ N} = 5 \text{ kN}$$

**Interpretation:** A modest 200 N input force produces a **5 kN** output — a force amplification of **25×**. This is the essence of every hydraulic press, jack, and injection moulding machine.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydraulic Power Transmission

**Problem Setup:** A pump delivers oil at $P = 200 \text{ bar}$ and $Q = 60 \text{ lpm}$. Calculate the hydraulic power transmitted.

**Step 1:** Recall the standard hydraulic power formula.

$$W = \frac{P \cdot Q}{600}$$

where the 600 acts as a conversion factor (bar × lpm → kW).

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$W = \frac{200 \cdot 60}{600} = \frac{12{,}000}{600}$$

**Step 3:** Final result.

$$\boxed{W = 20 \text{ kW}}$$

**Alternative Verification using SI Units:**

Convert: $P = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$, and $Q = 60 \text{ lpm} = \dfrac{60}{60{,}000} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$W = P \cdot Q = 2 \times 10^7 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-3} = 20{,}000 \text{ W} = 20 \text{ kW}$$

✅ Both methods agree.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cylinder Piston Velocity & Stroke Time

**Problem Setup:** A hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 80 \text{ mm}$ and a rod diameter $d = 45 \text{ mm}$. The pump supplies $Q = 30 \text{ lpm}$. Calculate (a) extension velocity, (b) retraction velocity, and (c) time to complete a stroke of $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$.

**Step 1:** Compute full bore area (cap end).

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.08)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute annulus area (rod end).

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.08^2 - 0.045^2)$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.0064 - 0.002025) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.004375$$

$$A_r = 3.4361 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Convert flow rate.

$$Q = 30 \text{ lpm} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute extension velocity.

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_c} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.1 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5:** Compute retraction velocity.

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.4361 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1455 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6:** Compute stroke time (extension).

$$t_{ext} = \frac{L}{v_{ext}} = \frac{0.5}{0.0995} = 5.025 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7:** Compute stroke time (retraction).

$$t_{ret} = \frac{L}{v_{ret}} = \frac{0.5}{0.1455} = 3.436 \text{ s}$$

**Observation:** Retraction is **~45% faster** than extension because the effective piston area is smaller on the rod side. This asymmetry is a **KTU-favourite exam insight**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pressure Loss vs IIoT Sensor Placement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Diagnostic Table:** Pressure losses across hydraulic components are the #1 cause of inefficiency and overheating. The IIoT sensor placement strategy below is tested in KTU 2024 Module 2.

| Component | Typical Pressure Loss (bar) | IIoT Sensor Location | Diagnostic KPI |
|-----------|-----------------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Suction Strainer (clogged) | 0.2 – 0.5 | Inlet of pump | Vacuum pressure trend |
| Piping (per 10 m, 25 mm dia) | 0.1 – 0.4 | Mid-pipe | $\Delta P$ vs flow |
| 4/3 DCV (spool shifted) | 3 – 8 | Port P and T | Pilot pressure |
| Pressure Relief Valve | 5 – 15 | Pump discharge | Cracking pressure drift |
| Cylinder (load) | 50 – 200 | Cap-end + rod-end | Force = $P \cdot A$ |
| Return-line Filter | 0.5 – 2.0 | Return line | $\Delta P$ rise = clog |
| Heat Exchanger | 1 – 3 | Inlet & outlet oil | $\Delta T$ for cooling duty |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
