- ✅ **Five Layers:** Perception → Network → Edge → Cloud → Application.
- ✅ **Vital Signs Mnemonic:** **T-H-R-B-S-O** = Temperature, Heart rate, Respiration, Blood pressure, SpO₂, Other.
- ✅ **ECG Sampling Rate:** ≥ 360 Hz (Nyquist + margin); 12-bit ADC typical.
- ✅ **Heart Rate Formula:** $HR = (60 \cdot f_s) / N_{RR}$.
- ✅ **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$.
- ✅ **Quantization SNR:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB → ECG needs ≥ 12 bits.
- ✅ **MQTT vs HTTP:** MQTT = 2-byte header, pub/sub, low latency, ideal for vitals.
- ✅ **BLE** for wearables (low power); **Wi-Fi/5G** for high-bandwidth bedside; **LoRaWAN/NB-IoT** for outdoor/at-home RPM.
- ✅ **FHIR / HL7** for EHR interoperability.
- ✅ **Edge Computing** reduces alarm latency from ~1 s to ~100 ms.
- ✅ **Security trio:** AES-256 (at rest) + TLS 1.3 (in transit) + X.509 mutual authentication.
- ✅ **Regulatory frameworks:** HIPAA (USA), GDPR (EU), DISHA/ABDM (India), FDA 510(k), CE Marking.
- ✅ **Smart Hospital verticals:** RPM, Tele-ICU, Asset Tracking, Cold Chain Pharma, AAL, Connected Imaging.
- ✅ **Common challenges:** Security, Interoperability, Power, Latency, Regulation, Reliability.
- ✅ **Engineering mantra:** *Medical telemetry = low bit-rate + high reliability + ultra-low latency.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Power Plants # Power Plants in Industrial IoT: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Power Plant (IIoT Context):** A power plant, when viewed through the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) lens, is a **cyber-physical energy generation system** in which conventional electro-mechanical assets (turbines, boilers, generators, transformers, switchgear) are augmented with a distributed network of intelligent sensors, edge controllers, and cloud-based analytics platforms to enable real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance, load optimization, and grid-stability automation. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 situates the **Power Plant** as a flagship use-case of **Industry 4.0 verticals**. The integration transforms a legacy SCADA-controlled plant into a **Self-Aware, Self-Predictive, and Self-Optimizing** energy infrastructure. ## 1.2 The Five Major Classifications of Power Plants | # | Type | Primary Energy Source | Typical Capacity (MW) | IIoT Criticality | |---|------|-----------------------|------------------------|-------------------| | 1 | **Thermal (Fossil)** | Coal, Oil, Natural Gas | 100 – 3000 | **Very High** | | 2 | **Hydroelectric** | Gravitational Potential of Water | 10 – 22000 | **High** | | 3 | **Nuclear** | Controlled Fission of U-235 / Pu-239 | 500 – 7000 | **Extreme** | | 4 | **Solar (PV / CSP)** | Solar Irradiance (~1000 W/m²) | 1 – 550 | **High** | | 5 | **Wind** | Kinetic Energy of Air | 0.5 – 800 (offshore) | **High** | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The course emphasizes the *digitization* layer over the *electromechanical* layer. Therefore, students must understand how IIoT overlays a **common reference architecture** across all five plant types. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nervous System of a Power Plant" Imagine the human body: - The **Power Generation Equipment** (boiler, turbine, generator) acts like **muscles and organs**. - The **Sensors** (RTDs, thermocouples, vibration probes) are the **nerve endings**. - The **PLCs / RTUs / Edge Gateways** act as the **spinal cord**, processing reflexes locally. - The **SCADA / Cloud Historian** is the **brain** (cerebrum), performing long-term memory and analytics. - The **Control Center Operator** is the **conscious mind** receiving summarized, actionable insights. Just as a nervous system lets the body react to heat, cold, or pain, IIoT lets a power plant react to load surges, vibration anomalies, emission spikes, and fuel quality variations — **in milliseconds, not hours**. ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Valuation) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Plant Availability Factor (PAF)** = (Operating Hours / Total Hours in Period) × **100%** > - **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** = (Actual Energy Generated / Maximum Possible Energy) × **100%** > - **Heat Rate** = Energy Input / Energy Output (typically **1800 – 10000 kJ/kWh**) > - **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** = Fuel Used / Energy Generated > - **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** for renewables = (Energy Generated / (Installed Capacity × 8760)) × **100%** These metrics are the **primary KPIs** monitored by IIoT dashboards in modern power plants. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layered IIoT Architecture of a Modern Power Plant The architecture follows the **5-layer ISA-95 / RAMI 4.0 reference model** adapted for energy verticals: ### Layer 1 — Physical / Sensor Layer - **Temperature:** RTD (Pt100), Type-K / Type-J Thermocouples (range: $-200°\text{C}$ to $1260°\text{C}$). - **Pressure:** Strain-gauge / Piezoelectric transmitters (boiler drum: up to **300 bar**). - **Flow:** Orifice-plate, Venturi, Coriolis, and ultrasonic flowmeters. - **Vibration:** Accelerometers (IEPE) on turbine bearings (range: 0–10 kHz). - **Emission:** NDIR gas analyzers for $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{SO}_x$, $\text{NO}_x$, and opacity dust monitors. - **Electrical:** CTs, PTs, and digital multifunction meters (PMU — Phasor Measurement Units at 30–60 samples/sec). ### Layer 2 — Communication / Network Layer - **Fieldbus:** Modbus RTU, PROFIBUS, Foundation Fieldbus, HART. - **Industrial Ethernet:** PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT. - **Wireless:** WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a, LoRaWAN (for large solar/wind farms). - **Backhaul:** MPLS, 4G/5G private APN, or fiber-optic rings. ### Layer 3 — Edge / Control Layer - **PLCs** (Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix) handle closed-loop control at **1–10 ms** cycle time. - **RTUs** (Remote Terminal Units) collect substation telemetry and forward to SCADA. - **Edge Gateways** (e.g., AWS Greengrass, Azure Edge Zones) run anomaly-detection ML models locally. ### Layer 4 — Platform / Cloud Layer - **Historian databases** (PI System, Wonderware Historian) store time-series data at sub-second resolution. - **Analytics engines** (Apache Spark, Flink, InfluxDB + Grafana). - **Digital twin** of the plant continuously updated with sensor data. ### Layer 5 — Application / Enterprise Layer - **APM (Asset Performance Management):** GE Predix, Bentley AssetWise. - **EMS (Energy Management System):** OSI Monarch, ABB Network Manager. - **Predictive Maintenance Dashboards** for O&M engineers. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked "Explain IIoT in Power Plants", always structure the answer using the **5-layer model** above. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for clear layering. ## 2.2 Why IIoT is Mission-Critical in Power Generation | Engineering Pain Point (Pre-IIoT) | IIoT-Enabled Solution | |-----------------------------------|------------------------| | Unplanned downtime (loss: **$500,000 / day** in a 500 MW plant) | **Predictive Maintenance** using vibration + thermal ML models | | Manual emission logging (error-prone) | **Continuous Emissions Monitoring Systems (CEMS)** with real-time regulatory alerts | | Slow fault localization (hours) | **AI-driven root cause analysis** in minutes | | Inefficient combustion (excess air → fuel waste) | **Closed-loop AI combustion optimization** (saves 1–3% fuel) | | Reactive grid balancing | **PMU-based Wide-Area Monitoring (WAMS)** for predictive grid stability | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Saver — All Key Equations for the Power Plant Module** | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Electrical Power Output | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | $P$ in kW, $V_L$ line voltage, $I_L$ line current | | 2 | Energy Generated (period) | $E = \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\, dt$ | $E$ in kWh or MWh | | 3 | Plant Load Factor (PLF) | $\text{PLF} = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot T} \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** | | 4 | Plant Availability Factor | $\text{PAF} = \dfrac{T_{operating}}{T_{scheduled}} \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** | | 5 | Heat Rate | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{E_{out}}$ | kJ per kWh | | 6 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{3600}{\text{HR}}$ (in kJ/kWh) | Dimensionless (**%**) | | 7 | Capacity Utilization Factor (Renewables) | $\text{CUF} = \dfrac{E_{generated}}{P_{installed} \cdot 8760} \times 100$ | $8760$ = hours in a year | | 8 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{E_{out}}$ | kg per kWh | | 9 | Rankine Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Rankine} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{condenser}}{T_{boiler}}$ | $T$ in Kelvin (ideal) | | 10 | Wind Power Capture | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3} \cdot C_p$ | $\rho \approx 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ | | 11 | Solar PV Output | $P_{PV} = G \cdot A \cdot \eta_{PV}$ | $G$ = irradiance W/m² | | 12 | Sensor Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist Criterion | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Rule:** In every formula table above, $\cos\phi$ denotes the **power factor** and $\eta$ denotes **efficiency**. Always mention the unit in the final answer line to earn full KTU marks. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In a **coal-fired thermal plant** rated at 500 MW: - IIoT sensors reduce **specific fuel consumption** by ~0.5%. - Annual saving = 500 MW × 8760 h × 0.005 × 0.5 kg/kWh = **~10,950 tonnes of coal/year**. - This translates to **~25,000 tonnes of $\text{CO}_2$** avoided annually. - In **wind farms**, IIoT-driven pitch control can boost **Annual Energy Production (AEP)** by 2–5%. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Plant Load Factor from Sensor Data **Given:** A thermal power plant is rated at **$P_{rated} = 200$ MW**. Over a billing month of **30 days**, the IoT historian logs the following generation profile: - Days 1–10: Full load, $P_1 = 200$ MW for **$t_1 = 10 \times 24 = 240$ hours**. - Days 11–20: Partial load, $P_2 = 150$ MW for **$t_2 = 10 \times 24 = 240$ hours**. - Days 21–25: Outage (planned maintenance), **$t_3 = 0$ hours generation**. - Days 26–30: Partial load, $P_3 = 100$ MW for **$t_3 = 5 \times 24 = 120$ hours**. **Find:** (a) Total Energy Generated $E_{actual}$, (b) Plant Load Factor (PLF), (c) Plant Availability Factor (PAF). ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute Actual Energy Generated (Trapezoidal Integration)** The energy is the time-integral of instantaneous power. Since power is piecewise-constant, the integral reduces to a discrete sum. $$ E_{actual} = \sum_{i} P_i \cdot t_i $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Operational Segments** $$ E_{actual} = (200 \cdot 240) + (150 \cdot 240) + (100 \cdot 120) $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the Multiplications** $$ E_{actual} = 48{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 12{,}000 $$ $$ E_{actual} = 96{,}000 \text{ MWh} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Maximum Possible Energy** Maximum energy is the rated output if the plant had run at full capacity for the entire period. $$ T_{total} = 30 \text{ days} = 30 \times 24 = 720 \text{ hours} $$ $$ E_{max} = P_{rated} \cdot T_{total} = 200 \times 720 $$ $$ E_{max} = 144{,}000 \text{ MWh} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Plant Load Factor (PLF)** $$ \text{PLF} = \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{96{,}000}{144{,}000} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{PLF} = 66.67\,\% $$ **Step 6 — Compute Plant Availability Factor (PAF)** Operating hours = $240 + 240 + 120 = 600$ hours (outage days contribute zero). $$ \text{PAF} = \frac{T_{operating}}{T_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{600}{720} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{PAF} = 83.33\,\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Insight:** Note that **PAF (83.33%) > PLF (66.67%)** — the plant was *available* 83% of the time, but ran at *reduced load* during most of those available hours. This distinction is a common 3-mark short question in KTU exams. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wind Turbine Power Using IIoT Anemometer Data **Given:** A horizontal-axis wind turbine with: - Rotor diameter $D = 80$ m - Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ - Wind speed $v = 12$ m/s - Power coefficient $C_p = 0.40$ (Betz limit theoretical max = **0.593**) **Find:** The electrical power output $P_{wind}$. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute the Swept Area of the Rotor** The rotor sweeps a circular disk. Using $D = 80$ m: $$ A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 80^{2}}{4} $$ $$ A = 1600 \cdot \pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Wind Power Equation** $$ P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3} \cdot C_p $$ **Step 3 — Substitute All Values** $$ P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^{3} \times 0.40 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the Cube of Velocity** $$ v^{3} = 12^{3} = 1728 $$ **Step 5 — Combine Stepwise** $$ P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \times 0.40 $$ $$ P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 691.2 $$ $$ P_{wind} \approx 2{,}128{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 2.13 \text{ MW} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters in IIoT:** A SCADA system that *misreads the wind speed by just 1 m/s* (i.e., reports 11 instead of 12) underestimates the power by: > $$\Delta P \approx P(v=12) - P(v=11) = 2.13 - 1.55 \approx 0.58 \text{ MW}$$ > That is a **27% revenue error** — which is why IIoT sensor calibration is treated as a **Tier-1 operational concern**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IIoT Benefits by Plant Type | Plant Type | Primary Sensors | IIoT Use Case | Expected KPI Improvement | |------------|------------------|---------------|--------------------------| | **Thermal** | Temperature, $\text{O}_2$ / $\text{NO}_x$ analyzers, vibration | Combustion optimization, predictive maintenance | **+1.5% efficiency, –20% downtime** | | **Hydro** | Water level, flow, penstock pressure, turbine vibration | Spillway optimization, cavitation prediction | **+2% annual energy** | | **Nuclear** | Neutron flux, radiation, temperature, seismic | Safety-system digital twin, regulatory compliance | **Zero-incident target** | | **Solar PV** | Pyranometer, panel temperature, DC current | Soiling detection, inverter health | **+5% CUF** | | **Wind** | Anemometer, blade pitch, gearbox temperature | Pitch/yaw optimization, lightning detection | **+3% AEP** | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Predictive Maintenance Threshold Using Vibration The **ISO 10816-3 standard** classifies machine vibration velocity (RMS, mm/s) into zones: - **Zone A (Good):** $v_{rms} \leq 2.8$ mm/s - **Zone B (Acceptable):** $2.8 < v_{rms} \leq 4.5$ mm/s - **Zone C (Unsatisfactory):** $4.5 < v_{rms} \leq 7.1$ mm/s - **Zone D (Unacceptable, damage):** $v_{rms} > 7.1$ mm/s An IIoT vibration sensor on a 500 MW turbine bearing streams the following **hourly RMS values** (in mm/s) for a 10-hour window: $$ v = [1.5,\ 2.0,\ 2.3,\ 2.6,\ 3.1,\ 3.8,\ 4.4,\ 5.0,\ 5.9,\ 6.7] $$ **Find:** (a) Mean vibration level, (b) Identify the ISO zone trend. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean** $$ \bar{v} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i $$ $$ \bar{v} = \frac{1.5 + 2.0 + 2.3 + 2.6 + 3.1 + 3.8 + 4.4 + 5.0 + 5.9 + 6.7}{10} $$ $$ \bar{v} = \frac{37.3}{10} = 3.73 \text{ mm/s} $$ **Step 2 — Zone Classification of the Mean** Since $2.8 < 3.73 \leq 4.5$, the **mean lies in Zone B (Acceptable)**. **Step 3 — Identify the Trend** The data exhibits a **monotonic upward trend**, with the latest reading **6.7 mm/s** falling in **Zone C (Unsatisfactory)**. **Step 4 — IIoT Alarm Decision** A rule-based IIoT system would trigger a **predictive alarm** because: - The trend is monotonically increasing. - The current value has crossed the **Zone B → C** threshold at 4.5 mm/s. > [!NOTE] > **Engineer's Action:** Schedule bearing inspection within 72 hours, even though the plant has not yet failed. This is the **economic value of IIoT** — preventing catastrophic failure. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IIoT Reference Architecture in a Thermal Power Plant > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end data flow** from the boiler/turbine floor to the enterprise cloud, as per the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["Temp Sensor Pt100"] S2["Pressure Transmitter"] S3["Vibration Accelerometer"] S4["Emission NDIR Analyzer"] S5["Flow Coriolis Meter"] S6["PMU Phasor Measurement Unit"] PLC1["PLC S7-1500 Boiler Control"] PLC2["PLC Turbine Governor"] RTU1["RTU Substation Telemetry"] EDGE1["Edge Gateway AI Inference"] SCADA1["SCADA HMI Operator Desk"] HIST1["Historian PI System"] CLOUD1["Cloud Analytics Platform"] DTWIN1["Digital Twin Engine"] APM1["APM Asset Performance Management"] EMS1["EMS Energy Management System"] S1 --> PLC1 S2 --> PLC1 S3 --> PLC2 S4 --> EDGE1 S5 --> PLC1 S6 --> RTU1 PLC1 --> EDGE1 PLC2 --> EDGE1 RTU1 --> SCADA1 EDGE1 --> SCADA1 SCADA1 --> HIST1 HIST1 --> CLOUD1 CLOUD1 --> DTWIN1 CLOUD1 --> APM1 SCADA1 --> EMS1 APM1 --> OPS1["Plant Manager Dashboard"] EMS1 --> GRID1["Utility Grid Control Center"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Five-Layer IIoT Stack Mapped to Power Plant Assets ```mermaid flowchart TB L5["Layer 5 Enterprise Apps
APM EMS Predictive Maintenance"] L4["Layer 4 Cloud Platform
Historian Big Data Analytics"] L3["Layer 3 Edge Control
PLC RTU Edge Gateway"] L2["Layer 2 Industrial Network
PROFINET Modbus WirelessHART"] L1["Layer 1 Physical Sensors
RTD Vibration Flow PMU Emission"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Cross-Layer Integration:** A **single event** (e.g., a turbine temperature spike from 480°C to 510°C) propagates through all five layers in **<100 ms**, triggering a SCADA alarm **and** a digital-twin simulation in the cloud. --- ## 4.3 Predictive Maintenance Closed-Loop Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Sensor Streaming Data"] --> A2["Edge Preprocessing"] A2 --> A3["ML Anomaly Detection Model"] A3 -->|Normal| A4["Store in Historian"] A3 -->|Anomaly Detected| A5["Generate Predictive Alert"] A5 --> A6["Maintenance Ticket Auto Creation"] A6 --> A7["Technician Dispatch with AR Guidance"] A7 --> A8["Component Health Updated in Digital Twin"] A8 --> A4 ``` --- ## 4.4 Smart Grid Integration Block Diagram (Power Plant as Prosumer) ```mermaid flowchart TB GEN1["Thermal Generator 500 MW"] GEN2["Wind Farm 200 MW"] GEN3["Solar PV Plant 100 MW"] SUB1["Substation 220 kV 400 kV"] PMU1["WAMS Wide Area Monitoring"] CTRL1["Load Dispatch Center"] DEM1["Industrial Consumer 50 MW"] DEM2["EV Charging Network 30 MW"] BAT1["Battery Energy Storage 100 MWh"] IOT1["IoT Cloud Demand Response Engine"] IOT1 --> CTRL1 GEN1 --> SUB1 GEN2 --> SUB1 GEN3 --> SUB1 SUB1 --> PMU1 PMU1 --> CTRL1 CTRL1 --> DEM1 CTRL1 --> DEM2 SUB1 --> BAT1 BAT1 --> DEM2 ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Plant Load Factor. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Model Answer:** > Plant Load Factor (PLF) is the ratio of actual energy generated by a power plant to the maximum possible energy that *could* have been generated if the plant had operated at its rated capacity continuously for the entire period. > $$\text{PLF} = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot T} \times 100\,\%$$ > A high PLF indicates **efficient utilization** of installed capacity. *(2 marks formula, 1 mark explanation)* ### Question 2: List any four sensors used in a thermal power plant and their purpose. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** > 1. **RTD (Pt100)** – Measures boiler steam temperature (range up to 600°C). > 2. **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** – Monitors turbine bearing vibration. > 3. **NDIR Gas Analyzer** – Continuous emission monitoring of $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{NO}_x$, $\text{SO}_x$. > 4. **Coriolis Flow Meter** – Measures feedwater mass flow to the boiler. > *(½ mark per sensor, 1 mark for proper purpose mapping)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-layer IIoT architecture** of a modern thermal power plant. List at least **two sensors** used in each physical-layer category (Temperature, Pressure, Vibration, Emission). **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A **250 MW** thermal power plant records the following generation in a week: Mon–Wed full load (24 h each), Thu off (maintenance), Fri–Sat 200 MW each, Sun 150 MW. Compute: **(i)** total energy generated, **(ii)** Plant Load Factor, **(iii)** Plant Availability Factor. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): - **Layer 1 – Sensors:** RTD Pt100 (boiler temp), Type-K thermocouple (superheater), strain-gauge pressure transmitter (drum), piezoelectric accelerometer (bearing), NDIR gas analyzer (stack), Coriolis flow meter (feedwater). - **Layer 2 – Network:** PROFINET for control loop, Modbus RTU for sub-asset telemetry, WirelessHART for non-critical wireless nodes. - **Layer 3 – Edge/PLC:** Siemens S7-1500 PLC for boiler/turbine closed-loop control, RTUs for switchyard, edge gateways running ML inference. - **Layer 4 – Platform:** PI Historian for time-series storage, Apache Kafka for event streaming, cloud-based digital twin engine. - **Layer 5 – Application:** APM dashboards (GE Predix), EMS (OSI Monarch), regulatory reporting. *(1.4 marks per layer — total 7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Compute Hours per Segment** Mon–Wed: $3 \times 24 = 72$ h at 250 MW Thu: $0$ h (off) Fri–Sat: $2 \times 24 = 48$ h at 200 MW Sun: $24$ h at 150 MW **Step 2 — Energy from Each Segment** $$ E_1 = 250 \times 72 = 18{,}000 \text{ MWh} $$ $$ E_2 = 200 \times 48 = 9{,}600 \text{ MWh} $$ $$ E_3 = 150 \times 24 = 3{,}600 \text{ MWh} $$ **Step 3 — Total Actual Energy** $$ E_{actual} = 18{,}000 + 9{,}600 + 3{,}600 = 31{,}200 \text{ MWh} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum Possible Energy (in a 168-hour week)** $$ E_{max} = 250 \times 168 = 42{,}000 \text{ MWh} $$ **Step 5 — Plant Load Factor** $$ \text{PLF} = \frac{31{,}200}{42{,}000} \times 100 = 74.29\,\% $$ **Step 6 — Plant Availability Factor** Operating hours = $72 + 0 + 48 + 24 = 144$ h $$ \text{PAF} = \frac{144}{168} \times 100 = 85.71\,\% $$ *(Full marks: 2 for energy, 2.5 for PLF, 2.5 for PAF — including units)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks > **(a)** Explain with examples how **IIoT enables predictive maintenance** in a power plant. Compare it with traditional **reactive** and **preventive** maintenance strategies. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **90 m**, air density **1.225 kg/m³**, wind speed **10 m/s**, and power coefficient **0.38**. Compute the captured wind power. If the IIoT system upgrades the pitch controller to raise $C_p$ to **0.45** (all else same), what is the **percentage gain** in power? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): - **Reactive Maintenance:** "Run-to-failure" — fix *after* breakdown. High downtime cost (~$500,000/day in 500 MW plants). *(2 marks)* - **Preventive Maintenance:** Scheduled (calendar-based) servicing. Better than reactive but causes *over-maintenance* and *unnecessary downtime*. *(2 marks)* - **Predictive Maintenance (IIoT-enabled):** Real-time sensors (vibration, thermal, acoustic emission) feed ML models that predict remaining useful life (RUL). Action is taken *only* when degradation is detected. Benefits: **20–30% reduction** in maintenance cost, **70–75% reduction** in breakdowns, **20–25% productivity gain**. Examples: General Electric's Predix platform monitoring 600+ plant assets. *(3 marks)* #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Compute Swept Area** $$ A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 90^{2}}{4} = 2025\pi \approx 6361.73 \text{ m}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Initial Power ($C_p = 0.38$)** $$ P_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3} \cdot C_p $$ $$ P_1 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6361.73 \times (10)^{3} \times 0.38 $$ $$ P_1 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6361.73 \times 1000 \times 0.38 $$ $$ P_1 \approx 1{,}481{,}150 \text{ W} \approx 1.48 \text{ MW} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Upgraded Power ($C_p = 0.45$)** $$ P_2 = P_1 \times \frac{0.45}{0.38} = 1.48 \times 1.1842 \approx 1.75 \text{ MW} $$ **Step 4 — Percentage Gain** $$ \% \text{Gain} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1} \times 100 = \frac{0.27}{1.48} \times 100 $$ $$ \% \text{Gain} \approx 18.42\,\% $$ *(Distribution: 1.5 marks for swept area, 2.5 for initial power, 2 for gain computation, 1 for proper units)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Always include units** in the final answer (e.g., "MW", "%", "mm/s"). A correct numerical answer **without units** loses **½ to 1 mark** per sub-question. > 2. In the PLF formula, students often write $E_{actual} / E_{max}$ without the **×100** multiplication — examiners treat this as a "conceptual error" and deduct **1 mark**. > 3. For wind power, forgetting the **½ factor** in $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 C_p$ results in **double the actual power** — a guaranteed **2-mark penalty**. > 4. When asked "Explain IIoT in Power Plants", **do not** discuss only sensors. Examiners expect the **full 5-layer architecture** to be drawn and explained for full marks. > 5. Avoid mixing up **PAF vs PLF**: PAF is time-based (operational availability); PLF is energy-based (load utilization). This is a **favourite 3-mark trap question**. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Power Plants** - **Power Plant Types:** Thermal, Hydro, Nuclear, Solar, Wind — each has distinct IIoT use-cases. - **Core IIoT Architecture:** 5 layers (Sensor → Network → Edge → Cloud → Application). - **Sensors to Remember:** RTD Pt100 (temp), Piezoelectric (vibration), NDIR (emission), Coriolis (flow), PMU (grid). - **Key Formulas:** - $\text{PLF} = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot T} \times 100$ - $\text{PAF} = \dfrac{T_{operating}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ - $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} C_p$ - $P_{PV} = G \cdot A \cdot \eta_{PV}$ - $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{3600}{\text{Heat Rate}}$ - **Betz Limit:** Maximum theoretical $C_p = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ — actual turbines achieve 0.35–0.45. - **Predictive Maintenance Threshold:** ISO 10816-3 zones (A: ≤2.8, B: ≤4.5, C: ≤7.1, D: >7.1 mm/s). - **CEMS:** Continuous Emissions Monitoring System — mandatory under EPA / CPCB regulations. - **PMU Sampling Rate:** 30–60 samples/second (compared to traditional SCADA at 1 sample/2–4 sec). - **Digital Twin:** Real-time virtual replica of plant used for "what-if" simulations and operator training. - **Smart Grid:** Power plant + IIoT becomes a **prosumer** (producer + consumer) interacting with battery storage and EV charging. - **Big 4 IIoT Platforms in Energy:** GE Predix, Siemens MindSphere, ABB Ability, Schneider EcoStruxure. - **KPI Improvements (Memory Hooks):** +1.5% efficiency, –20% downtime, +5% CUF, +3% AEP. - **Examiner Traps:** Units, ×100 in PLF, ½ factor in wind power, PAF vs PLF distinction. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Power Plants**

topics.title: Inventory Management and Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inventory Management and Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inventory Management

**Inventory Management** is the systematic process of ordering, storing, tracking, and controlling a manufacturing or retail organization's inventory. In the context of the **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, it evolves from a static, ledger-based activity into a **real-time, sensor-driven, predictive discipline** where smart devices continuously report stock levels, locations, and consumption rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Inventory management in IIoT refers to the integration of cyber-physical systems, RFID, smart sensors, and cloud analytics to maintain optimal stock levels while minimizing carrying costs, stockouts, and wastage across the supply chain.

**Three Pillars of Industrial Inventory:**
1. **Raw Materials** – Inputs awaiting processing.
2. **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** – Partially completed goods on the production line.
3. **Finished Goods** – Completed products ready for dispatch.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> Think of a hospital pharmacy. Without a system, the pharmacist guesses when insulin is running low. With IIoT, every vial has a smart tag that "tells" the central system "I'm 80% consumed." The system then automatically places an order to the supplier, schedules a delivery slot, and alerts the floor manager — all without a human touching a clipboard. This is **smart inventory management**.

---

## 1.2 Quality Control (QC)

**Quality Control (QC)** is the operational technique used to fulfill quality requirements by observing, measuring, and inspecting manufactured products against defined engineering specifications.

**Quality Assurance (QA)** is the broader, process-oriented umbrella that covers the entire production pipeline, while **Quality Control (QC)** is the product-level, post-production inspection activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In IIoT-enabled factories (Industry 4.0), Quality Control transforms from a *sampling-based, end-of-line check* to a *continuous, in-line, real-time defect detection system* powered by machine vision, vibration analytics, and AI models.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a chocolate factory's conveyor belt. A human inspector checks 1 in 100 chocolates — slow, tiring, error-prone. An IIoT vision camera checks **every single chocolate**, 60 per second, flagging cracked or misshapen ones. The defective chocolate is automatically pushed off the line by a robotic arm. The line keeps moving. This is **IIoT-driven Quality Control**.

### Key Performance Metrics (in **bold**)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | **DPMO** | ppm | Defect density indicator |
| **Process Capability Index** | **Cpk** | dimensionless | Measures process centering |
| **First Pass Yield** | **FPY** | % | Products passing QC without rework |
| **On-Time-In-Full** | **OTIF** | % | Logistics + inventory KPI |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inventory Management Models

### 2.1.1 Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

The **EOQ model** determines the optimal order quantity that minimizes the **total inventory cost** — the sum of **ordering cost** and **holding (carrying) cost**.

**Assumptions of the Basic EOQ Model:**
- Demand is **deterministic** and constant over time.
- Lead time is **fixed and known**.
- Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate).
- No **quantity discounts** or stockouts.
- Holding cost is **linear** with inventory level.

**Why does EOQ exist?**
If you order *too frequently*, you pay huge ordering costs (admin, transport, paperwork). If you order *too much* at once, you pay huge holding costs (warehousing, spoilage, capital lock-up). EOQ finds the *sweet spot*.

### 2.1.2 Reorder Point (ROP) and Safety Stock

**Reorder Point (ROP)** is the inventory level at which a new order must be placed to avoid stockout during the **lead time**.

**Safety Stock (SS)** is the buffer inventory kept to absorb **demand variability** and **supply lead time variability**.

### 2.1.3 ABC Analysis (Always Better Control)

Inventory items are classified into three classes based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**:

- **A-Items** → ~20% of SKUs, ~80% of annual consumption value. Tight control.
- **B-Items** → ~30% of SKUs, ~15% of value. Moderate control.
- **C-Items** → ~50% of SKUs, ~5% of value. Loose control.

### 2.1.4 Just-In-Time (JIT) Inventory

JIT aims to receive materials **exactly when needed**, eliminating inventory as a buffer. It depends on **reliable suppliers** and **IIoT-enabled demand signals**.

---

## 2.2 Quality Control Frameworks

### 2.2.1 Statistical Process Control (SPC)

SPC uses **control charts** to monitor whether a process is in a state of **statistical control** (only common-cause variation) or out of control (special-cause variation present).

**Western Electric Rules (1 of 3 standard violation patterns):**
1. A single point beyond **±3σ** from the mean.
2. **2 of 3 consecutive points** beyond ±2σ on the same side.
3. **8 consecutive points** on the same side of the centerline (drift).

### 2.2.2 Six Sigma Methodology — DMAIC

- **D**efine the problem
- **M**easure baseline performance
- **A**nalyze root causes
- **I**mprove the process
- **C**ontrol the gains

**Six Sigma Goal:** Achieve ≤ **3.4 DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities).

### 2.2.3 Process Capability Indices

**Cp** measures the *potential* capability — the spread of the process relative to specification width.

**Cpk** measures the *actual* capability — penalizes the process if it is not centered between the specification limits.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU ESE. The vertical bar symbol is written as `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Economic Order Quantity | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand (units), $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹), $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹) | Min total cost |
| 2 | Total Annual Inventory Cost | $TC = \dfrac{D}{Q} S + \dfrac{Q}{2} H$ | $Q$ = order quantity | At EOQ, both terms equal |
| 3 | Number of Orders per Year | $N = \dfrac{D}{EOQ}$ | dimensionless | Frequency |
| 4 | Time Between Orders | $TBO = \dfrac{365}{N}$ | days | Reorder cycle |
| 5 | Reorder Point | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$ | $\bar{d}$ = avg daily demand, $L$ = lead time (days) | Trigger level |
| 6 | Safety Stock (variable demand) | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | $z$ = service-level z-score, $\sigma_d$ = std dev of daily demand | Statistical buffer |
| 7 | Safety Stock (variable lead time) | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \bar{d}$ | $\sigma_{LT}$ = std dev of lead time | Alternative form |
| 8 | Process Capability (potential) | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6 \sigma}$ | dimensionless | $\sigma$ = process std dev |
| 9 | Process Capability (actual) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ = process mean | Penalizes off-center |
| 10 | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | ppm | Six Sigma metric |
| 11 | Sigma Level (Six Sigma) | $\sigma_{level} = \dfrac{1}{2}\vert Z_{LSL} \vert + \dfrac{1}{2}\vert Z_{USL} \vert$ | dimensionless | Yield-based |
| 12 | First Pass Yield | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good units first time}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100$ | % | Throughput indicator |

### Real-World Utility

- **EOQ** is used by Amazon's warehouse robotics, Toyota's JIT lines, and pharma cold-chain logistics.
- **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the typical automotive industry threshold (AIAG standard).
- **SPC charts** are mandated by **ISO 9001:2015** and **IATF 16949** for automotive suppliers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EOQ Formula

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- $D$ = annual demand in units
- $Q$ = order quantity (the variable we want to optimize)
- $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)

Since the **Average Inventory** under instantaneous replenishment is half the order quantity, we get:

$$Average\ Inventory = \dfrac{Q}{2}$$

### 3.1.2 Cost Functions

**Annual Ordering Cost (OC):** Each order costs ₹$S$, and we place $\dfrac{D}{Q}$ orders per year.

$$OC(Q) = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

**Annual Holding Cost (HC):** We pay ₹$H$ per unit per year on average $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ units.

$$HC(Q) = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

**Total Annual Cost (TC):**

$$TC(Q) = \dfrac{D S}{Q} + \dfrac{H Q}{2}$$

### 3.1.3 Minimize TC(Q)

Take the first derivative with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} &= \dfrac{d}{dQ}\!\left(\dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}\right) \\[6pt]
&= -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} \\[6pt]
\text{Set } \dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} &= 0 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{H}{2} &= \dfrac{DS}{Q^2} \\[6pt]
Q^2 &= \dfrac{2DS}{H} \\[6pt]
Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **EOQ**. To confirm it is a minimum, take the second derivative:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad (\text{positive} \Rightarrow \text{minimum verified})$$

### 3.1.4 Optimal Total Cost at EOQ

Substituting $Q^{*}$ back into $TC(Q)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC(EOQ) &= \dfrac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \dfrac{H \sqrt{2DS/H}}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{2 D S H} \quad \text{(after simplification)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This elegant result shows that at the optimum, **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost**, each equal to half of $TC$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — EOQ + ROP

**Problem (KTU Style):** A manufacturing plant uses **12,000 units/year** of a raw material. Ordering cost is **₹250/order**, and holding cost is **₹20/unit/year**. Lead time is **10 days**. Calculate EOQ, number of orders/year, optimal cost, and the reorder point (assume 360 operating days/year, 0 safety stock for simplicity).

### Step 1: Compute EOQ

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOQ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 12000 \times 250}{20}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{6{,}000{,}000}{20}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{300{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 547.72 \text{ units} \\[6pt]
&\approx 548 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Number of Orders per Year

$$N = \dfrac{D}{EOQ} = \dfrac{12000}{547.72} = 21.91 \approx 22 \text{ orders/year}$$

### Step 3: Optimal Total Cost

$$TC(EOQ) = \sqrt{2 \times 12000 \times 250 \times 20} = \sqrt{120{,}000{,}000} = ₹10{,}954.45$$

### Step 4: Reorder Point

Average daily demand: $\bar{d} = \dfrac{12000}{360} = 33.33$ units/day.

$$ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L = 33.33 \times 10 = 333.3 \text{ units}$$

**Answer:** Order **548 units** every time. Reorder when stock falls to **333 units**. Annual inventory cost ≈ **₹10,954**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Process Capability

**Problem:** A CNC lathe produces shafts. The specification is **24.50 mm ± 0.05 mm** (i.e., USL = 24.55, LSL = 24.45). The process mean $\mu = 24.50$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.012$ mm. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on capability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{24.55 - 24.45}{6 \times 0.012} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.10}{0.072} \\[6pt]
&= 1.389
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \min\!\left(\dfrac{0.05}{0.036},\ \dfrac{0.05}{0.036}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \min(1.389,\ 1.389) \\[6pt]
&= 1.389
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.389$ (and the process is **centered**), the process is **capable**. The automotive industry standard requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$, so this passes.

> [!NOTE]
> If $\mu$ had been $24.52$ mm (drifted), then $C_{pk}$ would be:
> $C_{pk} = \min(0.83,\ 1.94) = 0.83$ — **not capable**, even though $C_p$ remains 1.389. This is why $C_{pk}$ is the true indicator.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IIoT-Enabled Inventory Management Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of an IIoT inventory management system, mapping the data flow from physical assets to the analytics layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[RFID Tags on Pallets] --> B1[Warehouse Gate Reader]
    A2[Smart Shelf Load Cells] --> B2[Edge Gateway]
    A3[Barcode Scanners at Dock] --> B1
    A4[GPS Trackers on Trucks] --> B3[Cellular Gateway]
    B1 --> C1[Edge Aggregator]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Cloud Time Series DB]
    D1 --> E1[Inventory Analytics Engine]
    E1 --> F1[EOQ Optimizer Module]
    E1 --> F2[Reorder Point Calculator]
    E1 --> F3[ABC Classifier]
    F1 --> G1[Auto Purchase Order Trigger]
    F2 --> G2[Alert to Floor Manager]
    F3 --> G3[Dashboard for SCM Head]
    G1 --> H1[ERP SAP S4HANA Integration]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> I1[Operator HMI Tablet]
    H1 --> I1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left column** represents physical data sources (RFID, sensors, scanners, GPS).
- The **middle column** represents the data ingestion and storage layers.
- The **right column** represents the analytics, action triggers, and human-machine interface.

---

## 4.2 IIoT Quality Control Closed-Loop System

The diagram below shows the **closed-loop feedback system** used in an IIoT-enabled quality control pipeline, from in-line sensing to automated process correction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[CNC Machine Producing Part] --> P2[In-Line Vision Camera]
    P2 --> P3[Edge AI Inference Node]
    P3 --> P4{Defect Detected Question Mark}
    P4 --> P5[Reject Pneumatic Pusher]
    P4 --> P6[Pass to Next Station]
    P3 --> P7[SPC Control Chart Engine]
    P7 --> P8[Statistical Process Control Module]
    P8 --> P9{Out of Control Question Mark}
    P9 --> P10[Alert to Process Engineer]
    P9 --> P11[Adjust Machine Parameters]
    P11 --> P1
    P7 --> P12[Six Sigma Dashboard Cloud]
    P12 --> P13[Management Review]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Vision cameras + Edge AI perform **real-time defect classification**.
- A **defect** triggers a pneumatic reject mechanism.
- Concurrently, the **SPC engine** updates the control chart. If a **Western Electric rule** is violated, the process is auto-corrected by adjusting machine parameters, forming a **closed loop**.

---

## 4.3 ABC Analysis Pyramid for IIoT Warehouses

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[A Items 20 percent SKUs 80 percent Value] --> L1A[Daily Cycle Count]
    L1 --> L1B[RFID Real Time Tracking]
    L2[B Items 30 percent SKUs 15 percent Value] --> L2A[Weekly Cycle Count]
    L2 --> L2B[Barcode Scanning]
    L3[C Items 50 percent SKUs 5 percent Value] --> L3A[Monthly Cycle Count]
    L3 --> L3B[Manual Visual Audit]
```

This pyramid emphasizes that **A-class items justify the highest IIoT investment** (RFID + real-time analytics), while **C-class items** are managed with low-cost, low-frequency audits.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Pattern:** Part A carries 2-mark or 3-mark short answer questions. The two questions below are 3-markers.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Economic Order Quantity (EOQ). List the assumptions of the basic EOQ model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EOQ is the fixed order quantity that minimizes the sum of annual ordering and holding costs, given by $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$, where $D$ is annual demand, $S$ is ordering cost, and $H$ is holding cost per unit per year. *(1 mark)*

Assumptions: *(2 marks — at least 4 expected)*
1. Constant, known annual demand.
2. Fixed, known lead time.
3. Instantaneous replenishment (infinite production rate).
4. No quantity discounts or stockouts.
5. Holding cost is linear with average inventory.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Process Capability Index Cpk? Why is Cpk preferred over Cp in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ measures how well a process stays **within specification limits**, while also accounting for whether the process mean is **centered** between them. *(1 mark)*

Why preferred: *(2 marks)*
- $C_p$ only measures *spread*, ignoring process centering.
- $C_{pk}$ penalizes off-center processes, giving a more realistic capability.
- $C_{pk}$ aligns with industry standards (AIAG, IATF 16949).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Question (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with **internal choice** (either OR). You attempt one. We provide two alternative questions below for self-practice.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) A factory consumes 18,000 units/year of a critical bolt. Ordering cost is ₹400 per order and holding cost is ₹15 per unit per year. Lead time is 12 days. The firm operates 360 days/year and wants a 95% service level (z = 1.65). The standard deviation of daily demand is 4 units. Calculate: (7 marks)**
- (i) EOQ
- (ii) Optimal annual total cost
- (iii) Reorder Point with safety stock

**(b) Discuss how IIoT technologies (RFID, smart sensors, edge computing, cloud analytics) transform traditional inventory management into a real-time, predictive discipline. Support your answer with a block diagram. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Given:** $D = 18000$, $S = 400$, $H = 15$, $L = 12$ days, $z = 1.65$, $\sigma_d = 4$, working days = 360.

**(i) EOQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOQ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 18000 \times 400}{15}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{14{,}400{,}000}{15}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{960{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 979.80 \approx 980 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Optimal Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC(EOQ) &= \sqrt{2 \times 18000 \times 400 \times 15} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{216{,}000{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= ₹14{,}696.94
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Reorder Point:**

Average daily demand: $\bar{d} = \dfrac{18000}{360} = 50$ units/day.

Safety stock: $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 4 \times \sqrt{12} = 1.65 \times 4 \times 3.464 = 22.86$ units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROP &= \bar{d} \cdot L + SS \\[6pt]
&= 50 \times 12 + 22.86 \\[6pt]
&= 600 + 22.86 = 622.86 \approx 623 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

IIoT transforms inventory management in **four key ways** (each worth ~1.5 marks, plus 1 mark for diagram):

1. **Real-Time Visibility** — RFID tags + smart shelves provide continuous, automated stock counts. Replaces manual cycle counts and human errors.
2. **Predictive Replenishment** — Cloud analytics on time-series data computes dynamic ROP and EOQ based on actual consumption trends, not historical estimates.
3. **Supply Chain Synchronization** — GPS trackers on trucks + ERP integration allow end-to-end visibility. Suppliers receive forecasts 24/7 via API.
4. **Anomaly Detection** — ML models detect unusual consumption (e.g., shrinkage, spoilage, theft) by comparing real-time vs predicted demand.
5. **Block Diagram** — Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes (IIoT-Enabled Inventory Management Architecture). *(1 mark)*

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the components of a Statistical Process Control (SPC) chart. The shaft diameter specification is 25.00 ± 0.10 mm. For a sample of 25 subgroups of size 4, the grand mean is 25.01 mm and the average range is 0.06 mm. For n = 4, $A_2 = 0.729$. Compute the upper and lower control limits for the X-bar chart and comment. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the Six Sigma DMAIC methodology. How does IIoT enable each phase? (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**SPC Chart Components** *(2 marks)*: Centerline (process mean), Upper Control Limit (UCL = $\bar{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$), Lower Control Limit (LCL = $\bar{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$), plotted data points representing subgroup means.

**Calculation:**

Given: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.01$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.06$ mm, $n = 4$, $A_2 = 0.729$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R} \\[6pt]
&= 25.01 + 0.729 \times 0.06 \\[6pt]
&= 25.01 + 0.04374 \\[6pt]
&= 25.05374 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCL &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R} \\[6pt]
&= 25.01 - 0.04374 \\[6pt]
&= 24.96626 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment** *(1 mark)*: Both control limits lie within the specification limits of 24.90 and 25.10 mm. The process is in **statistical control** and has margin against specification, indicating capability.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**DMAIC Phases** *(5 marks — 1 mark each)*:
- **Define** — Identify problem, customer CTQ (Critical-to-Quality), project charter.
- **Measure** — Collect baseline data, validate measurement systems, quantify defects.
- **Analyze** — Use Ishikawa diagrams, regression, hypothesis testing to find root causes.
- **Improve** — Pilot solutions, optimize via DOE (Design of Experiments).
- **Control** — Standardize via SOPs, control plans, mistake-proofing (poka-yoke).

**IIoT Enablement** *(2 marks)*:
- **Define** — Smart sensors capture customer usage telemetry, defining CTQs objectively.
- **Measure** — IoT devices auto-collect millions of data points (defect rates, process parameters).
- **Analyze** — Edge AI + cloud ML run root cause analysis on big data.
- **Improve** — Digital twins simulate improvements before physical deployment.
- **Control** — Real-time SPC dashboards maintain gains 24/7 with auto-correction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in final answer.** Always write "548 units" or "₹10,954", not just "548".
> 2. **Wrong divisor in Safety Stock formula.** For variable demand, use $\sigma_d \sqrt{L}$, **not** $\sigma_d \cdot L$. The square root is a frequent mark-killer.
> 3. **Confusing Cpk with Cp.** Writing $C_p$ when the question asks for $C_{pk}$ (or vice versa) loses 2-3 marks immediately.
> 4. **Not justifying the centering statement.** When $C_p = C_{pk}$, you must explicitly state "the process is centered" to get full credit.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram in IIoT questions.** KTU examiners allocate a clear 1-2 marks for diagrams. Omitting it = guaranteed loss.
> 6. **Ignoring working days.** Always convert annual demand to daily demand using the actual working days (e.g., 360), not 365.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ☐ **EOQ** = $\sqrt{2DS/H}$ minimizes ordering + holding cost.
- ☐ At EOQ, **ordering cost = holding cost**, each = half of total cost.
- ☐ **ROP** = $\bar{d} \cdot L + SS$ triggers replenishment.
- ☐ **Safety Stock** = $z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L}$ (variable demand) or $z \cdot \bar{d} \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ (variable lead time).
- ☐ **Service Level → z-score**: 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 99% → 2.33, 99.73% → 3.00.
- ☐ **ABC Analysis** follows Pareto 80/20 — A = high value/tight control.
- ☐ **JIT** eliminates inventory as a buffer, requires reliable IIoT demand signals.
- ☐ **Cp** = process potential; **Cpk** = actual capability (penalizes off-centering).
- ☐ **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the typical automotive/industrial threshold.
- ☐ **X-bar chart** uses $A_2 \bar{R}$ factor; **R-chart** uses $D_3$, $D_4$ factors.
- ☐ **Six Sigma** = 3.4 DPMO, achieved via DMAIC.
- ☐ **Western Electric Rules** detect out-of-control signals: 1 beyond 3σ, 2-of-3 beyond 2σ, 8 on one side.
- ☐ **IIoT pillars** for inventory: RFID, smart shelves, edge gateways, cloud analytics, ERP integration.
- ☐ **IIoT pillars** for QC: vision cameras, edge AI, SPC control charts, digital twins, auto-correction loops.
- ☐ **ISO 9001:2015** mandates process approach + risk-based thinking; SPC charts are widely accepted evidence.
- ☐ Remember: **Stockout cost + Carrying cost + Ordering cost = Total Relevant Cost**; EOQ minimizes the latter two.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always end with a concluding sentence linking the answer to **Industry 4.0** or **IIoT context**. KTU 2024 examiners reward industrial relevance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Safety and Security (Including AR and VR Safety Applications)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT635 - INDUSTRIAL INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plant Safety and Security (Including AR and VR Safety Applications)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plant Safety — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Safety (KTU 2024 Definition):** Plant Safety refers to the integrated set of engineered procedures, hardware systems, software protocols, and human-factor controls designed to prevent, detect, mitigate, and respond to hazardous conditions within an industrial plant (refinery, chemical plant, power station, manufacturing facility) in order to protect human life, equipment integrity, and the surrounding environment.

In IIoT context, **Plant Safety = Functional Safety + Process Safety + Occupational Safety + Cyber-Physical Safety**, all instrumented and connected through sensor-actuator networks, edge gateways, and cloud analytics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:**
> - **Hazard:** A potential source of harm (chemical release, fire, explosion, fall, mechanical failure).
> - **Risk:** Probability × Severity of a hazard materializing.
> - **Incident:** An unintended event that could have caused harm (Near Miss) or did cause harm (Accident).
> - **LOPA:** Layer of Protection Analysis (KTU frequently tested concept).

---

## 1.2 Plant Security — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Security (KTU 2024 Definition):** Plant Security is the protection of industrial assets (physical infrastructure, control systems, intellectual property, and personnel) against intentional malicious acts — including physical intrusion, sabotage, terrorism, theft, and cyber-attacks on Industrial Control Systems (ICS) / SCADA / DCS networks.

**Three Pillars of Plant Security (in IIoT):**

| Pillar | Scope | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Security** | Perimeter, access control, intrusion detection | RFID badges, biometric gates, fences |
| **Cyber Security** | Network, OT/IT convergence, IIoT endpoints | Firewalls, DMZ, intrusion detection (IDS) |
| **Operational Security (OPSEC)** | Procedures, personnel vetting, supply chain | Background checks, vendor audits |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for Students

> [!NOTE]
> **🛡️ The "Smart Fortress" Analogy**
> Imagine a medieval castle. The **king (Plant Manager)** wants to protect the **treasure (raw materials & products)** and the **citizens (workers)**.
> - **Walls + Moat = Physical Security** (fences, gates, barriers).
> - **Guards + Watchtowers = Surveillance** (CCTV, vibration sensors, motion detectors).
> - **Trained Knights = Safety Systems** (fire suppression, emergency shutdowns, alarms).
> - **A Magic Crystal Ball in the Sky = IIoT Cloud** that watches everything in real-time.
> - **A Magical Helmet giving Knights "X-ray vision" = AR/VR Safety Applications** — workers can *see* invisible hazards (gas leaks, radiation) overlaid on their vision, or train in a *virtual* plant without risk.

**Safety protects from ACCIDENTS. Security protects from ATTACKS.** Both are needed — and both now rely on IIoT.

---

## 1.4 Standards, Frameworks & Mandates (Bold for Memorization)

| Standard | Domain | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 61508** | Functional Safety (generic) | **IEC** |
| **IEC 61511** | Functional Safety in Process Industry | **IEC** |
| **ISO 12100** | Machinery Safety | **ISO** |
| **ISO 45001** | Occupational Health & Safety Management | **ISO** |
| **ISA 84 / IEC 62443** | Industrial Automation & Control Systems Cyber Security | **ISA / IEC** |
| **OSHA 29 CFR 1910** | US Occupational Safety (referenced) | **OSHA** |
| **NFPA 70E** | Electrical Safety in Workplace | **NFPA** |
| **ATEX 2014/34/EU** | Equipment for Explosive Atmospheres | **EU Directive** |
| **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** | Risk Reduction Metric (SIL 1 → SIL 4) | **IEC 61508** |

---

## 1.5 The IIoT Safety-Security Convergence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> With OT/IT convergence, **safety and security can no longer be treated separately.** A cyber-attack that compromises a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) sensor can disable plant safety. This is the modern **Cyber-Safety Co-engineering** principle taught in KTU 2024.

---

## 1.6 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — Quick Intro

$$\text{SIL} = \text{Discrete level (1 to 4) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of the Safety Instrumented Functions (SIF)}$$

| SIL | Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD<sub>avg</sub>) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | **10 to 100** |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | **100 to 1,000** |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | **1,000 to 10,000** |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | **10,000 to 100,000** |

**Higher SIL = Lower failure probability = Stricter design, testing, and certification.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Industrial Hazard Classification (KTU High-Yield)

### A. Physical Hazards
- Mechanical (rotating shafts, presses, conveyors)
- Electrical (arc flash, shock, short circuit)
- Thermal (burns, fire, explosion)
- Noise (> 85 dB causes hearing damage)
- Radiation (ionizing & non-ionizing)

### B. Chemical Hazards
- Toxic gases (H₂S, CO, Cl₂, NH₃)
- Flammable liquids/vapors (LPG, gasoline, acetone)
- Reactive chemicals (oxidizers, water-reactive)
- Corrosives (acids, alkalis)

### C. Biological Hazards
- Pathogens in pharma, food, water treatment plants

### D. Ergonomic / Human Factor Hazards
- Repetitive strain, poor posture, fatigue

### E. Process Hazards
- Over-pressure, over-temperature, runaway reactions, loss of containment

---

## 2.2 Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) — The "Swiss Cheese Model"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** is a semi-quantitative risk assessment methodology mandated in **IEC 61511** for process industry. It examines how multiple independent protection layers (IPLs) reduce the frequency of a hazardous event.

### The Independent Protection Layers (IPLs):

1. **Layer 1 — Process Design** (inherent safety: lower pressure, lower inventory)
2. **Layer 2 — Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** (regulatory control loops)
3. **Layer 3 — Critical Alarms & Operator Intervention** (human supervision)
4. **Layer 4 — Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** (automatic shutdown on unsafe condition)
5. **Layer 5 — Physical Protection** (relief valves, rupture discs, dikes)
6. **Layer 6 — Plant Emergency Response** (fire-fighting, evacuation)
7. **Layer 7 — Community Emergency Response**

**Each layer is the "slice of Swiss cheese" — only when holes in ALL layers align does the accident occur.**

---

## 2.3 Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Architecture

$$\text{SIS} = \text{Sensor(s)} + \text{Logic Solver} + \text{Final Element (Actuator)}$$

A **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** performs a single safety action (e.g., close block valve if pressure > X).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Safety & Risk Mathematics

| Formula | Meaning | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Risk} = f \times C$ | Risk = Frequency × Consequence | $f$ = event frequency (per year), $C$ = consequence cost |
| $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ = average Probability of Failure on Demand |
| $\text{SIL Target} \Rightarrow \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ | Maps SIL to failure probability | See table in §1.6 |
| $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure | $\lambda$ = failure rate (per hour) |
| $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTTR}$ = Mean Time To Repair |
| $\text{Availability } A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System uptime fraction | Range: 0 to 1 |
| $\lambda_D = \lambda \times \text{DC}$ | Diagnostic-Covered Dangerous Failures | $\text{DC}$ = Diagnostic Coverage (0 to 1) |
| $\text{PFD}_{\text{system}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i$ | Series (low) architecture | Sum of subsystem PFDs |
| $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \dfrac{(\text{PFD}_1)^2}{3}$ | 1-out-of-2 voted system | Redundancy formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Note:** The diagnostic coverage fraction $\text{DC}$ represents the proportion of dangerous failures detected by automatic self-tests. **DC = 0% (none), DC = 60% (low), DC = 90% (medium), DC = 99% (high).**

---

## 2.4 Plant Security Architecture (IIoT-Aligned)

### The Purdue Model (Adapted for IIoT Security)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Purdue Reference Model (PRM)** divides industrial networks into **Levels 0–5**. KTU 2024 includes this as a foundational IIoT-security architecture.

| Level | Function | Typical Devices | Security Zone |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L0** | Physical Process | Sensors, actuators, motors | **Zone 0 (Field)** |
| **L1** | Basic Control | PLCs, DCS controllers | **Zone 1 (Control)** |
| **L2** | Supervisory Control | SCADA, HMI | **Zone 1 (Control)** |
| **L3** | Operations Management | Historian, MES | **Zone 2 (Operations)** |
| **L3.5** | **DMZ** | Firewall, jump server | **DMZ** |
| **L4** | Business Planning | ERP, email | **Zone 3 (Enterprise/IT)** |
| **L5** | Internet / Cloud | External network | **Outside** |

**IIoT sensors typically sit at L0/L1 but stream data upward to L4/L5 — creating NEW attack surfaces.**

---

## 2.5 Cyber-Physical Attack Vectors on Plants

1. **Sensor Spoofing** — sending fake sensor readings to SIS (e.g., fake "low pressure" while actual pressure is dangerously high).
2. **Command Injection** — sending malicious shutdown/start commands to actuators.
3. **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** — intercepting fieldbus traffic (Modbus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP).
4. **Firmware Tampering** — modifying PLC/RTU firmware.
5. **Ransomware on HMI/SCADA** — locking operators out of control screens.
6. **Side-Channel Attacks** — inferring process state from power/EM emissions.
7. **Physical Intrusion** — rogue USB device, unauthorized cabinet access.

---

## 2.6 IIoT Safety & Security Enabling Technologies

| Technology | Safety Role | Security Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)** | Real-time gas/temp monitoring | Anomaly detection |
| **Edge Computing** | Low-latency trip decisions | Local filtering, encryption |
| **Digital Twin** | What-if safety simulation | Cyber-attack impact modelling |
| **AI / Machine Learning** | Predictive safety analytics | Intrusion detection (ML-IDS) |
| **Blockchain** | Tamper-proof safety logs | Immutable audit trails |
| **5G / Private LTE** | Reliable alarm communication | Network slicing for isolation |
| **AR/VR** | Worker training, remote expert | Phishing-resistant operations |

---

## 2.7 Augmented Reality (AR) in Plant Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AR (Augmented Reality):** A live, direct or indirect, view of a physical, real-world environment whose elements are *augmented* by computer-generated sensory input (sound, video, graphics, GPS overlays).

### Industrial AR Safety Applications:

| Application | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard Visualization** | Overlay invisible hazards on worker's view | Show gas plume dispersion overlay on smart glasses |
| **Step-by-Step Procedures** | Hands-free SOPs on smart glasses | Microsoft HoloLens guiding valve isolation |
| **Remote Expert Assistance** | Field worker sees expert's annotations live | Engineer in office guides on-site technician |
| **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) Verification** | AR confirms correct isolation points | AR checklist + barcode scan |
| **Emergency Response Guidance** | AR arrows show nearest exit, assembly point | Evacuation routes in 3D |
| **Safety Inspection** | Auto-fill digital checklists, flag anomalies | Drone + AR inspection of flare stack |

**Key AR Devices in Industry:** Microsoft **HoloLens 2**, RealWear **Navigator 520**, Vuzix **M400**, Google Glass Enterprise Edition 2.

---

## 2.8 Virtual Reality (VR) in Plant Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VR (Virtual Reality):** A fully immersive, computer-simulated environment experienced through a head-mounted display (HMD) that replaces the real world entirely.

### Industrial VR Safety Applications:

| Application | Description | Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Immersive Safety Training** | Trainee enters a virtual plant with realistic hazards | Learn emergency response without real danger |
| **Confined Space Entry Rehearsal** | Practice entry into tanks, vessels in VR | Identify claustrophobia, plan tools |
| **Fire & Explosion Drills** | Experience VR fire, practice extinguisher use | Build muscle memory |
| **Pre-Construction Safety Review** | Walk through 3D model of new plant before built | Identify ergonomic / egress issues |
| **Incident Reconstruction** | Re-walk a real accident in VR for root-cause analysis | Learning from past failures |
| **Hazard Perception Testing** | Test worker's hazard-recognition skills | Quantifiable competency assessment |

**Key VR Devices:** Meta Quest 3, HTC Vive Pro 2, Valve Index, PICO 4.

---

## 2.9 AR vs. VR — Examiner's Comparison Table

> [!CAUTION]
> **Highly Testable in KTU. Memorize the differences:**

| Aspect | AR (Augmented Reality) | VR (Virtual Reality) |
|---|---|---|
| **World** | Real + digital overlay | Fully virtual |
| **Immersion** | Partial | Full |
| **Device** | Smart glasses, tablets, phones | HMD (head-mounted display) |
| **Connectivity** | Real-time to live process | Can be offline (training) |
| **Primary Safety Use** | Live operations, remote assist | Training, simulation |
| **Risk to User** | Low (still aware of surroundings) | Moderate (cannot see hazards while immersed) |
| **Examples in Plant** | HoloLens overlay for valve tagging | VR fire extinguisher drill |

---

## 2.10 Smart PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) in IIoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Smart PPE = Traditional PPE + Embedded IIoT Sensors**

| Smart PPE Item | Embedded Sensors | Safety Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Helmet** | Gas sensor, accelerometer, temp, SOS button | Fall detection, toxic gas alarm, man-down alert |
| **Smart Vest** | Heart-rate, GPS, body-temp, RFID | Worker location, fatigue, heat-stress warning |
| **Smart Gloves** | Haptic feedback, flex sensors | Warn of approaching hot/cold surfaces |
| **Smart Boots** | Pressure sensors, RFID, G-sensor | Fall, slip, proximity to vehicles |
| **Smart Goggles** | Camera, AR display, eye-tracking | Hazard visualization, biometric monitoring |
| **Respirator with sensor** | VOC, O₂, CO sensors | Real-time respirator effectiveness & breach alert |

---

## 2.11 Real-World Utility (Engineering Perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Use Cases (KTU Real-World Examples):**
> 1. **Shell Oil** — Uses HoloLens for offshore platform inspections.
> 2. **BP** — VR-based confined-space entry training globally.
> 3. **BASF** — Smart helmets with gas detection on chemical plants.
> 4. **Siemens (Amberg Electronics Plant)** — 75% automation + AR-based worker guidance achieves near-zero-defect.
> 5. **Honeywell** — Connected Worker solutions (mobility + PPE + analytics).

---

## 2.12 KTU Examiner Mnemonics

> [!TIP]
> - **"PSDMF"** for Security Layers — **P**urdue, **S**egmentation, **D**MZ, **M**onitoring, **F**irewall.
> - **"SLA"** for SIS — **S**ensor, **L**ogic Solver, **A**ctuator.
> - **"SFAIRP"** — Safety must be **A**s **F**ar **A**s **R**easonably **P**racticable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Computing PFD<sub>avg</sub> for a 1oo2 System (SIL Determination)

> A pressure transmitter has a failure rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. Proof-test interval $T_i = 8760$ hours (1 year). Diagnostic coverage $\text{DC} = 90\%$. Two such transmitters are arranged in a **1-out-of-2 (1oo2)** voted architecture. Determine the SIL achievable.

### Step 1 — Recall the formula for average PFD of a single sensor

The standard IEC 61508 simplified formula for a single sensor with proof testing:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{single}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \times T_i}{2}
$$

Where $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate.

### Step 2 — Compute the dangerous failure rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_D &= \lambda \times \text{DC} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.90 \\
&= 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: For sensors, DC relates to the fraction of dangerous failures detected by self-diagnostics; the remaining $1 - \text{DC} = 10\%$ becomes the undetected dangerous failure rate.)*

### Step 3 — PFD of a single transmitter

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{single}} &= \dfrac{\lambda_D \times T_i}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{0.03942}{2} \\
&= 1.971 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply 1oo2 voted-system formula

For a 1-out-of-2 architecture (both must fail for system to fail):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{1oo2} &= \dfrac{(\text{PFD}_{\text{single}})^2}{3} \\
&= \dfrac{(1.971 \times 10^{-2})^2}{3} \\
&= \dfrac{3.884 \times 10^{-4}}{3} \\
&= 1.295 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compare with SIL bands

From the SIL table in §1.6:
- **SIL 2 band:** $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-2}$
- **SIL 3 band:** $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-3}$

Since $1.295 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in $[10^{-4}, 10^{-3})$:

$$
\boxed{\text{System achieves SIL 3}}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RRF} &= \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{1oo2}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1.295 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 7{,}722
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with the **SIL 3 RRF range (1,000 to 10,000)**.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Quantitative Risk Calculation

> A chemical reactor has an over-pressure event frequency $f = 0.01$ per year. Consequence cost (injury + asset loss) $C = \text{₹} 50{,}00{,}000$ per event. The plant has 3 Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) — BPCS, Alarm+Operator, and SIS — each with PFD = $10^{-1}$, $10^{-1}$, $10^{-2}$ respectively.

### Step 1 — Compute combined PFD of the protection layers

Since layers are independent, total PFD of the *protection system* is the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} &= \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Alarm}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIS}} \\
&= 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute mitigated event frequency

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{mitigated}} &= f \times \text{PFD}_{\text{total}} \\
&= 0.01 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 10^{-6} \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute risk (per year)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk}_{\text{residual}} &= f_{\text{mitigated}} \times C \\
&= 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{6} \\
&= \text{₹} 5 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The residual risk is ₹5/year** — acceptable under ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable). If unacceptable, additional IPL (e.g., relief valve) must be added.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Availability of a Redundant Safety System

> A Safety PLC has MTTF = 50,000 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. Compute availability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \\
&= \dfrac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 + 8} \\
&= \dfrac{50{,}000}{50{,}008} \\
&= 0.99984 \approx 99.984\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the typical availability target for SIL 3 SIS systems (≥ 99.9%).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Protection Strategy

| Hazard Type | Primary Sensor | Primary Actuator | Typical SIL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Over-pressure in vessel | Pressure transmitter | Block valve + relief | SIL 2 / 3 |
| Gas leak (toxic) | Electrochemical gas sensor | Ventilation + isolation | SIL 2 |
| Fire | Smoke/heat/IR detector | Suppression + ESD | SIL 2 |
| Over-temperature | RTD / thermocouple | Coolant valve | SIL 2 |
| Flame failure (furnace) | UV/IR flame scanner | Fuel shut-off | SIL 3 |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Matrix — AR vs. VR in Plant Safety

| Criterion | AR | VR |
|---|---|---|
| **Real-time plant data** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (simulation) |
| **Operator awareness of surroundings** | ✅ Maintained | ❌ Obscured |
| **Training without risk** | ⚠ Limited | ✅ Excellent |
| **Cost of hardware** | High (₹3–5L) | Moderate (₹40k–1L) |
| **Use during live operations** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Use for incident reconstruction** | ⚠ Limited | ✅ Excellent |
| **Cyber-security footprint** | Higher (connected to OT) | Lower (mostly offline) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIS Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Variable\nPressure/Temp/Level] --> B[Sensor 1\nTransmitter]
    A1[Process Variable] --> B2[Sensor 2\nRedundant]
    B --> C[Safety Logic Solver\nSIS PLC - SIL 3]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D{Voting Logic\n1oo2 / 2oo3}
    D -- Trip Condition True --> E[Final Element 1\nShutdown Valve]
    D -- Trip Condition True --> E2[Final Element 2\nRedundant Valve]
    D -- Normal --> F[BPCS\nBasic Control]
    F --> G[Process Operation]
    E --> H[Safe State\nShutdown]
    E2 --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Operator HMI +\nAlarm Management]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Two sensors independently measure the same process variable. The Safety Logic Solver (a SIL-certified PLC) performs voting logic (e.g., 1oo2D — either sensor trip OR diagnostic failure triggers shutdown). Two shutdown valves provide redundancy at the actuator.

---

## 4.2 IIoT Plant Safety & Security Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L0_Field
        S1[Gas Sensor]
        S2[Temperature Sensor]
        S3[Vibration Sensor]
        S4[Smart PPE - Helmet]
    end

    subgraph L1_Edge
        G1[Edge Gateway 1\nLocal SIS]
        G2[Edge Gateway 2\nML Inference]
    end

    subgraph L2_Site
        SC[SCADA / HMI]
        HIS[Process Historian]
        DMZ[Industrial DMZ\nFirewall]
    end

    subgraph L3_Cloud
        AN[Analytics Platform]
        DT[Digital Twin]
        SIEM[Security Info +\nEvent Management]
        AR[AR/VR Service\nTraining Server]
    end

    subgraph L4_Enterprise
        ERP[ERP / EHS System]
        MOB[Mobile Worker App]
    end

    S1 --> G1
    S2 --> G1
    S3 --> G2
    S4 --> G2
    G1 --> SC
    G2 --> SC
    SC --> HIS
    HIS --> DMZ
    DMZ --> AN
    DMZ --> DT
    DMZ --> SIEM
    DMZ --> AR
    AN --> ERP
    DT --> MOB
    AR --> MOB
    SIEM --> ERP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reading Points:**
> - Field sensors (L0) stream to edge gateways (L1) which host the **SIS logic** with low latency.
> - Data crosses the **DMZ** at L3.5 — the only permitted boundary between OT and IT.
> - The **Digital Twin** mirrors the physical plant and runs **predictive safety simulations**.
> - **AR/VR services** provide both live remote assistance (from cloud to mobile/glass) and offline training simulations.

---

## 4.3 LOPA "Swiss Cheese" Layered Defense

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H[Hazardous Event Initiates] --> L1[Layer 1 - Process Design\nInherent Safety]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 - BPCS\nBasic Control]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 - Alarms +\nOperator Action]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 - SIS\nAutomatic Shutdown]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 - Physical Relief\nValves / Dikes]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 - Emergency\nResponse]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 - Community\nResponse]
    L7 --> O[Outcome\nAccident Prevented]
    H -. hole alignment .-> O
```

**Each "slice" has holes (failures). Only when ALL holes align does the accident reach the community.**

---

## 4.4 Purdue Model with IIoT Overlay

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L5_Internet
        I[External Cloud /\nInternet]
    end
    subgraph L4_Enterprise
        E[ERP / Email / IIoT Cloud]
    end
    subgraph L35_DMZ
        D[DMZ - Jump Server\nReverse Proxy]
    end
    subgraph L3_Operations
        H[Historian / MES]
    end
    subgraph L2_Supervisory
        SC[SCADA / HMI]
    end
    subgraph L1_Control
        P[PLCs / DCS Controllers]
    end
    subgraph L0_Process
        F[Sensors / Actuators /\nSmart Field Devices]
    end

    I <--> E
    E <--> D
    D <--> H
    H <--> SC
    SC --> P
    P --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Security Note:** Data flow between **DMZ and Operations** is mediated by firewalls and jump servers. **IIoT devices (L0/L1) must NEVER directly connect to enterprise IT (L4)** — all traffic must traverse the DMZ.

---

## 4.5 AR/VR Safety Application Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant W as Worker\n(Smart Glasses)
    participant AR as AR Platform
    participant DT as Digital Twin
    participant PLC as Plant SIS/PLC
    participant E as Remote Expert

    Note over W,PLC: AR-ASSISTED LIVE OPERATION
    W->>AR: Camera frame (live view)
    AR->>DT: Query equipment data
    DT->>PLC: Read sensor/state
    PLC-->>DT: Real-time state
    DT-->>AR: Hologram overlay (gas plume, valve status)
    AR-->>W: Augmented view on glasses
    W->>AR: "Need help"
    AR->>E: Live video + audio
    E-->>AR: Annotations (draw arrows, highlight)
    AR-->>W: Expert annotations overlaid
    Note over W,E: VR-ASSISTED TRAINING (Offline)
    W->>AR: Select training module
    AR-->>W: Fully immersive VR scenario\n(fire, gas leak, confined space)
    W->>AR: Perform actions
    AR-->>W: Score + feedback
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
